stem: All of the following structures are derived from the aponeurosis of the external oblique muscle except:
subject: Anatomy
explanation:
Solution
Linea semilunaris (also known as Spigelian line) is not derived from the aponeurosis of the external oblique muscle.

It is a curved tendinous line formed by the aponeuroses of all three flat abdominal muscles: external oblique, internal oblique, and transversus abdominis.
It represents the lateral border of the rectus sheath, not a derivative of any single muscle's aponeurosis.
• Therefore, it is the exception among the given options.

Other options:
Option A: Derived from the lacunar ligament, which in turn is formed by fibers of the external oblique aponeurosis.
Option B: Formed by the lower rolled border of the external oblique aponeurosis, extending from ASIS to pubic tubercle.
Option C: A triangular extension from the medial end of the inguinal ligament, also derived from the external oblique aponeurosis.

stem: Which of the following lobes of the prostate forms the uvula vesicae?
subject: Anatomy
explanation:
Solution
• The median lobe of the prostate is located between the urethra and the ejaculatory ducts.
It projects into the bladder neck, forming a slight elevation called the uvula vesicae.
• Enlargement of this lobe is commonly responsible for obstructive urinary symptoms in benign prostatic hyperplasia.
Other lobes do not contribute to the formation of the uvula vesicae.

Other options:
Option A: The anterior lobe is small, fibromuscular, and does not affect the bladder neck.
Option B: The posterior lobe lies posterior to the urethra and is the site most commonly affected by carcinoma.
Option D: The lateral lobes are located on either side of the urethra and form the bulk of the prostate but do not project into the bladder neck.

stem: The movements at which joints permits a person to look to the right and left?
subject: Anatomy
explanation:
Solution
The atlanto-axial joint is a pivot type of synovial joint between the atlas (C1) and axis (C2).
It allows rotation of the head, enabling a person to look to the right and left.
The atlanto-occipital joint primarily allows flexion and extension (nodding "yes").
• Movements at lower cervical joints (C2-C3, C3-C4) contribute only slightly to rotation.

Other options:
Option A: Permits flexion and extension of the head, not rotation.
Option C: Provides limited rotational movement; main rotation occurs at C1-C2.
Option D: Provides minimal rotation; major rotational movement is at the atlanto-axial joint.

stem: All of the following are true about the right coronary artery except:
options: Its diameter is less than left coronary artery
It arises from the anterior aortic sinus
It gives rise to circumflex coronary branch
Right conal artery is its first branch
subject: Anatomy
explanation:
Solution
• The right coronary artery (RCA) supplies the right atrium, right ventricle, SA node, and AV node in most hearts.

• Its diameter is smaller than the left coronary artery.
It arises from the anterior (right) aortic sinus.
The right conal artery is typically its first branch.
The circumflex branch is a branch of the left coronary artery, not the RCA.

Other options:
Option A: Correct, the RCA has a smaller diameter than the left coronary artery.
Option B: Correct, RCA arises from the anterior (right) aortic sinus.
Option D: Correct, the right conal artery is usually the first branch of the RCA.

stem: Foot drop is caused by damage to:
subject: Anatomy
explanation:
Solution
• Foot drop is characterized by inability to dorsiflex the foot at the ankle, leading to a high-stepping gait.
• The common peroneal nerve (branch of the sciatic nerve) innervates the anterior and lateral compartments of the leg, which are responsible for dorsiflexion and eversion.
• Damage to this nerve leads to weakness or paralysis of dorsiflexors, resulting in foot drop.
Other nerves may affect the leg but do not directly cause foot drop.

Other options:
Option B: The femoral nerve innervates the anterior thigh muscles, primarily responsible for knee extension, not dorsiflexion.
Option C: The tibial nerve innervates the posterior compartment of the leg, responsible for plantarflexion and inversion.
Option D: The sciatic nerve supplies the posterior thigh and leg; injury here can cause broader deficits, but isolated foot drop is typically due to the common peroneal nerve.

stem: What is the basis for the formation of omphaloceles?
options: Physiological hernia failing to go back
subject: Anatomy, Surgery, Pediatrics
explanation:
Solution
Omphalocele is a congenital defect in which abdominal contents herniate through the umbilical ring into the base of the umbilical cord.
It occurs when the physiological hernia of the midgut fails to return to the abdominal cavity during the 10th week of development.
The herniated contents are usually covered by amniotic sac and peritoneum. Other causes listed are incorrect; the defect is specifically due to failure of midgut return.


CharacteristicGastroschisisOmphalocele
Defect size (diameter)2-3 cm2-15 cm
SacNeverAlways, may be ruptured
Gestational agePrematurityTerm
Umbilical cordAdjacent to the defect (defect is seen usually on its right side).Attached to sac
Herniated visceraSmall bowel, stomach, colonSmall bowel, stomach, colon, liver
MalrotationYesYes
Bowel characterInflammatory, edematousNormal
Enteral nutritionDelayedNormal
Associated abnormalitiesUncommon (10% atresia)Common (50%)

Other options:
Option A: Excessive length of the intestine alone does not cause omphalocele.
Option C: Herniation of the liver may occur in large omphaloceles but is a consequence, not the cause.
Option D: Herniation of umbilicus is incorrect; omphalocele occurs through the umbilical ring, not the umbilicus itself.

stem: 1st pharyngeal arch gives rise to all except:
subject: Anatomy
explanation:
Solution
The 1st pharyngeal arch (mandibular arch) gives rise to the muscles of mastication, which include medial pterygoid, lateral pterygoid, masseter, and temporalis.

It also gives rise to tensor veli palatini and tensor tympani.
Levator veli palatini is derived from the 2nd pharyngeal arch, not the 1st. Therefore, option B is the correct exception.
First pharyngeal arch gives rise to:
• 1. Muscles of mastication (temporalis, masseter, lateral and medial pterygoids)
• 2. Mylohyoid
• 3. Anterior belly of digastric
• 4. Tensor tympani
• 5. Tensor veli palatini
• 6. Anterior 2/3 of tongue

ArchNerveSkeletal derivativesPouch DerivativesMuscle DerivativesArtery
1st (mandibular arch)Trigeminal (V)-Maxillary and mandibular divisionsIncus, Malleus, Anterior ligament of malleus, Spine of sphenoid, Sphenomandibular ligamentAuditory tube, Middle ear cavityTensor tympani, Muscles of mastication, Mylohyoid, Anterior belly of digastric, Tensor veli palatiniMaxillary artery
2nd (hyoid arch)Facial (VII)Stapes, Styloid process (temporal), Lesser cornu and upper hyoid, Stylohyoid ligamentPalatine tonsil, Tonsillar fossaStapedius, Stylohyoid, Facial Muscles (Buccinator, Platysma, Posterior belly of digastric)Stapedial artery, Hyoid artery
3rdGlossopharyngeal (IX)Greater cornu of hyoid, Lower part of hyoid bodyInferior parathyroids, ThymusStylopharyngeusCommon carotid, Proximal internal carotid arteries
4thSuperior laryngeal of Vagus (X)Thyroid cartilage, Corniculate cartilage, Cuneiform cartilageSuperior parathyroids, Parafollicular C cells of Thyroid (from ultimobranchial body)Pharyngeal muscles, Larynx -Cricothyroid, Levator veli palatiniRight side: Right subclavian artery (proximal portion); Left side: Arch of the aorta from the left common carotid to the left subclavian arteries.
6thRecurrent laryngeal of Vagus (X)Arytenoid cartilagesAll intrinsic muscles of the larynx (except the cricothyroid muscle)Right side: right pulmonary artery; Left side: left pulmonary artery and ductus arteriosus

Other options:
Option A: Medial pterygoid arises from the 1st pharyngeal arch.
Option C: Lateral pterygoid arises from the 1st pharyngeal arch.
Option D: Tensor veli palatini arises from the 1st pharyngeal arch.

stem: Cholecysto-vena caval line separates which of the following?
options: Porta hepatis and Inferior vena cava
Right and left lobe of liver
subject: Anatomy, Surgery
explanation:
Solution
• The cholecysto-vena caval line (Cantlie line) separates the right and left functional lobes of the liver.

It is an imaginary line extending from the midpoint of the gallbladder fossa at the inferior margin of the liver to the midpoint of the inferior vena cava (IVC).
The line contains the middle hepatic vein and is important in hepatic surgery.
Couinaud's liver segment classification uses this functional division; each segment has its own vascular inflow, biliary drainage, and lymphatic drainage.
Segment I corresponds to the gross anatomical caudate lobe and is considered the posterior segment.
Other segments:

Liver sectionsCoulnaud's Segments
Right anterior sectionsSegments V, VIII
Right Posterior sectionsSegments VI, VII
Left medial sectionsSegment IV (quadrate lobe)
Left lateral sectionsSegment II, III

Other options:
Option A: The line passes near the gallbladder and IVC but does not separate them.
Option B: The porta hepatis is not separated from the IVC by this line.
Option C: The caudate and quadrate lobes are separated by the porta hepatis, not the Cantlie line.

stem: Which of the following is false about skin?
options: Skin accounts for 15% of total body weight
Dermis is made up of type 1 and type 3 collagen
subject: Anatomy, Dermatology
explanation:
Solution
The dermis is derived from the mesoderm, not ectoderm.

• The epidermis is derived from the ectoderm, forming the outer protective layer.
• The dermis is primarily composed of type I and type III collagen, providing strength and elasticity.
Skin constitutes approximately 15% of total body weight in adults.

Other options:
Option B: Skin makes up roughly 15% of total body weight.
Option C: Epidermis originates from ectoderm.
Option D: Dermis contains type I and type III collagen fibers.

stem: Which of the following is true regarding De Quervain's tenosynovitis?
options: Fingers are held in mild extension
Most common involvement is index finger
subject: Orthopaedics, Surgery
explanation:
Solution
• De Quervain's tenosynovitis is a stenosing tenosynovitis affecting the abductor pollicis longus (APL) and extensor pollicis brevis (EPB) tendons at the first dorsal compartment of the wrist.

Patients typically present with pain at the radial side of the wrist, especially with thumb movement.
Finkelstein's test is positive, confirming the diagnosis.
Initial treatment is conservative (splinting, NSAIDs, corticosteroid injections); surgery is reserved for refractory cases.
Treatment:
• 1. The majority of the patients are treated medically.
• 2. Only few require the release of the 1st dorsal compartment which houses the APL and EPB tendons.

Other options:
Option A: Fingers are not specifically held in extension; pain is localized to the radial wrist.
Option C: Thumb is most commonly involved, not the index finger.
Option D: Surgery is only for cases not responding to conservative management.

stem: The arrow in the image denotes which of the following muscles?
subject: Anatomy, Orthopaedics
explanation:
Solution
The arrow indicates the extensor carpi radialis longus (ECRL) muscle.

• It originates from the distal third of the lateral supracondylar ridge of the humerus and inserts into the base of the second metacarpal bone (radial side).
It is innervated by the radial nerve (C6, C7).
The radial recurrent artery supplies this muscle.
The main actions include extension and abduction of the wrist joint.

Other options:
Option B: Brachioradialis flexes the forearm at the elbow, not the wrist.
Option C: Supinator is a deep muscle that helps in forearm supination.
Option D: Flexor carpi radialis brevis is an incorrect name; the correct muscle is extensor carpi radialis brevis.

stem: Nerve supply of the area marked by arrow in the image is:
subject: Anatomy, Orthopaedics
explanation:
Solution
The area indicated in the image corresponds to the cutaneous distribution of the radial nerve.

The radial nerve supplies the posterior aspect of the arm, forearm, and dorsum of the hand (lateral 3½ digits, excluding tips).
It provides no motor supply to the hand muscles, as it ends as sensory branches.
The posterior cutaneous nerve of the forearm is one of its important sensory branches.
Injury to the radial nerve leads to wrist drop due to loss of extensor muscle function.

Other options:
Option B: Posterior interosseous nerve is a motor branch of the radial nerve and does not provide cutaneous supply.
Option C: Median nerve supplies the palmar aspect of the lateral 3½ digits and their tips.
Option D: The ulnar nerve supplies the medial 1½ digits and corresponding part of the hand.

stem: What is the nerve supply of the structure marked in the image below?
subject: Anatomy
explanation:
Solution
The structure marked in the image is the second lumbrical of the hand.

The lateral two lumbricals (first and second) are supplied by the median nerve.
The medial two lumbricals (third and fourth) are supplied by the ulnar nerve.
Function: These muscles flex the metacarpophalangeal joints and extend both the proximal and distal interphalangeal joints, enabling fine digital movements.

Other options:
Option A: Posterior interosseous nerve supplies the extensor muscles of the forearm, not lumbricals.
Option C: Anterior interosseous nerve supplies deep flexors like flexor pollicis longus and pronator quadratus.
Option D: The ulnar nerve supplies only the medial two lumbricals, not the second.

stem: Which nerve passes through the foramen marked in the image below?
subject: Anatomy, ENT
explanation:
Solution
The foramen marked in the image is the mandibular foramen.

The inferior alveolar nerve (IAN) passes through this foramen.
The IAN is a branch of the mandibular nerve (CN V3).
It descends posterior to the lateral pterygoid muscle, passing between the sphenomandibular ligament and mandibular ramus, before entering the mandibular canal via the mandibular foramen.
Within the canal, it supplies the lower teeth, gums, and lower lip through its terminal branches.

Other options:
Option A: Lingual nerve lies anterior and medial to the inferior alveolar nerve but does not enter the mandibular foramen.
Option B: Mandibular nerve gives rise to the inferior alveolar nerve but itself does not pass through the foramen.
Option C: Chorda tympani joins the lingual nerve and does not traverse the mandibular foramen.

stem: Which fibre is marked by the arrow in the image given below?
subject: Anatomy
explanation:
Solution
The structure marked in the image is the internal capsule, which is a type of projection fiber.

Projection fibers connect the cerebral cortex with lower centers such as the brainstem and spinal cord.
They include both afferent (ascending) and efferent (descending) fibers.
The internal capsule is a compact bundle of projection fibers situated between the caudate nucleus and lentiform nucleus.
Damage to these fibers can result in contralateral motor and sensory deficits.
Commissural fibers: connect corresponding regions of two hemispheres. They include:
• Corpus callosum
• Anterior and posterior commissure
• Habenular commissure
• Hippocampal commissure of fornix
Association fibers: connect various cortical regions within the same hemisphere. They are classified as:
• Short association fibers: lie immediately under the cortex and connect adjacent gyri. Eg: arcuate fasciculus
• Long association fibers: 1. Uncinate fasciculus 2. Cingulum 3. Superior longitudinal fasciculus 4. Inferior longitudinal fasciculus 5. Fronto-occipital fasciculus 6. Fornix (except hippocampal commissure of fornix)


Projection fibers: fibers passing to and from the brainstem to the cerebral cortex.
• Internal capsule
• Corona radiata



Other options:
Option A: Short association fibers connect adjacent gyri within the same hemisphere.
Option B: Long association fibers connect distant cortical regions within the same hemisphere (e.g., cingulum, superior longitudinal fasciculus).
Option D: Commissural fibers connect corresponding areas of the two hemispheres (e.g., corpus callosum, anterior commissure).

stem: The area marked in the given image is supplied by which dermatome number?
subject: Anatomy
explanation:
Solution
The area marked in the image corresponds to the umbilicus, which is supplied by the T10 dermatome.

Dermatomal distribution of the abdominal region:
• 1. T7-T9: Skin from the xiphoid process to just above the umbilicus.
• 2. T10: Skin around the umbilicus.
• 3. T11-T12: Skin below the umbilicus up to the suprapubic region.
• 4. L1: Skin around the genitalia and upper medial thigh.
• Knowledge of dermatomes is clinically important in assessing spinal nerve injuries and mapping sensory loss.

Other options:
Option A: T8 supplies skin above the umbilicus, not the umbilical region.
Option B: T9 supplies skin just above the umbilicus.
Option D: T11 supplies skin below the umbilicus, not the umbilical region.

stem: Boundaries of Calot triangle are formed by all except:
subject: Anatomy, Surgery
explanation:
Solution
The gallbladder does not form a boundary of Calot's triangle.
Calot's triangle (also called cystohepatic triangle) is bounded by three structures:
• 1. Medially: Common hepatic duct
• 2. Inferiorly: Cystic duct
• 3. Superiorly: Cystic artery
• Proper identification of Calot's triangle is critical during laparoscopic cholecystectomy to avoid common bile duct injury, which is the most frequent site of surgical injury.
Note: According to Gray's Anatomy, the Calot's triangle is specifically defined by the cystic duct, hepatic duct, and cystic artery, while the hepatocystic triangle includes the inferior surface of the liver, hepatic duct, and cystic duct.

BoundariesCalot's TriangleHepatobiliary Triangle
SuperiorlySuperior border of the cystic arteryInferior surface of the liver
MediallyCommon hepatic ductCommon hepatic duct
InferiorlyCystic ductCystic duct



Contents of Calot's triangle:
• 1. Lymph node of Lund - Sentinel lymph node for the gallbladder
• 2. Branching of cystic artery from the right hepatic artery

Other options:
Option A: Common hepatic duct forms the medial boundary.
Option B: Cystic duct forms the inferior boundary.
Option C: Cystic artery forms the superior boundary.

stem: Identify the structure marked B in the image given below:
subject: Anatomy
explanation:
Solution
The image shows a midsagittal section of the brain.

The structure marked B is the fornix, a C-shaped bundle of white matter fibers.
The fornix connects the hippocampus to the mammillary bodies and is part of the limbic system, involved in memory functions.
It lies inferior to the corpus callosum and superior to the thalamus.

Other options:
Option A: Pineal gland is located posterior to the thalamus, near the midline.
Option B: Falx cerebri is a fold of dura mater separating the cerebral hemispheres.
Option D: The pituitary gland is located in the sella turcica of the sphenoid bone.

stem: In a patient with Wilson's disease which of the following is decreased in urine?
subject: Biochemistry, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
• Urinary levels of 3-methylhistidine are decreased in patients with Wilson's disease.

• Amino acids in urine exist either in conjugated form (majority) or free form. In Wilson's disease, the amount of conjugated amino acids far exceeds free amino acids.
3-methylhistidine exists largely in free form and does not increase on hydrolysis, hence its low urinary levels are observed.
Wilson disease is an autosomal-recessive disorder caused by mutation of the ATP7B gene on chromosome 13.
• Deficiency of ATP7B protein leads to decreased copper excretion into bile, impaired incorporation into ceruloplasmin, and accumulation of toxic copper levels in liver, brain, and eyes.

Other options:
Option A: Tyrosine levels may be normal or slightly elevated but not specifically decreased.
Option B: Phosphorus is not affected in Wilson's disease.
Option C: Serine excretion is not decreased in Wilson's disease.

stem: NADPH is used in
subject: Biochemistry
explanation:
Solution
NADPH provides the reducing equivalents necessary for fatty acid synthesis.

• Sources of NADPH include:
• 1. HMP shunt pathway (main source)
• 2. Extramitochondrial isocitrate dehydrogenase (minor source)
• 3. Malic enzyme
NADPH is essential for anabolic reactions and detoxification processes, but its primary role in fatty acid synthesis is supplying electrons for reductive biosynthesis.

Reducing agentsUses
NADPHLipid synthesis
Cholesterol synthesis
Fatty acid chain elongation
Cytochrome P450 hydroxylation of aromatic compounds, steroids, alcohols, and drugs
Metabolizes alcohol
NADHGlycolysis & Krebs cycle
Oxidative phosphorylation
FADH2B-Oxidation of fatty acid
Catalyzes reactions in muscle and liver cells
Electron Transport Chain
Krebs cycle
Oxidative Phosphorylation
Cytochrome P450 hydroxylase cycle

Other options:
Option B: Ketone synthesis does not require NADPH; it uses acetyl-CoA.
Option C: Gluconeogenesis does not directly use NADPH.
Option D: Glycolysis generates NADH, not NADPH, and does not consume NADPH.

stem: True regarding mitochondrial genome:
subject: Biochemistry, Pediatrics
explanation:
Solution
The mitochondrial genome is inherited exclusively from the mother.
Sperm mitochondria are few and usually degraded after fertilization, so all mitochondria in the zygote come from the ovum.
Both male and female offspring of an affected mother inherit mitochondrial traits or mitochondrial diseases.
Pedigree analysis of mitochondrial inheritance shows affected mothers transmitting the condition to all children, while affected fathers do not pass it on.

These diseases include (mnemonic: KLMNOP)

DiseaseFeatures
K: Kearn-Sayre Syndrome (KSS)1. Ophthalmoplegia
2. Pigmentary retinopathy
3. Cardiomyopathy
L: Leigh diseaseSubacute necrotizing encephalomyelopathy
L: Leber hereditary optic neuropathy1. Acute or subacute vision loss due to B/L optic atrophy
2. Cerebellar ataxia
3. Hyperreflexia
4. Babinski sign
5. Psychiatric symptoms
6. Peripheral neuropathy
7. Cardiac conduction abnormalities
8. Some have white matter lesions
M: MELAS1. Mitochondrial myopathy
2. Encephalopathy
3. Lactic acidosis
4. Stroke-like episodes
5. (MERRF) Myoclonic epilepsy, ragged red fibers in muscle, ataxia, sensorineural deafness
N: NARP (2 clinical patterns)1. Neuropathy, ataxia, retinitis pigmentosa, dementia
2. Severe infantile encephalopathy with lesions in basal ganglia
3. Ophthalmoplegia
4. Cardiomyopathy
5. Ptosis
O: Chronic progressive external ophthalmoplegia (CPEO)1. Ophthalmoplegia
2. Cardiomyopathy
3. Ptosis
4. Heart block
5. Ataxia
6. Retinal pigmentation
7. Diabetes
P: Pearson syndrome1. Pancreatic Insufficiency
2. Pancytopenia
3. Lactic acidosis

Other options:
Option B: Mitochondrial DNA has a high mutation rate, not low.
Option C: Mitochondrial DNA is double-stranded, not single-stranded.
Option D: Paternal inheritance does not occur under normal circumstances.

stem: Common precursor amino acid for urea, creatine and nitric oxide is
subject: Biochemistry
explanation:
Solution
Arginine serves as the common precursor for the synthesis of urea, creatine, and nitric oxide (NO).
In the urea cycle, arginine is cleaved by arginase to produce urea.
In creatine biosynthesis, arginine contributes its guanidino group to form creatine.
Nitric oxide synthase uses arginine as a substrate to produce nitric oxide, a critical signaling molecule.


Other options:
Option A: Glycine is involved in heme and purine synthesis, not a precursor for urea, creatine, or NO.
Option C: Aspartate contributes to the urea cycle but is not the common precursor.
Option D: Alanine primarily participates in the glucose-alanine cycle, not in urea, creatine, or NO synthesis.

stem: In Menke's disease, which of the following enzymes would be affected?
subject: Biochemistry, Pediatrics
explanation:
Solution
• Menke's disease (Menkes kinky hair disease) is caused by a defect in lysyl oxidase, a copper-dependent enzyme responsible for cross-linking collagen and elastin.
• The disease is an X-linked recessive disorder due to a mutation in the ATP7A gene, which encodes a copper-transporting ATPase responsible for copper absorption and distribution.
Defective copper transport leads to decreased activity of lysyl oxidase, resulting in defective connective tissue and clinical features such as brittle hair, growth failure, and neurodegeneration.


Other options:
Option A: Lysyl hydroxylase is a vitamin C-dependent enzyme involved in collagen hydroxylation.
Option C: Prolyl oxidase is not a recognized enzyme in collagen synthesis.
Option D: Prolyl hydroxylase is also vitamin C-dependent and not copper-dependent.

stem: Which of the following is autosomal dominant?
subject: Pediatrics, Pathology
explanation:
Solution
Achondroplasia is an autosomal dominant disorder caused by a mutation in the FGFR3 gene (fibroblast growth factor receptor 3).
The mutation results in abnormal chondrocyte proliferation at the growth plate, leading to shortened long bones and disproportionate dwarfism.
Most cases occur due to spontaneous mutations, though they can also be inherited.
Clinical features include:
• 1. Short limbs with normal trunk
• 2. Large head with frontal bossing
• 3. Saddle nose deformity
• 4. Exaggerated lumbar lordosis
• 5. Possible spinal cord compression due to spinal deformities


Autosomal dominantAutosomal recessive
HEAVY DOMINANTABC DEF GHI
H: Hereditary spherocytosis, Hypercholesterolemia, Hypertrophic obstructive cardiomyopathy, Huntington's diseaseA: Ataxia-telangiectasia, Albinism, Alkaptonuria, Autosomal recessive polycystic kidney disease
E: Ehler-Danlos syndromeB: Blood disorders like beta-thalassemia and sickle-cell anemia
A: AchondroplasiaC: Cystic fibrosis, Congenital adrenal hyperplasia
V: Von Willebrand disease, Von Hippel Lindau diseaseD: Sensorineural deafness
D: Dystrophia myotonicaE: Emphysema (alpha1-antitrypsin deficiency)
O: Osteogenesis imperfecta, Otosclerosis (variable)F: Friedreich ataxia
M: Marfan syndrome, Multiple endocrine neoplasias (MEN)G: Gaucher's disease, Galactosemia, glycogen storage diseases
I: Intermittent porphyriaH: Homocystinuria, Hemochromatosis
N: Neurofibromatosis 1 and 2I: Inborn errors of metabolism-Phenylketonuria, lysosomal storage diseases
A: Autosomal-dominant polycystic kidney diseaseOther conditions: Wilson's disease, sickle cell anemia, thalassemias, spinal muscular atrophy, Kartagener syndrome
N: Noonan's syndromeMucopolysaccharidoses (except Hunter syndrome)
T: Tuberous sclerosisSphingolipidoses (except Fabry disease)
Other conditions: Familial adenomatous polyposis, Hereditary hemorrhagic telangiectasia, Li-Fraumeni syndrome, Pseudohypoparathyroidism

Other options:
Option A: Autosomal recessive (CFTR gene mutation).
Option B: X-linked recessive (dystrophin gene).
Option C: Autosomal recessive (beta-globin gene mutation).

stem: Which of the following diseases have autosomal recessive Inheritance?
subject: Pediatrics, Pathology
explanation:
Solution
Albinism follows an autosomal recessive pattern of inheritance.

It is caused by defective synthesis of melanin due to mutations in genes involved in tyrosinase enzyme activity or melanin biosynthetic pathways.
For manifestation of the disease, both alleles must be defective-one inherited from each parent.
Individuals with one defective allele (heterozygotes) are carriers and do not show the disease phenotype.

X-linked recessiveX-linked dominant
Lady Hardinge College Girls Don't Care About Foolish WordsRed Rose for All Children
(Lady) Lesch-Nyhan syndrome, Lowe syndrome(Red) Rett's syndrome
(Hardinge) Hunter's syndrome, Hemophilia A and B(Rose) Rickets
(College) Chronic granulomatous disease(All) Alport syndrome (common inheritance pattern)
(Girls) G-6-PD deficiency(Children) Type X Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease
(Don't) Duchenne muscular dystrophy, Diabetes insipidus
(Care) Color blindness
(About) Agammaglobulinemia, Alport syndrome, Ocular albinism
(Foolish) Fabry disease
(Words) Wiskott-Aldrich syndrome
Other conditions: Menke's disease, Ornithine transcarbamylase deficiency

Other options:
Option B: It is an X-linked recessive disorder.
Option C: It is an autosomal dominant disorder caused by mutation in the FBN1 gene.
Option D: It is an X-linked recessive disorder caused by HPRT deficiency.

stem: Which of the following enzyme deficiencies leads to accumulation of ammonia and glutamine in the blood?
subject: Biochemistry, Pediatrics
explanation:
Solution
• Ornithine transcarbamoylase (OTC) deficiency is a urea cycle disorder that leads to accumulation of ammonia and glutamine in the blood.
Elevated ammonia levels drive the conversion of glutamate to glutamine via glutamine synthetase, leading to hyperglutaminemia.
OTC deficiency is X-linked recessive and presents with hyperammonemia, vomiting, lethargy, and encephalopathy in neonates.
Blood levels show increased glutamine and ammonia, decreased citrulline, and presence of orotic acid in urine (due to excess carbamoyl phosphate).


Other options:
Option A: Deficiency of argininosuccinate lyase causes argininosuccinic aciduria, not increased glutamine.
Option B: Deficiency of alpha-galactosidase-A causes Fabry disease, a lysosomal storage disorder.
Option D: Deficiency of arginase causes increased arginine levels in plasma, not glutamine.

stem: Which of the following amino acid is necessary for the conversion of nor-epinephrine to epinephrine?
subject: Biochemistry
explanation:
Solution
• Methionine is required for the conversion of norepinephrine to epinephrine as it provides the methyl group through S-adenosylmethionine (SAM).
• The reaction is catalyzed by phenylethanolamine-N-methyltransferase (PNMT), which transfers the methyl group from SAM to norepinephrine, forming epinephrine.
This step occurs primarily in the adrenal medulla and is stimulated by cortisol.


Other options:
Option A: Tyrosine is the precursor for catecholamine synthesis, forming DOPA, dopamine, and norepinephrine, but not involved in methylation.
Option B: Tryptophan is the precursor for serotonin and melatonin, not catecholamines.
Option C: Phenylalanine is converted to tyrosine by phenylalanine hydroxylase, an earlier step in catecholamine synthesis.

stem: Excess ammonia reduces which of the following substrates of the Krebs cycle?
subject: Biochemistry
explanation:
Solution
• Excess ammonia in the body reacts with alpha-ketoglutarate to form glutamate, catalyzed by glutamate dehydrogenase.

This leads to depletion of alpha-ketoglutarate, an important intermediate of the Krebs (TCA) cycle, thereby inhibiting ATP production in the brain.
The reduction in energy availability contributes to neurological symptoms such as encephalopathy seen in hyperammonemia.
• Thus, ammonia toxicity primarily affects brain metabolism due to loss of alpha-ketoglutarate and impaired oxidative metabolism.

Other options:
Option A: Malate levels are not directly affected by ammonia.
Option B: Oxaloacetate may decrease secondarily due to reduced TCA activity, but not directly by ammonia.
Option C: Fumarate remains unaffected in hyperammonemia.

stem: An infant presents with hypotonia and seizures. It was confirmed to be cerebrohepatorenal syndrome. Which of the following is accumulated in the brain in cerebrohepatorenal syndrome?
subject: Biochemistry, Pediatrics
explanation:
Solution
• Cerebrohepatorenal syndrome (Zellweger syndrome) is a peroxisomal biogenesis disorder caused by mutations in PEX genes, leading to absence or dysfunction of peroxisomes.

Peroxisomes are responsible for B-oxidation of very long chain fatty acids (VLCFAs) and synthesis of plasmalogens (important in myelin).
Due to defective peroxisomal function, VLCFAs accumulate in the brain, liver, and kidneys, leading to neurological dysfunction, hypotonia, seizures, and hepatic failure.
• Histologically, demyelination and hepatic fibrosis are often seen.

Other options:
Option A: Glucose accumulation is not a feature of Zellweger syndrome.
Option C: Lactic acid accumulation is seen in mitochondrial disorders, not peroxisomal defects.
Option D: Triglyceride accumulation occurs in lipid storage disorders such as Gaucher's or Niemann-Pick disease.

stem: In a patient with lipoprotein lipase deficiency, which of the following is increased following a fatty meal?
subject: Biochemistry, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
Lipoprotein lipase (LPL) is an enzyme located on the capillary endothelium of adipose tissue, skeletal muscle, and heart.

It hydrolyzes triglycerides present in chylomicrons and VLDL into free fatty acids and glycerol.
In LPL deficiency (Familial hyperchylomicronemia, Type I hyperlipoproteinemia), chylomicrons accumulate in plasma after a fatty meal, leading to milky (lipemic) plasma.
Clinical features include eruptive xanthomas, hepatosplenomegaly, lipemia retinalis, and recurrent pancreatitis.

TypeIncreased levelsManifestation
1. Hyperchylomicronemia (type I hyperlipoproteinemia)Chylomicrons, VLDL, TGPancreatitis, hepatosplenomegaly, and eruptive xanthomas
2. Familial hypercholesterolemia (type II hyperlipoproteinemia)LDL and cholesterolAtherosclerosis and tendon xanthomas
3. Dysbetalipoproteinemia (type III hyperlipoproteinemia)Chylomicrons and VLDLAtherosclerosis, palmar and tuberoeruptive xanthomas
4. Hypertriglyceridemia (type IV hyperlipoproteinemia)VLDL and TGPancreatitis

Other options:
Option B: LDL levels are not primarily affected; elevated in Type IIa hyperlipoproteinemia.
Option C: HDL may be normal or reduced, not increased.
Option D: Apo A is a component of HDL, unrelated to LPL deficiency.

stem: The device shown in the given image is used for the monitoring of:
subject: Anesthesia, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
The device shown is a pulse oximeter, which measures arterial oxygen saturation (SpO₂) and pulse rate noninvasively.

It functions on the principle of spectrophotometry, comparing absorption of red (660 nm) and infrared (940 nm) light by oxyhemoglobin and deoxyhemoglobin.
• The ratio of light absorption is processed to determine the percentage of hemoglobin saturated with oxygen.
• Pulse oximetry provides a continuous and noninvasive estimate of arterial oxygen saturation, widely used in surgery, anesthesia, and intensive care.
Oxygen content and partial pressure of oxygen (PaO2) cannot be measured by pulse oximetry; these require arterial blood gas (ABG) analysis.

Other options:
Option B: Pulse oximetry does not measure the oxygen content of the blood; it only estimates hemoglobin saturation.
Option C: The device does not determine the partial pressure of oxygen (PaO2); this measurement requires arterial blood sampling.
Option D: Pulse oximetry is used solely for monitoring oxygen saturation and not for oxygen content or partial pressure.

stem: Brown-Séquard syndrome is characterized by
options: Fine touch lost on the opposite side
Pain and temperature lost on the same side
Pain and temperature loss on the opposite side
Only pain lost on the same side
subject: Medicine, Anatomy
explanation:
Solution
Brown-Séquard syndrome results from hemisection of the spinal cord, which damages both ascending sensory and descending motor pathways.

• Dorsal column tract (fasciculus gracilis and fasciculus cuneatus) is affected on the same (Ipsilateral) side, leading to loss of discriminative touch, vibration, and proprioception below the level of the lesion.
Spinothalamic tract crosses within 1-2 segments after entering the cord, hence pain and temperature sensations are lost on the opposite (contralateral) side below the lesion.
Corticospinal tract damage causes spastic paralysis and weakness on the Ipsilateral side below the lesion.
• This pattern of sensory and motor loss is characteristic of Brown-Séquard syndrome.

Other options:
Option A: Fine touch is lost on the same side as the lesion, not on the opposite side.
Option B: Pain and temperature sensations are lost on the opposite side, not the same side.
Option D: Both pain and temperature sensations are affected, not pain alone.

stem: Acid Citrate Dextrose (ACD), used to store blood, preserves the RBCs for a period of 21 days. The storage period when adding phosphate alone and when adding adenine and phosphate together to ACD would respectively be?
subject: Pathology, Biochemistry
explanation:
Solution
• Addition of phosphate to ACD forms Citrate Phosphate Dextrose (CPD) solution.

CPD maintains 2,3-DPG levels in RBCs but does not increase storage time, which remains 21 days.
Addition of adenine and phosphate together forms Citrate Phosphate Dextrose Adenine (CPDA-1).
Adenine promotes ATP synthesis in RBCs, improving their viability and membrane Integrity.
CPDA-1 extends the storage period of RBCs to 35 days.
• Additive solutions like SAGM further extend shelf life to 42 days, but they are not ACD derivatives.

Other options:
Option A: 42-day storage applies only to additive solutions (SAGM), not ACD or CPD systems.
Option C: Durations are not standard for blood preservation and are physiologically inaccurate.
Option D: Sequence is incorrect; phosphate alone does not extend RBC storage duration.

stem: Lesion in the medial lemniscus at the level of pons causes which of the following?
options: Pain and temperature loss in the same side
Pain and temperature loss in the opposite side
Loss of tactile and proprioception on the same side
Loss of tactile and proprioception on the opposite side
subject: Anatomy, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
The medlal lemniscus carries fibers of the dorsal column pathway (gracile and cuneate tracts) after they decussate (cross over) in the medulla.

• Therefore, a lesion of the medial lemniscus in the pons will result in contralateral (opposite side) loss of fine touch, vibration, and proprioception.
These sensations are mediated by large, myelinated fibers that ascend to the thalamus and then project to the somatosensory cortex.
• Pain and temperature sensations are not affected because they are carried by the spinothalamic tract, which lies laterally and follows a different pathway.
• Thus, the hallmark of a medial lemniscus lesion is contralateral tactile and proprioceptive sensory loss below the level of the lesion.

Other options:
Option A: Pain and temperature sensations are not transmitted via the medial lemniscus pathway.
Option B: Pain and temperature loss occurs due to damage to the spinothalamic tract, not the medial lemniscus.
Option C: Tactile and proprioceptive loss occurs on the opposite, not the same, side because fibers have already crossed in the medulla.

stem: Which of the following is not a dietary fiber?
subject: Biochemistry
explanation:
Solution
Inulin is a fructan composed mainly of fructose molecules linked by B-2,1 glycosidic bonds.

It acts primarily as a preblotic, stimulating the growth of beneficial intestinal bacteria.
It is rapidly fermented in the colon, unlike true dietary fibers that resist digestion.
Does not contribute to stool bulk or viscosity like insoluble and soluble fibers do.
• Provides limited effect on bowel motility compared to other fibers.
• Therefore, inulin is not considered a true dietary fiber in physiological terms.

Other options:
Option A: Insoluble dietary fiber that adds bulk to stool and promotes bowel movement.
Option C: Soluble fiber found in fruits that helps reduce cholesterol and slow glucose absorption.
Option D: Soluble fiber that aids in controlling blood glucose and lipid levels.

stem: Testosterone production is mainly contributed by
subject: Physiology, Anatomy
explanation:
Solution
Leydig cells, also known as Interstitial cells, are located in the interstitial tissue between seminiferous tubules of the testes.

They are stimulated by luteinizing hormone (LH) from the anterior pituitary to produce testosterone.
Testosterone is essential for the development of male secondary sexual characteristics and spermatogenesis.
It acts locally on Sertoll cells to support sperm maturation and systemically on various tissues.
Cholesterol serves as the precursor for testosterone synthesis within Leydig cells.
• Testosterone secretion follows a pulsatile pattern regulated by the hypothalarnic-pituitary-gonadal axis.

Other options:
Option B: Provide nourishment to developing sperm cells and secrete inhibin but do not produce testosterone.
Option C: Site of spermatogenesis but not a source of testosterone synthesis.
Option D: Responsible for sperm storage and maturation, not hormone production.

stem: Functional residual capacity is:
options: Volume that remains after forced expiration
subject: Physiology
explanation:
Solution
• Functional Residual Capacity (FRC) represents the volume of air remaining in the lungs after a normal expiration.
It is the sum of expiratory reserve volume (ERV) and residual volume (RV).
FRC acts as a buffer that prevents large fluctuations in alveolar gas concentrations during respiration.
It maintains continuous gas exchange between breaths and prevents alveolar collapse.
FRC Increases in conditions like emphysema and decreases in restrictive lung diseases.
It cannot be measured by simple spirometry because residual volume cannot be directly measured. (FRC=ERV+RV).


MeasurementDescriptionNormal Value
Tidal volume (TV)The amount of air that moves into the lungs with each Inspiration (or the amount that moves out with each expiration) during quiet breathing500-750 mL
Inspiratory reserve volume (IRV)The air inspired with a maximal inspiratory effort in excess of the TV~3 L
Expiratory reserve volume (ERV)The volume expelled by an active expiratory effort after passive expiration~1.2 L
Residual volume (RV)The air left in the lungs after a maximal expiratory effort~1.2 L
Total lung capacity (TLC)IRV+TV+ERV+RV~5.8 L
Vital capacity (VC)IRV+TV+ERV4.5 L
Inspiratory capacity (IC)TV+IRV~3.5 L
Expiratory capacity (EC)TV+ERV~1.6 L
Functional residual capacity (FRC)ERV+RV~2.3 L

Other options:
Option A: Describes tidal expiration, not the definition of FRC.
Option B: Refers to residual volume, not functional residual capacity.
Option D: Refers to inspiratory capacity, not FRC.

stem: The function of the Golgi tendon organ is to
options: Sense changes in position of the muscle
Sense changes in length of the muscle
subject: Physiology
explanation:
Solution
Golgi tendon organs (GTOs) are sensory receptors located at the junction of muscle fibers and tendons.


They are activated by tension generated during muscle contraction or passive stretch.
GTOs send afferent signals via type Ib sensory fibers to the spinal cord.
These signals cause Inhibition of the same muscle (autogenic Inhibition) to prevent excessive tension and potential muscle damage.
Thus, GTOs act as protective feedback mechanisms to regulate muscle force generation.
They help maintain muscle tone and smoothness of contraction during movement.

Other options:
Option A: Directional change is not sensed by Golgi tendon organs but by higher proprioceptive systems.
Option C: Position sense is primarily mediated by joint receptors and muscle spindles.
Option D: Muscle length changes are detected by muscle spindles, not Golgi tendon organs.

stem: Which part of the liver stores vitamin A?
subject: Anatomy, Physiology
explanation:
Solution
Ito cells, also known as hepatic stellate cells, are located in the space of Disse between hepatocytes and sinusoidal endothelial cells.

• Their primary function is the storage of vitamin A (retinoids) in lipid droplets within the cytoplasm.
• They play a role in regulating extracellular matrix production and maintaining normal sinusoidal architecture.
• Upon liver injury, Ito cells transform into myofibroblast-like cells, leading to fibrosis. These cells contribute to retinold metabolism, influencing liver growth and repair.
• Approximately 80-90% of the body's vitamin A is stored in the liver, mostly within Ito cells.

Other options:
Option B: Responsible for metabolism, protein synthesis, and detoxification, not vitamin A storage.
Option C: Line the hepatic sinusoids and are involved in filtration, not in vitamin storage.
Option D: Specialized macrophages responsible for phagocytosis of debris and old RBCs, not for storing vitamin A.

stem: Which of the following has the least conduction velocity?
subject: Physiology
explanation:
Solution
• Postganglionic sympathetic fibers are unmyelinated C fibers, which conduct impulses very slowly.

They have the smallest diameter (0.3-1.3 µm) and lack myelin, greatly reducing conduction velocity.
• Their conduction speed is typically around 0.4-2 m/s, the slowest among peripheral nerve fibers.
• In contrast, preganglionic autonomic fibers are myelinated B fibers with faster conduction (3-15 m/s).
• Somatic motor fibers (Aa) and touch/pressure fibers (Aβ) are large and heavily myelinated, allowing rapid conduction (30-120 m/s).
• Hence, due to their unmyelinated nature and small diameter, postganglionic sympathetic fibers conduct impulses the slowest.

Fiber typeFunctionFiber diameter (um)Conduction velocity (m/s)
AαProprioception, somatic motor12-2070-120
AβTouch, pressure5-1230-70
AγMotor to muscle spindles3-615-30
AδPain, temperature2-512-30
BPreganglionic autonomic<33-15
C, Dorsal rootPain, temperature0.4-1.20.5-2
C, SympatheticPost ganglionic sympathetic0.3-1.30.7-2.3

Other options:
Option B: Large, myelinated Aα fibers with very high conduction velocity (~120 m/s).
Option C: Myelinated B fibers that conduct faster than postganglionic fibers.
Option D: Myelinated Aβ fibers that transmit touch and pressure sensations rapidly (~35-90 m/s).

stem: Liver synthesizes all of the following except:
subject: Physiology, Biochemistry
explanation:
Solution
The liver is the main site for synthesis of most plasma proteins such as albumin, coagulation factors, and acute-phase proteins.

Immunoglobulins (antibodies) are synthesized by plasma cells derived from B lymphocytes, not by hepatocytes.
• Plasma cells are part of the Immune system and are located mainly in lymphoid tissues and bone marrow.
• The liver contributes to immune defense through complement proteins but not by producing antibodies.
Therefore, among the given options, Immunoglobulins are the only proteins not synthesized by the liver.

Other options:
Option A: Major plasma protein synthesized by the liver, maintains oncotic pressure.
Option B: Most coagulation factors (except factor VIII) are synthesized in the liver.
Option C: Acute phase proteins such as CRP, fibrinogen, and haptoglobin are synthesized in the liver.

stem: Which of the following ions is involved in peripheral oxygen sensing chemoreceptors?
subject: Physiology
explanation:
Solution
• Peripheral chemoreceptors are primarily located in the carotid bodles and aortic bodies.

• These receptors detect decreased arterial PO2, Increased PCO2, and decreased pH.
In low oxygen conditions, oxygen-sensitive potassium channels in type 1 (glomus) cells are Inhibited, leading to cell depolarization.
The depolarization opens voltage-gated calcium channels, causing calcium influx. This triggers the release of neurotransmitters, which stimulate the afferent nerve endings of the glossopharyngeal and vagus nerves to increase respiratory drive.

Other options:
Option B: Calcium plays a secondary role by entering after potassium channel inhibition, not in direct sensing.
Option C: Sodium is not involved in oxygen sensing in chemoreceptors.
Option D: Chlorine has no role in peripheral oxygen sensing.

stem: Which neurotransmitter is responsible for anterograde dilatation of the small intestine?
subject: Physiology, Pharmacology
explanation:
Solution
Vasoactive intestinal Peptide (VIP) is a neurotransmitter released by enteric Inhibitory motor neurons.
It causes relaxation of smooth muscle in the gastrointestinal tract, leading to dilatation.
During peristalsis, the segment ahead of the bolus (anterograde segment) relaxes due to the action of VIP and nitric oxide.
This relaxation allows the intestinal contents to move forward smoothly.
Thus, VIP mediates anterograde dilatation while excitatory neurotransmitters cause contraction behind the bolus.
In achalasia cardia, there is decreased release of NO and VIP in the distal part of the esophagus.



Other options:
Option B: Acetylcholine causes contraction, not relaxation, of Intestinal smooth muscle.
Option C: Substance P is an excitatory neurotransmitter that promotes smooth muscle contraction.
Option D: Serotonin (5-HT) modulates peristaltic reflexes but does not directly cause relaxation.

stem: Reflex responsible for tachycardia during right atrial distension is:
subject: Physiology
explanation:
Solution
The Bainbridge reflex is a cardiac reflex that causes tachycardia in response to Increased venous return and right atrial distension.

Stretch receptors located at the venoatrial Junctions sense the Increased pressure or stretch in the right atrium.
Afferent Impulses are transmitted via the vagus nerve to the medullary cardiovascular centers.
The response Involves Inhibition of parasympathetic (vagal) activity and activation of sympathetic efferents, leading to an Increase in heart rate.
• This reflex helps to prevent overfilling of the heart by increasing the cardiac output proportionally to venous return.

Other options:
Option A: J reflex (pulmonary chemoreflex) causes bradycardia and apnea, not tachycardia.
Option B: Cushing reflex results in bradycardia due to increased intracranial pressure.
Option C: Bezold-Jarisch reflex causes bradycardia, hypotension, and vasodilation.

stem: According to Herrington classification, the decerebrate rigidity is characterized by all except
options: Rigidity occurs in all the muscles of the body
Increase in the rate of discharge of the y efferent neuron
Increased excitability of the motor neuron pool
Decerebration produces no phenomenon akin to spinal shock
subject: Physiology
explanation:
Solution
• Decerebrate rigidity occurs when the brainstem is transected between the superlor and inferior collicull, removing inhibitory influence from higher centers.

This leads to excessive activity of y motor neurons, causing increased discharge from muscle spindle afferents.
The result is Increased tone and rigidity predominantly in the extensor muscles of the limbs and neck.
• Rigidity does not occur in all muscles of the body-flexors are relatively less affected.
• Spinal shock does not occur because brainstem reflex pathways below the level of transection remain functional.

Other options:
Option B: Decerebrate rigidity is associated with increased y efferent neuron activity.
Option C: Increased excitability of the motor neuron pool contributes to enhanced muscle tone.
Option D: Decerebration does not produce spinal shock, as basic reflex mechanisms are intact.

stem: Based on the agglutination test shown below, what is the blood group?
subject: Pathology, Physiology
explanation:
Solution
• The agglutination test is used to identify the presence of A, B, and Rh antigens on red blood cells.

In this test, agglutination indicates the presence of the corresponding antigen, while no agglutination Indicates Its absence.
Here, no agglutination occurs with anti-A and anti-B sera, meaning A and B antigens are absent.
Agglutination with anti-Rh serum indicates that the Rh (D) antigen is present.
Hence, the blood group is O positive (O Rh+).

Other options:
Option A: Would show agglutination with anti-B and anti-Rh sera.
Option B: Would show agglutination with anti-A and anti-Rh sera.
Option D: Would show agglutination with anti-A, anti-B, and anti-Rh sera.

stem: What is the function of tubuloglomerular feedback?
subject: Physiology
explanation:
Solution
• Tubuloglomerular feedback (TGF) is an autoregulation mechanism that maintains a relatively constant glomerular filtration rate (GFR) despite fluctuations in arterial blood pressure.

• It involves communication between the macula densa (of the distal convoluted tubule) and the Juxtaglomerular apparatus (JGA).
When GFR Increases, more NaCl reaches the macula densa, which releases adenosine to cause afferent arteriole constriction, thereby reducing GFR.
• When GFR decreases, macula densa cells signal the afferent arteriole to dilate and stimulate renin release, leading to restoration of GFR.
• This mechanism helps in stabilizing renal blood flow and filtration rate, ensuring proper tubular reabsorption and sodium balance.

Other options:
Option B: TGF does not directly decrease plasma sodium; It maintains sodium balance indirectly.
Option C: It does not increase plasma volume directly but contributes to long-term volume control via renin-angiotensin activity.
Option D: It does not regulate tubular secretion; its main function is control of GFR.

stem: Which of the following neurons predominantly fire during forceful expiration?
subject: Physiology
explanation:
Solution
• The ventral respiratory group (VRG) of neurons, located in the ventrolateral medulla, becomes active during forceful breathing, especially forceful expiration.

• These neurons send impulses to the expiratory muscles such as the Internal Intercostals and abdominal muscles to enhance expiratory effort.
During quiet breathing, expiration is passive and the VRG remains inactive.
The dorsal respiratory group (DRG), primarily controls Inspiration and rhythmic respiratory drive.
• Thus, the VRG plays a crucial role in active expiration during exercise or respiratory distress.

Respiratory center componentPrimary function
Pre-Bötzinger complexPacemaker cells of spontaneous or automatic breathing
Dorsal Respiratory Group (DRG)Respiratory rhythm generator (Inspiratory ramp signal)
Ventral Respiratory Group (VRG)Overdrive mechanism
Pneumotaxic centreControls the "switch off" point of inspiratory ramp signal
Apneustic centreDelays switch off signal- promotes inspiration
VagiInhibits inspiratory discharge

Other options:
Option A: DRG neurons are inspiratory in function, not expiratory.
Option C: Pneumotaxic center controls the rate and pattern of breathing, not expiratory muscle activity.
Option D: Chemoreceptors regulate breathing by sensing blood gas changes but do not initiate motor neuron firing.

stem: ADH acts through which aquaporin channel?
subject: Physiology
explanation:
Solution
• Antidiuretic hormone (ADH), also known as vasopressin, acts on the collecting ducts of the nephron to promote water reabsorption.

It binds to V2 receptors located on the basolateral membrane of the collecting duct cells.
This activates adenylate cyclase, increasing cAMP levels and triggering insertion of aquaporin-2 (AQP2) water channels Into the luminal membrane.
• AQP2 channels allow water to move from the tubular lumen into the cell, and then AQP3 and AQP4 on the basolateral side facilitate water exit into the interstitium.
• Hence, AQP2 is the specific aquaporin regulated directly by ADH action.

SegmentMechanism of Water Reabsorption
Proximal tubule (67%)Osmosis, AQ 1
Loop of Henle (15%)Descending thin limb only; aquaporin 1 channels
Early distal convoluted tubule (0%)No water reabsorption
Late distal tubule and collecting duct (8-17%)Passive(aquaporin-2) under the influence of ADH

Other options:
Option A: AQP1 is present in the proximal tubule and descending limb; not regulated by ADH.
Option C: AQP3 is located on the basolateral membrane but not ADH-dependent.
Option D: AQP4 is also basolateral and constitutive, not controlled by ADH.

stem: Which of the following is true for decorticate rigidity?
options: It is produced by the removal of cerebral cortex and basal ganglia
Flexion of lower limbs and extension of upper limbs occurs
It is characterized by flexion of upper limbs and extension of lower limbs
subject: Physiology
explanation:
Solution
• Decorticate rigidity occurs when there is damage to the cerebral cortex or interruption of corticospinal tracts above the level of the red nucleus (in the midbrain).
This results in disinhibition of the red nucleus and rubrospinal tract, which facilitates flexor muscle activity in the upper limbs.
At the same time, the pontine reticulospinal and vestibulospinal tracts remain active, causing extensor hypertonia in the lower Ilmbs.
• Hence, the characteristic posture is flexion of upper limbs and extension of lower limbs.
• Decorticate rigidity indicates a lesion above the level of the red nucleus (in contrast to decerebrate rigidity, which occurs below it).



Other options:
Option A: Lesions involve corticospinal tracts, not basal ganglia removal.
Option B: The pattern is opposite upper limbs flexed, not extended.
Option C: Rigidity is present but less pronounced than in decerebrate rigidity.

stem: According to myogenic hypothesis of renal autoregulation, the afferent arterioles contract in response to stretch-induced by
subject: Physiology
explanation:
Solution
• The myogenic mechanism is one component of renal autoregulation, maintaining a stable glomerular filtration rate (GFR) despite changes in arterial pressure.

• When arterial pressure increases, the afferent arteriole wall stretches, activating mechanosensitive ion channels in the smooth muscle.
This stretch leads to the opening of voltage-gated calcium channels, allowing Ca2+ influx into the smooth muscle cells.
The increased intracellular calcium causes vasoconstriction of the afferent arteriole, restoring renal blood flow and GFR to normal levels.
• Thus, the contraction response is intrinsic to the vascular smooth muscle and independent of hormones or neural input.

Other options:
Option A: Nitric oxide causes vasodilation, not constriction.
Option B: Noradrenaline acts through sympathetic stimulation, not via the myogenic mechanism.
Option D: Adenosine mediates tubuloglomerular feedback, not the myogenic response.

stem: Which of the following helps in maintaining the patency of ductus arteriosus?
subject: Pharmacology, Pediatrics
explanation:
Solution
PGE1 (Prostaglandin E1) helps maintain ductus arteriosus patency in the fetus by causing relaxation of smooth muscle in the ductal wall.

It acts through increased cyclic AMP (cAMP) levels in vascular smooth muscle. After birth the fall in PGE1 levels (due to loss of placental source and increased oxygen tension) leads to closure of the ductus arteriosus.
Exogenous PGE1 is therapeutically used to keep the ductus arteriosus open in neonates with congenital heart defects (eg, transposition of great vessels, pulmonary atresia).
PGE1 infusion is a temporary life-saving measure until surgical correction is possible.

OrgansPGE2PGF2aPGI2TXA2
Blood vesselVasodilatation, ↓ BPVasodilatation (mostly), Larger veins constrict, little effect on BPVasodilatation ↓ BPVasoconstriction
HeartWeak inotropic, reflex cardiac stimulationWeak inotropic
PlateletVariable effectAntiaggregatoryAggregation & release reaction
UterusContraction (in vivo), Softening of cervixContraction (in vivo & in vitro), Softening of cervix
BronchiDilatation, inhibit histamine releaseConstrictionDilatation, inhibit histamine releaseConstriction
Stomach↓ acid secretion, ↑ mucus production↓ acid secretion, mucosal vasodilatation
IntestineContracts longitudinal & relaxes circular muscles, peristalsis ↑, Cl & water secretion ↑Spasmogenic fluid & electrolyte secretion (weak)Weak spasmogenic, inhibit toxin induced fluid secretion.Weak spasmogenic
KidneyNatriuresis ↓ Cl reabsorption, inhibit ADH action vasodilatation, renin releaseNatriuresis, vasodilatation, renin releaseVasoconstriction

Other options:
Option A: PGF2 is involved in uterine contraction and bronchoconstriction, not ductal patency.
Option C: PGH2 is a precursor to other prostaglandins and thromboxanes.
Option D: PGI2 (prostacyclin) causes vasodilation and inhibits platelet aggregation, but PGE1 is the primary agent for ductus arteriosus patency.

stem: Which of the following fluoroquinolones has maximum bioavailability?
subject: Pharmacology
explanation:
Solution
Levofloxacin is the L-isomer of ofloxacin and has almost complete (~99%) oral bioavailability, meaning its oral dose produces nearly the same plasma concentration as an intravenous dose.

• It is well absorbed from the gastrointestinal tract, and food has minimal effect on its absorption.
The excellent tissue penetration and predictable pharmacokinetics make it suitable for both oral and parenteral therapy in serious infections.
Its high bioavailability allows seamless switching from IV to oral therapy, reducing hospital stay and treatment cost.
In contrast, other fluoroquinolones like ciprofloxacin and norfloxacin have lower bioavailability.

Other options:
Option A: Has moderate bioavailability (60-70%) and is affected by food and cation-containing substances.
Option C: Has good bioavailability but slightly less than levofloxacin.
Option D: Has the lowest bioavailability (30-40%) among the listed fluoroquinolones.

stem: What is the mechanism of triazoles?
options: Interferes with DNA and RNA synthesis
Inhibits epoxidation of squalene in fungi
subject: Pharmacology
explanation:
Solution
Triazoles (e.g., fluconazole, itraconazole, voriconazole) act by inhibiting the fungal cytochrome P450-dependent enzyme lanosterol 14-a-demethylase.

• This enzyme is essential for the conversion of lanosterol to ergosterol, a key component of the fungal cell membrane.
Inhibition of ergosterol synthesis leads to altered membrane permeability and impaired fungal growth.
These drugs are fungistatic against yeasts and fungicidal against certain molds.
Triazoles are more selective for fungal P450 enzymes, resulting in fewer adverse effects compared to imidazoles.

Other options:
Option A: Refers to flucytosine, which inhibits DNA and RNA synthesis.
Option C: Refers to amphotericin B, which forms pores in fungal membranes.
Option D: Refers to terbinafine, which inhibits squalene epoxidase.

stem: A dermatologist wants to prescribe acitretin to a patient with psoriasis. Which of the following statements is false regarding the drug?
options: It can cause increased sensitivity to sunlight
Female patients should avoid getting pregnant for 6 months after therapy
Acitretin has a half-life of about 50 hours
subject: Pharmacology, Dermatology
explanation:
Solution
• Acitretin is a second-generation systemic retinoid used for severe psoriasis.

It is highly teratogenic, and women must avoid pregnancy for at least 3 years after discontinuation, not just 6 months, because of the risk of re-esterification to etretinate, which has an extremely long half-life (~120 days).
Acitretin has a half-life of about 50 hours, but it can be converted to etretinate when alcohol is consumed, greatly prolonging teratogenic risk.
• It is contraindicated in hyperlipidemia and liver disease due to its effects on serum lipids and hepatotoxic potential.
• It can also cause photosensitivity, dry skin, cheilitis, and hair loss as common adverse effects.
• Female patients should avoid conception for 1 month after discontinuation of isotretinoin.

Other options:
Option A: True- it is contraindicated in hyperlipidemic patients.
Option B: True- acitretin increases photosensitivity.
Option D: True- it has a half-life of around 50 hours.

stem: Intravenous mannitol is used in
subject: Pharmacology, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
• Mannitol is an osmotic diuretic that increases plasma and tubular fluid osmolarity, promoting water excretion.

It is used to reduce intraocular pressure in glaucoma and intracranial pressure in cerebral edema by drawing fluid out of tissues into the vascular compartment.
Mannitol is freely filtered at the glomerulus but not reabsorbed, causing osmotic diuresis.
It helps maintain urine flow in acute renal failure (before complete anuria sets in).
• It is not used in pulmonary edema or cardiac failure, as it can worsen the condition by increasing plasma volume.

Other options:
Option A: Contraindicated-may worsen pulmonary edema due to plasma volume expansion.
Option B: Contraindicated-increases cardiac workload.
Option C: Ineffective and dangerous in complete anuria, as it cannot be excreted.

stem: The best approach in the treatment of chronic SIADH is
options: Reducing fluid intake to <500 ml per day
subject: Pharmacology, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
• Tolvaptan is a selective vasopressin V2 receptor antagonist that promotes excretion of free water (aquaretics) without significant electrolyte loss.

It is the drug of choice for chronic SIADH as it directly blocks the action of ADH on renal collecting ducts, correcting hyponatremia effectively.
It helps to increase serum sodium concentration while maintaining normal fluid balance.
It is administered orally and preferred for long-term management, unlike conivaptan (which is IV and used acutely).
• Fluid restriction alone is often insufficient in chronic cases, and demeclocycline has slower onset and more adverse effects.

Other options:
Option B: Fluid restriction is initial therapy but not the best long-term approach.
Option C: Demeclocycline can be used but has nephrotoxicity and delayed onset of action.
Option D: Fludrocortisone is used in salt-losing conditions, not SIADH.

stem: Cilastatin is administered to prolong the action of which of the following drugs?
subject: Pharmacology
explanation:
Solution
Imipenem is a broad-spectrum β-lactam antibiotic belonging to the carbapenem class.

It is inactivated in the renal tubules by the enzyme dehydropeptidase-1, which hydrolyzes and degrades imipenem.
• Cilastatin is a specific inhibitor of renal dehydropeptidase-I and is co-administered with imipenem to prevent its degradation, thereby prolonging its half-life and maintaining antibacterial activity.
Cilastatin itself has no antibacterial activity, but it ensures higher effective concentrations of imipenem in plasma and urine.
The combination imipenem + cilastatin is used for serious infections caused by resistant gram-positive and gram-negative organisms.

Other options:
Option A: Meropenem is stable to dehydropeptidase-I and does not require cilastatin.
Option C: Cefazolin is a first-generation cephalosporin- no need for cilastatin.
Option D: Piperacillin is a penicillin derivative and not degraded by dehydropeptidase-1.

stem: Which of the following carbapenems has increased risk of causing seizures?
subject: Pharmacology
explanation:
Solution
Imipenem is a broad-spectrum carbapenem antibiotic effective against a wide range of gram-positive, gram-negative, and anaerobic bacteria.

It has the highest propensity among carbapenems to induce seizures, particularly at high doses or in patients with renal impairment or CNS disorders.
The neuroexcitatory effect is due to inhibition of GABA-A receptors, leading to CNS stimulation and seizures.
• Co-administration with cilastatin prevents renal degradation of imipenem but does not prevent neurotoxicity.
Other carbapenems like meropenem, ertapenem, and doripenem have significantly lower seizure risk because they have less GABA antagonistic activity.

Other options:
Option A: Meropenem has minimal seizure risk and is preferred in CNS infections like meningitis.
Option C: Ertapenem has a longer half-life and lower neurotoxicity.
Option D: Doripenem has the least neurotoxicity among carbapenems.

stem: Which of the following antiglaucoma medications is unsafe in infants?
subject: Pharmacology, Ophthalmology, Pediatrics
explanation:
Solution
• Brimonidine is a selective α2-adrenergic agonist that decreases intraocular pressure by reducing aqueous humor production and increasing uveoscleral outflow.

It is contraindicated in infants and young children (<2 years) because it can cross the immature blood-brain barrier and cause severe CNS depression, apnea, hypotension, hypothermia, and even coma.
The drug's lipid solubility enhances CNS penetration, leading to these toxic effects in neonates and infants.
Safer alternatives such as topical beta-blockers (timolol) or carbonic anhydrase inhibitors (dorzolamide) are preferred in pediatric glaucoma.
• Latanoprost, a prostaglandin analog, is also considered safe in older children.

Other options:
Option A: Timolol is generally safe in infants, though systemic absorption can cause bradycardia in rare cases.
Option C: Latanoprost is safe and effective for pediatric glaucoma.
Option D: Dorzolamide is safe and used in congenital or juvenile glaucoma.

stem: Which of the following phases of a clinical trial is also referred to as an efficacy trial?
subject: Pharmacology
explanation:
Solution
• Phase 2 clinical trials are also known as efficacy or therapeutic exploratory trials.

• They are conducted in a small group of patients (usually 100-300) who actually have the target disease or condition.
• The main objective is to evaluate the drug's efficacy and short-term safety and to determine the optimal dose range for future studies.
• This phase helps in confirming biological activity observed in Phase 1 and provides early evidence of therapeutic benefit.
• Data from Phase 2 trials form the basis for designing larger Phase 3 trials that assess both efficacy and safety on a broader scale.

Phase I (First in human)Phase II (First in patient)Phase III (Multi-site trial)Phase IV (Post-marketing)
10-10050-500500-3000Large or according to study
Usually healthy volunteersPatients with the target diseasePatients with the target diseaseReal-world patients
Open-label studyRandomized and controlled trial; may be blindedRandomized and controlled trial; may be blindedUsually the observational or open-label
Human pharmacology and safety: To determine safety, tolerability and pharmacokinetic parametersTherapeutic exploration and dose-ranging: To determine efficacy and dose-rangingTherapeutic confirmation and comparison: indications are finalized and guidelines for therapeutic use are formulated. Monitoring of adverse reactions is done.Postmarketing surveillance: To monitor drug interactions. compliance and long-term or rare side effects
Extends from months to 1 year1-2 years3-5 yearsUnending, after drug approval

Other options:
Option A: Phase 1 assesses safety, tolerability, and pharmacokinetics in healthy volunteers.
Option C: Phase 3 confirms efficacy and monitors adverse effects in large populations (thousands of patients).
Option D: Phase 4 involves post-marketing surveillance for long-term safety and rare adverse effects.

stem: Which of the following is true about pegylated-filgrastim?
options: Duration of action is shorter than normal fligrastim
Added advantage is that it can be taken orally
It should not be administered in sickle cell patients
It is not effective in the treatment of severe neutropenia
subject: Pharmacology
explanation:
Solution
• Pegylated-filgrastim is a long-acting form of filgrastim (G-CSF) administered subcutaneously, obtained by covalent attachment of polyethylene glycol (PEG) to the parent molecule.

• PEGylation prolongs the plasma half-life by reducing renal clearance and proteolytic degradation, allowing once-per-chemotherapy-cycle dosing.
• It should not be used in patients with sickle cell disease, as it may precipitate or worsen sickle cell crisis due to excessive leukocytosis and increased blood viscosity.
• The drug is highly effective in preventing and treating chemotherapy-induced neutropenia by stimulating neutrophil production and maturation.
• Filgrastim is the first FDA-approved drug to treat patients with radiation-induced myelosuppression and to increase survival in such patients.
• Indications of recombinant G-CSF:
1. Severe neutropenia after autologous hematopoietic stem cell transplantation
2. Severe neutropenia after high dose cancer chemotherapy
3. Severe congenital neutropenias
4. Neutropenia of patients with AIDS receiving zidovudine

Other options:
Option A: False - it has a longer duration of action than normal filgrastim.
Option B: False - it cannot be given orally.
Option D: False - It is effective in severe neutropenia.

stem: Cisatracurium is preferred over atracurium because?
options: It has rapid onset of action
It has less depressant action on heart
It has short duration of action
It causes less release of histamine
subject: Pharmacology
explanation:
Solution
• Cisatracurium is a stereoisomer of atracurium, a non-depolarizing neuromuscular blocker used during anesthesia for muscle relaxation.

• It is preferred because it causes significantly less histamine release compared to atracurium, thereby reducing the risk of hypotension, bronchospasm, and skin flushing.
• Like atracurium, it undergoes Hofmann elimination, a non-enzymatic process independent of renal or hepatic function, making it safe in patients with organ impairment.
• It provides smooth onset and recovery of neuromuscular blockade with minimal cardiovascular effects.
• Its hemodynamic stability makes it especially useful in cardiac and critically ill patients.

Other options:
Option A: Both have similar onset times.
Option B: Both have minimal cardiac depressant effects.
Option C: Duration of action is similar for both (intermediate-acting).

stem: What is the drug of choice for diphtheria carrier state?
subject: Pharmacology
explanation:
Solution
• Erythromycin is the drug of choice for the eradication of Corynebacterium diphtheriae from the carrier state as well as for treatment of mild cases.

• It effectively eliminates the bacteria from the nasopharynx, thereby preventing transmission.
• It is preferred over penicillin because of its better tissue penetration and oral bioavailability.
• In penicillin-allergic individuals, erythromycin is also used as an alternative for the treatment of active diphtheria.

Other options:
Option A: Amoxicillin is not effective for complete eradication of the carrier state.
Option C: Rifampicin is not used for diphtheria carriers.
Option D: Tetracycline is not recommended for diphtheria treatment or carrier state.

stem: Which of the following is an oral factor Xa inhibitor?
subject: Pharmacology
explanation:
Solution
• Rivaroxaban is a selective, direct oral inhibitor of Factor Xa, which is a key enzyme in the coagulation cascade responsible for the conversion of prothrombin to thrombin.• By inhibiting Factor Xa, rivaroxaban reduces thrombin generation and thrombus formation.
• It is used for prevention and treatment of deep vein thrombosis (DVT), pulmonary embolism (PE), and for stroke prevention in atrial fibrillation.
• It has a predictable pharmacokinetic profile, allowing fixed oral dosing without the need for routine monitoring.
• Oral direct factor Xa inhibitors are indicated in:
1. Prevention of embolic stroke in patients with atrial fibrillation (without valvular heart disease).
2. Venous thromboembolic disease (VTE)
3. Postoperative thromboprophylaxis in patients undergoing hip and knee arthroplasty.


• Classification of anticoagulants:

1) Vitamin K antagonistWarfarin
2) Direct thrombin inhibitors (DTI)Parenteral: Monovalent DTI- Argatroban, Bivalent DTI-Lepirudin, Bivalirudin. Oral: Dabigatran
3) Indirect thrombin inhibitorsUnfractionated heparin, Low molecular weight heparins: Enoxaparin, Dalteparin, Tinzaparin, Nadroparin
4) Indirect factor Xa inhibitorsFondaparinux
5) Oral Direct factor Xa inhibitorsRivaroxaban, Apixaban, Edoxaban, Betrixaban

Other options:
Option A: Bivalirudin is a parenteral direct thrombin inhibitor, not a Factor Xa inhibitor.
Option B: Dabigatran is an oral direct thrombin inhibitor, not a Factor Xa inhibitor.
Option D: Enoxaparin is a low molecular weight heparin (LMWH) that inhibits Factor Xa and IIa but is given parenterally, not orally.

stem: What is the mechanism of action of oseltamivir and zanamivir?
subject: Pharmacology, Microbiology
explanation:
Solution
• Oseltamivir and Zanamivir are neuraminidase inhibitors that act by blocking the viral neuraminidase enzyme present on the surface of influenza A and B viruses.

• Neuraminidase facilitates the release of newly formed viral particles from infected host cells.
• Inhibition of this enzyme prevents viral spread within the respiratory tract and reduces the duration and severity of influenza infection.
• These drugs are most effective when started within 48 hours of symptom onset.

Other options:
Option A: It is seen with antiviral drugs like acyclovir, not oseltamivir or zanamivir.
Option B: It is the mechanism of some antibacterial agents, not antiviral neuraminidase inhibitors.
Option C: It is the mechanism of drugs like zidovudine or acyclovir, not relevant here.

stem: Which of the following drugs inhibits neutrophil recruitment?
subject: Pharmacology
explanation:
Solution
• Colchicine inhibits microtubule polymerization by binding to tubulin, preventing the formation of the mitotic spindle.

• This action inhibits neutrophil chemotaxis and recruitment to sites of inflammation.
• It also suppresses the activation of the NLRP3 inflammasome, reducing the release of inflammatory mediators like IL-1B.
• Therefore, it is highly effective in acute gout for reducing inflammatory response.

Other options:
Option A: It acts as a leukotriene receptor antagonist and does not inhibit neutrophil recruitment.
Option B: It stabilizes mast cells and prevents histamine release, not neutrophil migration.
Option D: It inhibits xanthine oxidase, reducing uric acid synthesis, but does not affect neutrophils.

stem: The below dose response curve shows the action of 3 drugs. Which of the following is true regarding the given graph?
options: C is more potent than A
B is more potent than A
subject: Pharmacology
explanation:
Solution
• Competitive antagonism causes a parallel rightward shift of the dose-response curve (DRC) without reducing the maximum efficacy, as higher doses of the agonist can overcome the antagonism.

• In the given curve, Drug B shows such a rightward shift, confirming it is a competitive antagonist.
• Non-competitive antagonists reduce the maximum response (flattening the curve) because they act through irreversible receptor blockade or allosteric modulation - this pattern is shown by Drug C.
• Potency refers to the amount of drug needed to produce a given effect - the more leftward the curve, the more potent the drug. Here, A > C > B in potency.
• Efficacy is the maximum response achievable by a drug - here, A = B > C in efficacy.

Other options:
Option A: It is incorrect; C shows a non-competitive pattern (reduced maximal response).
Option B: Incorrect, because A is more potent than C.
Option C: Incorrect, since B is less potent than A.

stem: Which of the following is a synthetic PGF2 alpha derivative used as an abortifacient?
subject: Pharmacology
explanation:
Solution
• Carboprost is a synthetic analogue of Prostaglandin F2a (PGF2a).

• It is used as an abortifacient and for the management of postpartum hemorrhage unresponsive to conventional methods like oxytocin or ergometrine.
• It induces uterine contractions and promotes cervical ripening, leading to expulsion of uterine contents.
• The mechanism of action involves stimulation of smooth muscle contraction in the uterus through PGF2a receptors.
• It is administered intramuscularly and is contraindicated in asthma due to its potential to cause bronchospasm.

Other options:
Option A: It is a natural PGF2a used for termination of pregnancy but not synthetic.
Option B: It is a PGE2 analogue, not a PGF2a derivative.
Option C: It is a PGE1 analogue, used for cervical ripening and medical abortion in combination with mifepristone.

stem: Which of the following drugs causes activation of K+ATP channels?
subject: Pharmacology
explanation:
Solution
• Nicorandil is a potassium channel opener with additional nitrate-like vasodilatory properties.
