Option D: It is used to detect and measure heterophoria and heterotropla using a series of cylindrical rods that convert a point of light into a line image.

stem: Which of the following would be prescribed for simple myopic astigmatism?
subject: Ophthalmology
explanation:
Solution
• The correct prescription for simple myopic astigmatism (SMA) is -1.00 DC x 180°.
• In SMA, one principal meridian is emmetropic (no refractive error), while the other is myopic.
• Hence, only a cylindrical lens (no spherical power) is required to correct the refractive error in one meridian.
• DS (Dioptre Sphere) indicates spherical correction (for myopia or hypermetropia).
• DC (Dioptre Cylinder) Indicates cylindrical correction (for astigmatism).
• Axis denotes the orientation of the cylindrical lens, given in degrees (0-180°).



Other options:
Option A: It indicates a +1.00 D hypermetropic correction not astigmatism.
Option C: It indicates simple myopla without astigmatism.
Option D: It represents compound myopic astigmatism, as both meridians are myopic.

stem: A 50-year-old male presented with progressive blurred vision, photophobia, and eye pain. On examination, there was conjunctival redness, a mid-dilated pupil, and a shallow anterior chamber in the right eye. He has been treated both medically and surgically for the condition but showed no Improvement. The following procedure incorporates a device to aid drainage. Identify the device:
subject: Ophthalmology
explanation:
Solution
• The given case is of refractory glaucoma not responding to medical therapy or conventional trabeculectomy.

• The Ex-Press Implant is a miniature stainless steel shunt used to facilitate aqueous humor drainage from the anterior chamber to the subconjunctival space.
• It is inserted beneath a partial-thickness scleral flap and acts as a modified trabeculectomy device.
• It is part of minimally Invasive glaucoma surgery (MIGS) techniques designed to reduce complications like hypotony compared to standard trabeculectomy.
• The procedure provides a controlled filtration of aqueous humor, maintaining intraocular pressure more effectively in resistant cases.

Other options:
Option B: It is a valved drainage device (Ahmed valve) that prevents postoperative hypotony; used in refractory glaucomas.
Option C: It is a non-valved drainage Implant (Molteno) with a tube and plate mechanism; also for complex glaucomas.
Option D: It is another non-valved shunt (Baerveldt implant) offering a larger surface area for drainage in severe glaucoma cases.

stem: Which of the following is not true about retinitis pigmentosa?
subject: Ophthalmology
explanation:
Solution
• Retinitis pigmentosa (RP) is a group of hereditary retinal dystrophles primarily affecting the photoreceptors (rods and cones).

• The typical triad includes retinal pigmentation (bone spicule appearance), attenuated retinal vessels, and a pale waxy optic disc.
• The Electroretinogram (ERG) is abnormal in RP-It shows reduced or extinguished responses, Indicating loss of photoreceptor activity.
• The Electrooculogram (EOG) is also abnormal, further confirming the retinal dysfunction.
• The classic triad of findings in RP comprises:
1. Bone-spicule retinal pigmentation
2. Arteriolar attenuation
3. "Waxy" disc pallor

Other options:
Option A: Retinal pigmentation (bone spicule type) is a classical finding in RP.
Option B: A pale, waxy optic disc is characteristic of chronic retinal degeneration.
Option C: Narrowing of retinal vessels is another hallmark feature due to photoreceptor and retinal atrophy.

stem: Treatment of ptosis caused by Horner's syndrome is:
subject: Ophthalmology, Surgery
explanation:
Solution
• Fasanella-Servat operation is indicated for mild ptosis (1.5-2 mm) where the levator palpebrae superioris function is preserved.

• In Horner's syndrome, ptosis occurs due to paralysis of Müller's muscle, a sympathetically Innervated smooth muscle responsible for a small portion of lid elevation.
• The levator muscle remains intact, hence a Fasanella-Servat operation effectively corrects the mild drooping.
• The procedure involves resection of the tarsus, Müller's muscle, and conjunctiva from the upper eyelid.

SurgeryAmount of ptosis that can be treatedIndications
Fasanella Servat<2 mm-mild ptosisHorner Syndrome
Levator Resection - Anterior approachAny amount (but with retained levator function)Larger resection in ptosis
Levator resection - Conjunctival approachAny amount (but with retained levator function)Moderate resection in ptosis
Levator resection with aponeurotic reinsertionAny amount (but with retained levator function)Acquired ptosis
Frontalis suspension - Fascia lata>4 mm-severe ptosisCongenital ptosis - Marcus Gunn ptosis

Other options:
Option A: Levator resection is used in moderate to severe ptosis with good levator function, not typically for Horner's syndrome.
Option B: Frontalis sling is reserved for severe ptosis with poor levator function.
Option D: incorrect, as Fasanella-Servat operation is the treatment of choice for mild ptosis in Horner's syndrome.

stem: Identify the lesion of vocal cord in the image given below:
subject: ENT
explanation:
Solution
• The lesion showing grape-like projections involving the anterior part of the vocal cords is characteristic of Laryngeal papilloma.
• It is a benign tumor of the larynx caused by Human Papillomavirus (HPV types 6 and 11).
• Commonly affects children aged 3-5 years (juvenile papillomatosis) but can also occur in adults.
• It usually Involves the supraglottic and glottic regions of the larynx.
• The condition is known for its high recurrence rate but low malignant potential.

Other options:
Option A: It is caused by chronic irritation (e.g., smoking, voice abuse) leading to diffuse and symmetrical swelling of the vocal cords with gelatinous appearance.

Option B: It presents as an irregular, ulcerated, or proliferative mass with potential for metastasis.

Option C: It refers to the weakness or softening of tracheal cartilage, not involving the vocal cords. It is usually associated with connective tissue disorders, like Ehlers-Danlos syndrome, Marfan syndrome, cutis laxa.

stem: Inspiratory stridor is found in what kind of lesions?
subject: ENT, Pediatrics
explanation:
Solution
• Inspiratory stridor is typically seen in supraglottic lesions.

• It occurs due to obstruction above the vocal cords, such as in laryngomalacia or retropharyngeal abscess.
• Stridor is a high-pitched sound caused by turbulent airflow through a partially obstructed upper airway.
• It is usually Inspiratory, though it may have an expiratory component.
• It is distinct from stertor, which is a snoring-like noise due to obstruction at the nasopharynx or oropharynx.

ConditionFindings
CroupNon-toxic, viral prodrome, barking cough. X-ray neck shows steeple sign
EpiglottitisToxic child, drooling saliva. Lateral X-ray neck shows thumb sign
DiphtheriaUnimmunized child, cervical lymph nodes (bull neck), dirty grey/white membrane
Foreign body aspirationChoking, decreased air entry, unilateral hyperinflation

Other options:
Option B: Subglottic lesions usually produce biphasic stridor, not purely inspiratory.
Option C: Tracheal lesions cause expiratory or biphasic stridor, depending on the site.
Option D: Bronchial lesions are associated with expiratory wheeze, not stridor.

stem: Pott's puffy tumor is:
subject: ENT, Surgery
explanation:
Solution
• Pott's puffy tumor is a subperiosteal abscess of the frontal bone that occurs as a complication of frontal sinusitis.

• Infection of the frontal sinus may spread to the frontal bone, causing osteomyelitis.
• The pus breaks through the outer table of the bone, forming a boggy, doughy swelling on the forehead - the characteristic feature of Pott's puffy tumor.
• The infection can also spread Internally, leading to subdural, epidural, or perlorbital abscesses, and sometimes septic thrombosis of dural sinuses.
• Investigation: CT of the brain and paranasal sinuses is the most appropriate.
• Treatment:
1. Large doses of antibiotics
2. Abscess drainage
3. Trephining of the frontal sinus through its floor
4. Sometimes removal of sequestra and necrotic bone may also be required
Red arrow- Potts Puffy Tumor, Blue arrow- Intracranial spread


Other options:
Option B: It is not called Pott's puffy tumor, it usually presents as orbital cellulitis or abscess.
Option C: it is a cystic lesion caused by obstruction of the frontal sinus ostium, not an abscess.
Option D: It is a cystic lesion Involving the ethmold sinus, not a subperiosteal abscess.

stem: What is the maneuver depicted in the image given below?
subject: ENT
explanation:
Solution
• The Image depicts Trotter's method, which is the first step in the management of epistaxis (nosebleed).
• In this maneuver, the patient is made to sit upright and lean slightly forward over a bowl or basin to allow blood to drain out and prevent aspiration.
• The patient is advised to breathe through the mouth quietly and spit out blood rather than swallow it.
• This position helps reduce venous pressure in the nasal vessels, aiding in hemostasis.

Other options:
Option A: It is used in benign paroxysmal positional vertigo (BPPV) to reposition otoliths from the posterior semicircular canal to the utricle.


Option C: It is used for closing perforations in the nasal septum using mucoperichondrial flaps.


Option D: It is performed by forcibly exhaling against a closed glottis, used to test Eustachian tube patency or Increase Intrathoracic pressure.

stem: Which among the following statements is true about keratosis obturans?
options: Fallure of migration of desquamated epithellum along posterior meatal wall
Widening of meatus and facial nerve palsy might be seen
subject: ENT
explanation:
Solution
• All the above statements are true regarding keratosis obturans, a rare condition characterized by accumulation of keratin debris in the external auditory canal due to defective epithelial migration.

• It typically affects young Individuals (5-20 years) and is usually unilateral.
• Clinical findings include severe otalgia, conductive hearing loss, and a pearly white keratin plug seen on otoscopy.
• The keratin debris appears in onion-skin layers, and chronic cases may lead to bony erosion.
• Treatment includes removal of the keratin plug and use of keratolytic agents (2% salicylic acid) to prevent recurrence.

Other options:
Option A: It is true - keratosis obturans occur due to fallure of epithellal migration along the posterior meatal wall, leading to accumulation of keratinous material.
Option B: It is true with progressive keratin bulldup, bony erosion of the ear canal can occur, resulting in widening of the meatus and occasionally facial nerve exposure and palsy.
Option C: It is true - keratosis obturans is commonly associated with systemic conditions like bronchiectasis and chronic sinusitis, suggesting a defect in mucocillary function.

stem: Which of the following is not a feature of tubercular otitis media?
subject: ENT
explanation:
Solution
• Earache is not a feature of tubercular otitis media (TOM).

• Unlike pyogenic otitis media, tubercular Infection of the middle ear is typically painless.
• TOM usually occurs secondary to pulmonary tuberculosis, and the Infection may spread hematogenously or via the Eustachian tube from a nasopharyngeal focus.
• It is characterized by painless, persistent, foul-smelling ear discharge that does not respond to standard antibiotic therapy.
• Hearing loss is marked and disproportionate to clinical findings primarily conductive, but can become sensorineural if the labyrinth is affected.
• Otoscopic findings include a thickened tympanic membrane with multiple perforations (especially in the pars tensa) and pale granulations in the middle ear cleft.
• Histopathology shows submucosal tubercles with Langhans giant cells and caseous necrosis.
• Treatment includes aural toilet, treatment of secondary bacterial infections, and a full course of antitubercular therapy followed by reconstructive surgery.

Other options:
Option B: Multiple perforations are a classical feature of tubercular otitis media. They occur due to caseation necrosis and breakdown of the tympanic membrane.
Option C: Pale granulations are characteristic of TOM and differentiate it from pyogenic infections, where granulations are bright red and vascular.
Option D: Foul-smelling ear discharge is commonly present in TOM and typically does not respond to usual antibiotic therapy.

stem: What is the mechanism of action of the curare group of muscle relaxants?
options: Competitively blocking the binding of ACh to its receptors
Repetitive stimulation of ACh receptors on muscle endplate
Inhibiting the calcium channels on presynaptic membrane
subject: Pharmacology, Anesthesia
explanation:
Solution
• The curare group comprises non-depolarizing neuromuscular blocking agents.

• These drugs competitively block acetylcholine (ACh) at nicotinic receptors on the motor endplate.
• By preventing ACh from binding, they Inhibit depolarization of the muscle membrane, resulting in muscle relaxation.
• Their effect can be reversed by acetylcholinesterase Inhibitors, which increase ACh concentration at the neuromuscular Junction.
• Unlike depolarizing agents, curare-type drugs do not depolarize the muscle membrane.

Other options:
Option A: It refers to depolarizing agents like succinylcholine, not curare.
Option C: Repetitive stimulation occurs in the depolarizing block, not with curare.
Option D: Inhibition of presynaptic calcium channels is not the mechanism of curare-type agents.

stem: Which of the following agents does not elicit pain on injection?
subject: Anesthesia, Pharmacology
explanation:
Solution
• Ketamine does not cause pain on injection; instead, it produces profound analgesia and dissociative anesthesia.

• It acts primarily by antagonizing NMDA receptors, preventing excitatory neurotransmission.
• Because ketamine formulations are aqueous and not lipid-based, they do not irritate the veln, which explains the absence of injection pain.

Other options:
Option B: Propofol causes pain on injection because it is formulated as a lipid emulsion. The newer prodrug fospropofol produces less pain.
Option C: Etomidate can cause pain and myoclonus during injection, though lipid formulations reduce the incidence.
Option D: Thiopentone can also produce pain and venous thrombosis, especially at concentrations above 2.5%.

stem: Which of the following is not used for induction of anaesthesia in pediatric patients?
subject: Anesthesia, Pediatrics
explanation:
Solution
• Desflurane is not used for induction of anaesthesia in pediatric patients because it is highly pungent and Irritant to the alrway, leading to laryngospasm, coughing, and breath-holding.

• The incidence of laryngospasm can be as high as 50% when used for inhalational induction.
• Therefore, desflurane is reserved for maintenance of anaesthesia, particularly in situations where rapid emergence is desirable, such as obese patients, neurosurgical, and spinal fusion procedures.

Other options:
Option A: Halothane is a non-pungent agent and was historically used widely for inhalational induction in children.
Option B: Sevoflurane is the agent of choice for induction in pediatric patients due to its pleasant odor, non-irritant nature, and rapid onset.
Option D: Nitrous oxide is safe and commonly used as an adjunct during induction because it reduces the concentration of other volatile agents needed.

stem: Which among the following IV anaesthetics does not cause myocardial depression?
subject: Anesthesia, Pharmacology
explanation:
Solution
• Etomidate is a short-acting Intravenous anesthetic known for its cardiovascular stability.

• It does not cause myocardial depression or a significant decrease in systemic blood pressure.
• Etomidate maintains sympathetic tone and baroreceptor reflex function, making it the IV Induction agent of choice for patients with coronary artery disease, cardiomyopathy, or cerebrovascular disease.

Other options:
Option B: Propofol causes dose-dependent myocardial depression and vasodilation, leading to hypotension.
Option C: Thiopentone (thiopental sodium) causes depression of myocardial contractility and peripheral vasodilation, also lowering blood pressure.
Option D: Ketamine actually Increases heart rate and blood pressure due to sympathomimetic stimulation, but it can cause myocardial depression in catecholamine-depleted patients (e.g., those with severe shock).

stem: Which among the following is most commonly used to monitor the depth of anaesthesia?
subject: Anesthesia
explanation:
Solution
• Bispectral Index (BIS) is the most commonly used monitor to assess the depth of anaesthesia in clinical practice.

• It is a processed EEG parameter that provides a single numerical value (0-100) reflecting the patient's level of consciousness.
1. 100 = Fully awake
2. 40-60 = Optimal range for general anaesthesia
3. 0 = Absence of brain electrical activity
• BIS helps prevent intraoperative awareness and optimizes anaesthetic dosing.
• Middle latency auditory evoked potential is the most sensitive indicator for assessing depth of anaesthesia, though less commonly used.

Other options:
Option A: EEG can monitor anaesthetic depth but is not routinely used due to complex interpretation.
Option B: Provoked lower oesophageal contractility is not related to anaesthetic depth monitoring.
Option C: Entropy is another processed EEG monitor similar to BIS, but BIS Is more widely used in clinical practice.

stem: A young female presented with vaginal itching and green frothy genital discharge. Strawberry cervix is seen on examination. What will be the drug of choice?
subject: Obstetrics and Gynaecology, Pharmacology
explanation:
Solution
• The presence of vaginal Itching, green frothy discharge, and a strawberry cervix is characteristic of Trichomoniasis.
• Metronidazole is the drug of choice for trichomoniasis. It is caused by Trichomonas vaginalis, an anaerobic, flagellated protozoan that exists only in the trophozolte form (no cyst stage).
• The infection is usually sexually transmitted, and it is the most common non-viral sexually transmitted Infection worldwide.
• Diagnosis:
1. Wet mount microscopy shows motile, flagellated trophozoltes.
2. Direct Immunofluorescent antibody testing and NAAT (Nucleic Acid Amplification Test) are more sensitive.
3. Culture can be performed on Feinberg-Whittington medium.
• Treatment:
1. Metronidazole 2 g orally in a single dose is the treatment of choice.
2. All sexual partners should be treated simultaneously to prevent reinfection.
3. Tinidazole can be used as an alternative.





Other options:
Option A: It is used for chlamydial infections, not for trichomoniasis.
Option B: It is used for vulvovaginal candidiasis.
Option D: It is used for Chlamydia trachomatis and Mycoplasma genitallum infections.

stem: The most common triggering factor of the given condition is
subject: Dermatology, Microbiology
explanation:
Solution
• The given Image shows target lesions, which are characteristic of Erythema Multiforme (EM).
• The most common trigger of erythema multiforme is Infection, particularly Herpes Simplex Virus (HSV) infection.
• Erythema multiforme is a self-limiting cytotoxic dermatitis resulting from cell-mediated hypersensitivity.
• Clinically, It presents as macular, papular, or urticarial lesions with typical "target" (Iris) lesions usually on the acral surfaces such as palms, soles, and extensor limbs.
• Mucosal involvement (oral or genital) may occur with painful erosions.
• Target lesions are:
1. <3 cm in diameter
2. Have three concentric zones: Central dusky erythema or purpura, Middle paler edematous zone, Outer erythematous ring with a well-defined edge



Other options:
Option A: Vaccination may rarely trigger EM but is not the most common cause.
Option B: Malignancy-associated EM is uncommon and seen in paraneoplastic settings.
Option C: Drugs can cause EM but are less frequent triggers than Infections.

stem: A 5-year-old boy had painful sores in the mouth with a rash over the hands along with fever. Which of these is the causative agent?
subject: Pediatrics, Dermatology
explanation:
Solution
• The clinical presentation of fever, painful oral sores, and vesicular rash on the hands and feet in a child is characteristic of Hand-Foot-and-Mouth Disease (HFMD).

• Causative Agent: The most common cause is Coxsackievirus A16, a member of the Picornaviridae family (enterovirus group).
• Clinical Features:
1. Incubation period: Around 3-7 days.
2. Prodrome: Mild fever, malaise, and sore throat.
3. Oral lesions: Aphthold ulcers on the buccal mucosa, tongue, and soft palate; these are less painful than typical aphthous ulcers.
4. Skin lesions: Small, oval vesicles with a graylsh roof and erythematous halo, seen mainly on palms, soles, and sometimes buttocks.
5. Lesions usually heal spontaneously within 7-10 days without scarring.
• Mode of Transmission: Fecal-oral route and respiratory droplets.
• Treatment: Supportive and symptomatic: Antipyretics, adequate hydration, and oral care.

Other options:
Option A: It causes diseases like molluscum contagiosum or smallpox, not HFMD.
Option C: It is associated with roseola infantum (exanthem subitum), not HFMD.
Option D: It causes erythema infectiosum (fifth disease), not HFMD.

stem: What is the diagnosis seen in the image below?
subject: Dermatology
explanation:
Solution
• The image shows a characteristic presentation of Candidal Intertrigo, a superficial Candida Infection that occurs in opposing skin folds such as the axillae, Inframammary areas, groin, and interdigital spaces.

• Clinical Features:
1. Bright red erythema with maceration and moist exudation.
2. Satellite lesions small pustules or papules around the main area of involvement.
• Common sites: Axillae, inframammary areas, groin, Intergluteal cleft, and Interdigital spaces.
• When the web spaces of toes or fingers are involved, white, soggy thickening of the stratum corneum may be seen.
• Pathophysiology: Friction, heat, and moisture in skin folds create a favorable environment for Candida overgrowth and often occurs in obese Individuals, diabetics, or those with Immunosuppression.

Other options:
Option A: It affects nail folds, causing erythema, swelling, and nall dystrophy. Does not present as large Intertriginous erythematous patches.
Option B: It presents with widespread superficial blistering, positive Nikolsky sign and not localized intertrigo with satellite lesions.
Option D: It is characterized by neuropathic or ischemic ulcers, not moist erythematous plaques with satellite pustules.

stem: A 15-year-old giri presents with itchy lesions on her arm as shown. Her family history is positive for asthma. What could be the most probable diagnosis?
subject: Dermatology, Pediatrics
explanation:
Solution
• Pruritic lesions in flexural areas with a positive family history of asthma strongly indicate atopic dermatitis, an atopic triad condition.
• Pruritus is the hallmark, leading to scratch marks and excoriations, as seen in the image.
• AD shows erythematous papules, vesicles, and later lichenification, with chronic relapsing episodes beginning in childhood.
• Distribution varles with age: infancy-face and extensor areas; childhood/adolescence-flexural involvement.
• Filaggrin dysfunction from FLG gene mutations weakens the epidermal barrier, causing xerosis and increased susceptibility to irritants and allergens.
• Environmental triggers (detergents, hard water, heat, friction) further worsen barrier damage and precipitate exacerbations.

Other options:
Option A: Seborrhoeic dermatitis affects scalp, face, and presternal area with greasy scales does not match flexural Itchy lesions with atopy.


Option C: Allergic contact dermatitis is a type IV hypersensitivity reaction with sharply demarcated vesicles at the site of allergen contact.


Option D: Erysipelas is an acute streptococcal infection causing painful, sharply demarcated erythema with systemic symptoms -absent here.

stem: What is correct regarding the diagnosis based on the given Image?
options: Trigeminal dermatome is most commonly affected
Anterior nerve roots are more commonly involved
Mucous membranes within the affected dermatomes are involved
subject: Dermatology, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
• The image depicts unilateral, painful, grouped vesicles distributed along a single dermatome, which is characteristic of Herpes Zoster (Shingles).

• Mucous membranes within the affected dermatomes can also be involved in herpes zoster, particularly in cases Involving the trigeminal or sacral dermatomes.
• Pathogenesis:
1. It results from the reactivation of latent varicella-zoster virus (VZV), which remains dormant in the dorsal root ganglia after a primary varicella (chickenpox) Infection.
2. Reactivation occurs when cell-mediated Immunity declines (e.g., with age, stress, or immunosuppression).
3. The virus travels along sensory nerve fibers to the skin, producing vesicular eruptions confined to the dermatome supplied by the affected sensory nerve.
• Clinical Features:
1. Prodrome: Pain, burning, or tingling in the affected dermatome, preceding rash by 1-3 days.
2. Eruption: Erythematous macules -> papules -> vesicles -> pustules -> crusting over 7-10 days.
3. Distribution: Always unilateral, not crossing the midline.
4. Common sites: Thoracic dermatomes (most frequent), followed by trigeminal.

Other options:
Option A: Herpes zoster is infectious.
Option B: Thoracic dermatomes are most commonly affected, not trigeminal.
Option C: Posterior (sensory) nerve roots are involved, not anterior.

stem: Bindi Leukoderma is caused by which chemical?
options: Croceln Scarlet MOO and Solvent Yellow 3
subject: Dermatology
explanation:
Solution
• Bindi leukoderma (also known as contact leukoderma or chemical leukoderma) is caused by para tertiary butylphenol (PTBP) present in the adhesive of sticker bindis.
• PTBP acts as a melanocytotoxic chemical-it causes increased sensitivity of melanocytes and induces apoptosis (cell death) in the area of contact, leading to localized depigmentation.
• The condition is common in indian women who use adhesive bindis, typically appearing as a well-defined depigmented patch corresponding to the site of bindi application.
• Pathogenesis: PTBP and related phenolic compounds structurally resemble tyrosine, the substrate of tyrosinase, a key enzyme in melanin synthesis. This similarity allows them to compete with tyrosine, inhibiting melanin production and causing selective destruction of melanocytes. Once depigmentation develops, it may spread beyond the site of contact due to autoimmune sensitization against melanocytes.
• Common chemicals in the Indian set up which cause Chemical leukoderma are:
Bindi LeukodermaPara Tertiary butyl phenol (PTBP)
Synthetic leather, rubber slippers, Latex condoms, rubber glovesMono-benzyl ether of Hydroquinone (MBH) - Rubber Antioxidant
Hair dyep-phenylenediamine (PPD)
Alta (red colour like henna)Crocein Scarlet MOO and Solvent Yellow 3 (azo dyes)







Other options:
Option A: It causes depigmentation when used in bleaching creams or in workers handling rubber products, not bindi leukoderma.
Option B: It is associated with textile dye-induced leukoderma, especially in garment workers.
Option C: It causes hair dye dermatitis and may lead to contact leukoderma on areas exposed to hair dye, such as the scalp or forehead.

stem: Which of the following is commonly associated with the condition shown below?
subject: Dermatology
explanation:
Solution
• The condition shown in the image is Alopecia areata, an autoimmune, non-scarring alopecia that targets hair follicies.
• The characteristic nail finding in alopecia areata is fine stippled pitting of the nails.
• These pits are usually regularly arranged and smaller than those seen in psoriasis.
• Nail changes are observed in up to 10-20% of cases and may precede or follow hair loss.
• Clinical Features: Presents as smooth, circumscribed patches of hair loss with no visible inflammation or scaling.
• The "exclamation mark hair" a short, broken hair that is narrower at the base is pathognomonic.
• White or gray hairs within the patch are often spared.
• The disease may progress to alopecia totalis (loss of all scalp hair) or alopecia universalls (loss of all body hair).



Other options:
Option A: It is seen in psoriasis, not alopecia areata; appears as a translucent yellow-red discoloration beneath the nail plate.


Option B: It is seen as white transverse bands (Mees' lines) in trauma or systemic disease, not associated with alopecia areata.


Option C: It is seen in lichen planus due to adhesion between the proximal nail fold and nail bed, leading to scarring.

stem: Which of the following drugs is used as an anti-smoking agent?
subject: Pharmacology, Psychiatry
explanation:
Solution
• Varenicline is a partial agonist at the a4ẞ2 subtype of nicotinic acetylcholine receptors in the brain.

• It reduces craving and withdrawal symptoms associated with smoking cessation by providing partial receptor stimulation.
• It also blocks nicotine's rewarding effects if the person smokes while on the drug.
• It is one of the FDA-approved drugs for smoking cessation along with Bupropion and Nicotine Replacement Therapy (NRT).

Other options:
Option A: It is an antineoplastic (alkylating) agent used in chronic myeloid leukernia, not for smoking cessation.
Option B: It is used in maintaining abstinence in alcohol dependence by modulating glutamatergic transmission, not in nicotine addiction.
Option D: It is an anticonvulsant and neuropathic pain agent, not approved for smoking cessation.

stem: What is the fear of heights known as?
subject: Psychiatry
explanation:
Solution
• Acrophobia refers to an Intense or irrational fear of heights, classified under specific phoblas in anxiety disorders.

• It often leads to avoidance of high places such as stairs, terraces, balconies, or mountains.
• Patients may experience symptoms like dizziness, Imbalance, sweating, palpitations, and panic when exposed to height-related situations.
• The fear is disproportionate to the actual danger posed by the situation.
• Behavioral therapy, especially systematic desensitization and exposure therapy, is highly effective in treatment.
• In severe cases, SSRIs or benzodiazepines may be used as adjuncts.

PhobiaFear of
AcarophobiaSmall bugs
AcrophobiaHigh places
AgoraphobiaOpen or crowded places, leaving familiar setting of home
AichmophobiaPointed objects
AilurophobiaCats
AlgophobiaPain
AndrophobiaMen
Astraphobia or astrapophobiaThunder and lightning
AutophobiaBeing alone, or of self
BathophobiaDepths
ClaustrophobiaClosed or confined places
CoprophobiaExcreta
CynophobiaDogs
DemophobiaCrowds
ErythrophobiaBlushing
GynophobiaWomen
HaphephobiaBeing touched
HematophobiaSight of blood
HydrophobiaWater
HypnophobiaSleep
Lalophobia or glossophobiaSpeaking
MysophobiaDirt, contamination
NecrophobiaDead bodies
NyctophobiaDarkness, night
PanphobiaEverything
Pathophobia or nosophobiaDisease, suffering
PeccatophobiaSinning
PedophobiaChildren
PhonophobiaSpeaking aloud
PhotophobiaStrong light
SitophobiaEating
TaphophobiaBeing burled allve
ThanatophobiaDeath
ToxozihobiaBeing poisoned
XenophobiaStrangers
ZoophobiaAnimals

Other options:
Option A: It is the fear of mites or small insects.
Option B: It is the fear of pain.
Option C: It is the fear of open or public spaces.

stem: Premature ejaculation is a disorder of which phase of the normal sexual cycle?
subject: Psychiatry
explanation:
Solution
• Premature ejaculation (PE) is classified as a disorder of the orgasm phase in the human sexual response cycle.

• It is characterized by ejaculation occurring before or shortly after penetration, leading to distress or unsatisfactory sexual activity.
• The underlying causes can be biological (e.g., increased penile sensitivity, neurotransmitter imbalance) or psychological (e.g., performance anxiety, relationship issues).
• It is one of the most common male sexual dysfunctions, often associated with anxiety or early sexual conditioning.
• Treatment options include behavioral therapy (stop-start technique, squeeze technique), SSRIs (like dapoxetine), and topical anesthetic agents.
• Counseling and couple therapy are often recommended to address psychological factors.

PhaseCharacteristicsDisorderTreatment
DesireFocuses on the patient's drives, motivation, and desiresHypoactive sexual desire: Decrease or absence of sexual fantasies, desires etc. Sexual aversion: Complete aversion to all sexual contactAddress issues like guilt, poor self-esteem, homosexual impulses, etc. Couples therapy if marital conflict
ArousalConsists of a sense of sexual pleasure with accompanying physiologic changesFemale sexual arousal disorder: Persistent fallure to achieve/maintain sufficient arousal. Impotence: Inability to attain or maintain an erection until completion of sexual actAddress issues like guilt, anxiety, fear. Check for medications causing vaginal dryness. Relaxation techniques. Determine if organic/psychological. Consider plethysmography or postage stamp test
OrgasmPhysiologic state in which sexual tension is released and contractions occur in various organsFemale orgasmic disorder & delayed ejaculation: Inability to achieve orgasm through masturbation or intercourse. Premature ejaculation: Ejaculation before desired, before penetration or just after penetrationAddress gullt, fear of Impregnation. Use vibrators, education, fantasy. Behavioral techniques (squeeze, stop-and-go). Address anxiety. SSRIs to delay ejaculation
PainSubjective sense of pain with sexual act; usually due to dynamic factorsGenito-pelvic pain disorder: Pain during intercourse (male or female). Penetration disorder: Involuntary constriction of outer 1/3 of vagina Interfering with intercourseHelp with anxiety, tension. Behavioral techniques (dilators, relaxation). Address issues of fear, religion, upbringing

Other options:
Option A: The desire phase involves sexual interest or libido: disorders Include hypoactive sexual desire disorder.
Option B: The arousal phase involves physiological excitement; disorders include erectile dysfunction.
Option D: The pain phase is not part of the normal sexual response cycle but refers to conditions like dyspareunia.

stem: Confusion assessment test is used in:
subject: Psychiatry
explanation:
Solution
• The Confusion Assessment Method (CAM), also known as the confusion assessment test, is specifically designed to diagnose delirium.

• It identifies key features: acute onset and fluctuating course, Inattention, disorganized thinking, and altered level of consciousness.
• Delirium is an acute, reversible disorder of attention and awareness, often secondary to an underlying medical condition.
• The CAM can be performed quickly at the bedside, making it highly practical for ICU and ward settings.
• Early detection allows prompt treatment of the cause, thereby reducing morbidity and mortality.
• It effectively distinguishes delirium from chronic cognitive impairments like dementia.

Other options:
Option B: Used for chronic cognitive decline, not acute confusional states.
Option C: Used for psychotic disorders; does not assess consciousness or attention.
Option D: Used for mood disorders; not associated with acute confusion.

stem: What is the false statement regarding somnambulism:
options: Usually, terminates in awakening followed by confusion
There may be neurological condition associated
subject: Psychiatry
explanation:
Solution
• Somnambulism (sleepwalking) is a non-REM arousal disorder, occurring during the first third of the night from slow-wave sleep.

• During episodes the person exhibits Incomplete arousal, with reduced awareness, Impaired judgement, and amnesia for the event; hence they do not have full consciousness.
• Commonly, the episode ends with partial awakening or returning to bed; confusion and disorientation on full awakening are usual.
• There is a clear familial and genetic predisposition, and somnambulism may be associated with other neurological or sleep-disorder conditions.
• Events occurring during NREM stages 3 and 4:
EventsDescription
SomnambulismSleepwalking
Sleep terror or Night terrorAlso called pavor nocturnus. Awakens with a scream or intense anxiety. Has increased autonomic disturbances. No memory of this after waking. Runs in familles, more frequent in childhood and boys. Treatment with benzodiazepines is rarely required.
Nocturnal enuresisBehavioral therapy is the mainstay. Drugs like desmopressin and imipramine are used.

• Events occurring in REM sleep:
EventsDescription
NightmaresFearful sleep without autonomic disturbances, remembered in the morning. TCAs may be used for treatment.
Nocturnal penile tumescenceNormal phenomenon, used to differentiate psychogenic and organic impotence.
NarcolepsyDisorder of REM sleep, disturbed night sleep excessive daytime sleep. Associated with cataplexy, hypnogogic and hypnopompic hallucination, sleep paralysis.

• Other events:
EventsDescription
Bruxism (teeth grinding)Occurs in stage 2 NREM sleep.
Frontal lobe epllepsyOccurs in stage 2 NREM sleep.
Sleep talkingOccurs in all stages of sleep. May accompany night terrors and sleepwalking. Common in children. No treatment necessary.

Other options:
Option A: True - It is a disorder of arousal from sleep.
Option C: True - It usually terminates with awakening followed by confusion.
Option D: True - Neurological conditions or other sleep disorders can co-exist.

stem: What is the false statement regarding melancholia?
options: Profound feelings of guilt over trivial events
subject: Psychiatry
explanation:
Solution
• Melancholia (or depression with melancholic features) is marked by a severe loss of pleasure or interest in most activities (anhedonia).

• It often involves early morning awakening (terminal insomnia) rather than difficulty falling asleep (sleep-onset insomnia).
• Significant weight loss or marked decrease in appetite is a typical vegetative or somatic symptom in melancholic depression.
• Profound feelings of guilt, often over trivial matters, are characteristic of the melancholic subtype of depression.
• Therefore, the statement "sleep-onset insomnia" is false in this context because melancholia is more associated with terminal Insomnia rather than initial insomnia.
• Melancholia is sometimes referred to as "endogenous depression", or depression that arises in the absence of external life stressors or precipitants.

Other options:
Option A: Severe anhedonia is characteristic.
Option B: Weight loss is common.
Option D: Profound feelings of guilt over trivial events is characteristic.

stem: Which of the following is not a risk factor for delusional disorder?
subject: Psychiatry
explanation:
Solution
• Delusional disorder is more common in middle-aged and elderly individuals; thus, young age is not a typical risk factor.

• Advanced age increases susceptibility due to sensory deficits and social isolation.
• Sensory Impairment (such as hearing or vision loss) can predispose to delusional beliefs.
• Personality traits like suspiciousness or interpersonal sensitivity are often noted in affected individuals.
• Social Isolation and recent Immigration can contribute to feelings of alienation, Increasing vulnerability.
• Family history of psychotic disorders may indicate genetic predisposition.
• Types of delusions:
Type of DelusionDescription
Delusion of controlFalse belief that a person's will, thoughts, or feelings are being controlled by external forces.
Delusion of grandeur or MegalomaniaExaggerated conception of one's importance, power, or Identity.
Delusion of infidelity or morbid jealousy or Othello syndromeFalse bellef that one's lover is unfaithful; sometimes called pathological Jealousy.
Delusion of love or erotomaniaFalse belief that another person is in love with them. In De Clerambault syndrome, the woman believes a man (frequently older and of higher status) is In love with her.
Delusion of misidentificationCapgras syndrome: Belief that a familiar person has been replaced by an exact double (an impostor). Fregoll syndrome: Bellef that different people are actually one single person in disguise.
Nihilistic delusionFalse belief that oneself, others, or the world is non-existent/about to end. With bodily failure ideas (e.g., putrefaction of intestine), called Cotard syndrome.
Delusion of infestationFalse bellef of being infested with small but visible organisms. When monosymptomatic, It is called Ekbom syndrome.
Delusion of persecutionThe most common delusion. False belief of being harassed or persecuted.
Delusion of povertyStrong false belief of being financially incapacitated.
Delusion of referenceFalse bellef that others' behavior has unusual significance (e.g., TV people talking about them).
Delusion of self-accusationFalse feelings of remorse and gullt. Seen in depression with psychotic features.

Other options:
Option A: Recent Immigration - Recognized as a risk factor.
Option B: Social Isolation - Commonly associated with delusional disorder.
Option C: Family history - Increases susceptibility to psychotic disorders.

stem: Which of the following has a poor prognosis with exposure and response prevention In OCD?
subject: Psychiatry
explanation:
Solution
• Hoarding behavior is associated with poor response to exposure and response prevention (ERP) therapy in OCD.

• Individuals with hoarding often show poor Insight, believing their possessions are valuable or necessary.
• They tend to have low motivation and resistance to discarding Items, making ERP implementation difficult.
• The behavior is now recognized as a distinct entity-Hoarding Disorder-in DSM-5, separate from typical OCD presentations.
• Neurobiological differences and strong attachment to possessions contribute to treatment resistance.
• Specialized cognitive-behavioral interventions focusing on decision-making and emotional attachment are more effective.

Other options:
Option A: Responds well to exposure and response prevention, as pathological doubt is a core OCD symptom targeted by ERP.
Option B: Often improves with cognitive-behavioral therapy, since magical thinking can be challenged through behavioral experiments.
Option D: Typically shows good prognosis with ERP, as contamination fears can be systematically desensitized through graded exposure.

stem: Which of the following is a water-soluble contrast?
subject: Radiology
explanation:
Solution
• Iohexol is a non-lonic, water-soluble lodinated contrast agent commonly used in diagnostic radiology.

• It provides excellent visualization in CT scans, anglography, urography, and myelography.
• Being water-soluble, it is safe for Intravenous, oral, and Intrathecal use.
• It is a low-osmolar contrast medium, reducing the risk of allergic and nephrotoxic reactions.
• It is marketed under brand names such as Omnipaque. It is the contrast of choice when a perforation or leak is suspected, as it is absorbed by the body.

Other options:
Option B: This is an insoluble contrast medium, not suitable for use when perforation or leakage is suspected.
Option C: This element is not used as a contrast agent in radiology.
Option D: This mineral occurs naturally in the body and has no application as a radiological contrast medium.

stem: Which of the following investigations is shown in the radiograph?
subject: Radiology
explanation:
Solution
• A barium meal is a double-contrast radiographic study used to examine the lower esophagus, stomach, and duodenum.

• The patient ingests barlum sulfate, which coats the mucosal surface, allowing for detailed visualization It helps in detecting ulcers, polyps, tumors, and gastric outlet obstruction.
• Air may be introduced to provide a double-contrast effect, improving mucosal detail.
• It is typically followed by a barium meal follow-through if small bowel evaluation is needed. This Investigation is valuable for diagnosing peptic ulcer disease and gastric carcinoma.
• GI Imaging - Preparations of barium:
InvestigationPreparationStructures best visualized
Barium swallowBarium pasteEsophagus
Barium meal95% BaSO4Stomach and duodenum
Barium meal follow-through50% BaSO4Small bowel
Barium enema25% BaSO4Large bowel

Barium swallow used to assess the pharynx, esophagus and stomach

Barium swallow showing eosinophilic esophagitis and associated strictures

Barium meal used to assess stomach and duodenum

Barium meal follow-throughused to assess Small intestine

Barium enema used to assess the large intestine


Other options:
Option A: Used to study the pharynx and upper esophagus, not the stomach or duodenum.
Option C: Examines the small intestine after a barlum meal, not limited to the stomach.
Option D: Used for imaging the large Intestine (colon), not the upper gastrointestinal tract.

stem: The following appearance in IVP is caused by:
subject: Radiology, Surgery
explanation:
Solution
• The cobra head (adder head) deformity or spring onion sign seen on Intravenous pyelography (IVP) is characteristic of ureterocele.

• It represents a dilated, contrast-filled distal ureter surrounded by a thin radiolucent halo of the ureteric wall and bladder mucosa.
• Ureterocele is a cystic dilation of the intramural portion of the ureter, typically caused by ureteric orifice atresia. It is more common in females and may be bilateral.
• Children usually present with recurrent urinary tract Infections, while adults may present with ureteric calculi.
• On ultrasonography, it appears as a fluid-filled cystic structure within the bladder, often near the trigone. Treatment: Surgical excision with re-implantation of the ureter.
• IVU signs and associated conditions:
1. Medullary sponge kidney: "Bouquet of flowers / paintbrush" appearance. Refers to cystic dilatation of distal collecting tubules.




2. Duplex kidney: Drooping lily sign. It refers to the inferolateral displacement of the lower pole due to an obstructed upper pole in a duplicated collecting system.


3. Horseshoe kidney: Handshake sign: Due to the position of the lower poles closer to the spine, and the lower calyces closer to each other. Flower vase sign: Due to medially rotated calyces.


4. Retroperitoneal fibrosis: Malden waist deformity. It refers to the medially drawn ureters caused by fibrosis at the lumbosacral junction.


5. Retrocaval ureter: Fish hook/Reverse J deformity. Right ureter hooks behind the IVC as it passes posterior to it.


6. Ureterocele: Cobra / Adder head appearance. It refers to the dilation of the distal ureter.


7. Transitional cell carcinoma of the ureter: Goblet sign. It is due to the focal dilation of the ureter by an intraluminal mass.



Other options:
Option A: This condition results in fusion of the lower poles of the kidneys but does not show a cobra head appearance.
Option C: May cause irregular füling defects in the bladder but not the characteristic cobra head sign.
Option D: Leads to bladder distension and posterior urethral dilation, not the classic IVP finding described.

stem: What is the best imaging study for the earliest diagnosis of cerebral infarct?
subject: Radiology, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
• Diffusion-weighted MRI (DWI) is the most sensitive imaging modality for detecting acute cerebral infarction.
• It can Identify Ischemic changes within 30 minutes of onset, well before structural changes appear on other modalities.
• DWI detects cytotoxic edema, which results from restricted water diffusion in ischemic brain tissue.
• It helps distinguish acute Ischemic stroke from chronic Infarcts or other lesions.
• Although non-contrast CT (NCCT) is the first-line imaging in suspected stroke (to exclude hemorrhage), DWI provides the earliest and most definitive diagnosis of infarction.
• DWI is also useful in guiding thrombolytic therapy and assessing stroke evolution and prognosis.
Acute MCA territory infarct on CT is shown below

The image below is a DWI MRI of a patientclearly showing the extent of the infarct


Other options:
Option A: FLAIR MRI is sensitive to edema but changes appear several hours after onset, not Ideal for early diagnosis.
Option B: Non-contrast CT is performed first to exclude hemorrhage but may miss very early Ischemic changes.
Option C: CECT is mainly used to identify vascular anatomy or hemorrhage, not for early Infarct detection.

stem: Match the following intractanial bleeds:
subject: Radiology, Surgery
explanation:
Solution
• Epidural Hematoma (A-II): Characterized by a biconvex or lentiform (lens-shaped) hyperdense area on non-contrast CT Usually results from middle meningeal artery rupture secondary to temporal bone fracture Does not cross suture lines but may cross dural attachments.
• A lucid Interval between trauma and neurological deterioration is a classic feature.
• Subdural Hematoma (B-IV): Appears as a crescent-shaped hyperdense collection between the brain and skull Caused by rupture of bridging veins.


• Can cross sutures but does not cross midline dural reflections (falx or tentorium) Seen in elderly, alcoholics, and patients on anticoagulants.
• Subarachnoid Hemorrhage (C-I): Blood in the basal cisterns and sulci, giving "star-shaped" or Texaco star appearance on CT Most commonly due to ruptured berry aneurysm or head trauma.
• Clinically presents with a sudden severe headache ("thunderclap headache"). CT brain shows hyperdensity in the subarachnoid space: CSF shows xanthochromia.


• Intracerebral Hemorrhage (D-III): It appears as a hyperdense area within the brain parenchyma, commonly in the basal ganglia (putamen) or thalamus.
• Most often caused by hypertension or rupture of microaneurysms. Can cause mass effect and midline shift.
• Management Includes blood pressure control and surgical evacuation if large or life-threatening.


• Types of Intracranial Hemorrhage:
1. Extradural hemorrhage / epidural hematoma (EDH)


2. Acute subdural hemorrhage (SDH)


3. Acute on chronic SDH


4. Subarachnoid hemorrhage


5. Intra-parenchymal hemorrhage



Other options:
Option A: Incorrect matching pattern of hemorrhage types.
Option C: Incorrect mapping of bleed types to radiological features.
Option D: Incorrect associations between bleed types and Imaging signs.

stem: During evaluation of PUO, a patient's chest X-ray is as seen below. What is your diagnosis?
subject: Radiology, Medicine, Microbiology
explanation:
Solution
• Millary tuberculosis results from hematogenous dissemination of Mycobacterium tuberculosis.

• The chest X-ray shows Innumerable, uniformly distributed, tiny (1-5 mm) nodules throughout both lung fields, resembling millet seeds hence the term millary.
• These millary nodules represent tuberculous granulomas deposited via the bloodstream.
• Sputum smear is often negative, and bronchoalveolar lavage or transbronchial blopsy may be needed for diagnosis.
• Common systemic findings include fever, hepatosplenomegaly, and lymphadenopathy. Other diseases that can produce similar miliary patterns include sarcoidosis, histoplasmosis, and pneumoconiosis.
• Causes of Miliary Pattern:
1. Millary tuberculosis
2. Histoplasmosis
3. Sarcoidosis
4. Pneumoconiosis - coal workers pneumoconiosis and silicosis
5. Healed varicella
6. Long-standing MS - can lead to pulmonary hemosiderosis
7. Alveolar microlithiasis
8. Papillary and medullary Ca thyroid

Other options:
Option B: Pulmonary edema shows bilateral perihilar "bat-wing" opacities, not uniformly scattered small nodules.
Option C: Necrobiotic nodules are seen in rheumatoid arthritis and are larger, peripheral, and non-uniform.
Option D: Tuberculoma appears as a solitary, well-circumscribed round opacity, not multiple fine nodules.

stem: The following Images A and B show:
subject: Orthopaedics
explanation:
Solution
• Jersey finger refers to an avulsion Injury of the flexor digitorum profundus tendon from its insertion at the base of the distal phalanx, most commonly affecting the ring finger.
• It occurs when a flexed finger is forcibly extended, such as when grabbing a jersey during a tackle.
• Clinically, the patient is unable to flex the DIP Joint of the affected finger.
• Baseball finger (Mallet finger), also called drop finger, results from extensor tendon rupture or avulsion fracture at the dorsal base of the distal phalanx.
• It occurs when a sudden Impact (like a ball strike) forces the DIP joint into hyperflexion while the finger is extended.
• The affected finger droops at the DIP Joint and cannot actively extend.






• Trigger finger: A nodule on the flexor tendon prevents it from moving along the A1 pulley. This causes the finger to be stuck in a flexed position.


• Boxer's fracture: it is a break in the neck of the metacarpal. It is caused by striking an object with a closed fist, as seen in boxing. The little finger is the most commonly affected.



Other options:
Option A: Incorrect pairing these two are not shown together in the given images.
Option B: Boxer and March fractures involve metacarpal and metatarsal bones, not fingers.
Option C: Incorrect combination Mallet and Boxer fractures are unrelated injuries.

stem: Identify the structure marked in the following MRI scan:
subject: Radiology, Anatomy
explanation:
Solution
• The cerebellum is located in the posterior cranial fossa, beneath the occipital lobes and behind the brainstem.

• On sagittal MRI, It appears as a densely follated structure with the characteristic vermis and cerebellar hemispheres.
• It is responsible for coordination of voluntary movements, balance, and posture.
• The cerebellum is separated from the cerebrum by the tentorium cerebelli. Lesions in this region may result in ataxia, dysmetria, or intention tremor.
• It receives input from the spinal cord, vestibular system, and cerebral cortex to fine-tune motor activity.
• The other areas also visualized in the image are: Cerebrum, Corpus callosum, Thalamus, Pons, 4th ventricle, Cerebellum, Medulla.



Other options:
Option A: Midbrain is part of the brainstem, located superior to the pons, not the marked structure.
Option C: Brainstem includes midbrain, pons, and medulla, but the marked structure is distinct and posterior.
Option D: Cerebrum occupies the anterior and superior portion of the cranial cavity, not the posterior inferior region shown.

stem: A 30-year-old HIV-positive man presents with fever for 3 weeks, dry cough, and significant weight loss. He complains of retrosternal chest pain that is worse on inspiration. His chest X-ray is given below. What is the most likely diagnosis?
subject: Radiology, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
• Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia (PCP) is the most common opportunistic infection in HIV-positive patients, especially with CD4+ counts <200/µL.

• Presents with fever, non-productive cough, dyspnea, and retrosternal pleuritic chest pain.
• Chest X-ray typically shows diffuse bilateral interstitial infiltrates, often symmetric and perihilar.
• Symptoms develop gradually over days to weeks, unlike bacterial pneumonia which is more acute.
• Characteristic retrosternal chest pain worsens on Inspiration due to involvement of lung interstitium.
• Early recognition is crucial, as untreated PCP can lead to respiratory fallure, and treatment includes trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole.
• Typical Pneumonia v/s Atypical Pneumonia:
Typical pneumoniaAtypical pneumonia
Extracellular organismIntracellular organism
Alveolar exudates in air spacesInterstitial involvement
Neutrophilic in natureLymphocytic in nature
High-grade feverLow-grade fever
Productive coughDry cough hemoptysis scanty sputum
Pleuritic chest painExtrapulmonary manifestation
X-Ray shows large areas of consolidationX-Ray shows an interstitial pattern of involvement

Other options:
Option A: Staphylococcal pneumonia presents with lobar consolidation, cavitation, and rapid onset, usually not diffuse interstitial pattern.


Option B: Pneumococcal pneumonia typically shows lobar consolidation and productive cough.


Option C: Tuberculosis may present with upper lobe infiltrates or cavitation, but diffuse interstitial pattern with retrosternal pain is less typical.

stem: Which of the following is the best treatment for the relapse of immune thrombocytopenic purpura?
subject: Medicine, Surgery
explanation:
Solution
• In relapsed or chronic ITP, splenectomy is the definitive treatment after fallure of medical therapy.

• Removes the primary site of antibody-mediated platelet destruction.
• Reduces autoantibody formation, improving platelet lifespan.
• Achieves long-term remission in around 70-80% of cases.
• Considered after 6-12 months of persistent or recurrent disease despite therapy.
• ITP is an acquired disorder characterized by the Immune-mediated destruction of platelets and possibly inhibition of platelet release from the megakaryocyte.
• Secondary causes of ITP include systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE), HIV-AIDS, and Hepatitis C.
• The treatment of ITP is as follows:
Clinical featuresManagement
Minor bleeding manifestations (and/or) Platelet count >5000/µLPrednisone (OR) Anti-D immunoglobulin therapy - effective only in Rh-positive patients (OR) Intravenous immunoglobulin (IVIG)
Severe ITP (any of the following): Significant Gl/intracranial bleeding. Platelet count <5000/µL. Signs of Impending bleeding (retinal or large oral mucosal bleeds)Platelet transfusion (AND) High-dose glucocorticoids (AND) IVIG/Anti-D immunoglobulins + Rituximab
Relapse after first-line therapySplenectomy
Unresponsive to all other therapy or relapse after splenectomyThrombopoietin receptor agonists - romiplostim (subcutaneous), eltrombopag (oral)

Other options:
Option A: Provides temporary increase in platelet count and is used for acute bleeding or peri-surgical support, not long-term control.
Option B: Useful as initial therapy but relapse often occurs after tapering; not suitable for chronic disease management.
Option D: Platelet transfusion is ineffective because transfused platelets are rapidly destroyed by circulating antibodies.

stem: Cushing's ulcers are seen in:
subject: Surgery, Pathology
explanation:
Solution
• Cushing's ulcers occur in patients with head injury or intracranial tumors due to increased vagal stimulation, leading to excessive gastric acid secretion.

• They typically affect the acid-producing regions of the stomach, mainly the fundus and body.
• Curling's ulcers, in contrast, are stress-related gastric/duodenal ulcers seen in severe burns, due to mucosal ischemia from hypovolemia.
• Stress-related gastric ulcers may also occur in severe shock, sepsis, or trauma, often presenting with minimal gastrointestinal bleeding.
• Pathophysiology involves Inflammatory mediators and sympathetic/parasympathetic Imbalance affecting gastric mucosal integrity.

Other options:
Option A: Burns lead to Curling's ulcers, not Cushing's ulcers.
Option B: Stress alone can cause stress ulcers, but Cushing's ulcers are specifically associated with Intracranial pathology.
Option D: Cell necrosis is not a cause of Cushing's ulcers.

stem: Osborn waves in ECG are seen in:
subject: Medicine
explanation:
Solution
• Osborn waves (J waves) are classically seen in hypothermia, appearing as a distinct positive deflection at the J point on the ECG.

• They result from prolonged ventricular repolarization due to systemic hypothermia.
• The wave is characterized by an abrupt ascent at the Junction of QRS and ST segment, followed by a sudden return to baseline.
• Mild Osborn waves can occasionally be seen in severe hypothyroidism, but hypothermia is the primary cause.
• Recognition of Osborn waves is clinically important as they may precede ventricular arrhythmias in hypothermic patients.
• Management Involves gradual rewarming and supportive care.
• Osborn wave can also be seen in:
1. Hypercalcemia
2. Brain injury
3. Subarachnoid hemorrhage
4. Vasospastic angina
5. Cardiopulmonary arrest from oversedation
6. Idiopathic ventricular fibrillation

Other options:
Option A: Severe hypothyroidism can rarely show Osborn waves, but hypothermia is usually the underlying mechanism.
Option C: Hypocalcemia produces prolonged QT Interval, not Osborn waves.
Option D: Hypokalemia causes U waves and flattened T waves, not J waves.

stem: Calcitonin levels are increased in:
subject: Medicine, Pathology
explanation:
Solution
• Calcitonin is secreted by parafollicular C-cells of the thyroid gland and acts to lower serum calcium and phosphate.

• Its actions include Inhibition of osteoclast activity in bone, reducing bone resorption, and decreased renal reabsorption of calcium and phosphate.
• In hyperparathyroidism, elevated parathyroid hormone (PTH) increases bone resorption, raising serum calcium levels.
• As a physiologic response to hypercalcemia, calcitonin levels rise to counteract high calcium.
• Thus, increased calcitonin serves as a compensatory mechanism in hyperparathyroidism.
• Calcitonin levels are not significantly altered in hyperthyroidism, hypoparathyroidism, or Cushing's syndrome.
• Biochemical parameters in metabolic bone diseases:
Metabolic bone diseaseCalciumPTHPhosphateALP
Rickets/Osteomalacia↓/N↑↓↑
Primary hyperparathyroidism↑↑↓↑
Secondary hyperparathyroidism↓↑↑↑
Tertiary hyperparathyroidism↑↑↓↑
OsteoporosisNNNN
Paget's diseaseNNN↑
Osteopetrosis↑/N↓/N↓/NN

• ↑: Increased, ↓: Decreased, N: Normal

Other options:
Option A: Hyperthyroidism affects metabolism but does not increase calcitonin significantly.
Option B: Hypoparathyroidism lowers calcium, which does not stimulate calcitonin release.
Option D: Cushing's syndrome primarily affects cortisol and does not influence calcitonin levels.

stem: Which of the following murmur increases on standing?
subject: Medicine
explanation:
Solution
• The systolic murmur of hypertrophic obstructive cardiomyopathy (HOCM) Increases with standing due to reduced preload, which decreases left ventricular end-diastolic volume.

• Reduced ventricular filling narrows the left ventricular outflow tract, Intensifying the murmur.
• HOCM murmur is mid-systolic, best heard at the left sternal border, and may radiate to the apex.
• Murmur intensity also Increases with maneuvers that reduce preload or afterload (e.g., Valsalva, standing, handgrip, or Inotropic stimulation).
• Conversely, the murmur decreases with Increased preload/afterload (e.g., squatting, passive leg raise, or vasoconstrictors).
• Recognition of these dynamic auscultatory changes is key for HOCM diagnosis and differentiation from other systolic murmurs.
• Dynamic auscultation of murmurs:
Bedside ManoeuvreEffect
Inspiration (↑ venous return to right atrium)↑ Intensity of right-sided sounds
Handgrip (↑ afterload)↑ intensity of MR, AR, and VSD murmurs
↓ intensity of hypertrophic cardiomyopathy and AS murmurs
MVP: delayed onset of click/murmur
Valsalva-Phase II, standing up (↓ preload)↑ Intensity of hypertrophic cardiomyopathy murmur
↓ intensity of most murmurs (including AS)
MVP: earlier onset of click/murmur
Rapid squatting (↑ venous return, ↑ preload, ↑ afterload)↑ Intensity of AS, MR, and VSD murmurs
↓ intensity of hypertrophic cardiomyopathy murmur
MVP: later onset of click/murmur


Other options:
Option B: Mitral regurgitation murmur does not Increase on standing; it is generally louder with squatting or handgrip.
Option C: Mitral stenosis murmur does not Increase on standing; intensity increases with exercise or maneuvers Increasing left atrial pressure.
Option D: VSD murmur does not vary significantly with positional changes.

stem: On performing a pulmonary function test, reduction in FEV1/FVC is characteristic of:
subject: Medicine
explanation:
Solution
• Obstructive lung diseases (e.g., asthma, COPD, bronchiectasis) are characterized by airflow limitation, causing a reduction In FEV1/FVC ratio.

• FEV1 (Forced Expiratory Volume in 1 second) measures the volume of air exhaled in the first second of forced expiration.
• FVC (Forced Vital Capacity) is the total volume of air exhaled forcefully after full inspiration.
• In obstructive disease, FEV1 decreases more than FVC, leading to a low FEV1/FVC ratio (<0.7).
• Restrictive lung diseases have a proportionate reduction in both FEV1 and FVC, so FEV1/FVC ratio is normal or slightly Increased.
• Pulmonary function tests help differentiate obstructive from restrictive patterns and guide disease management.
• PFT for obstructive vs restrictive diseases:
PFTObstructive diseaseRestrictive disease
FVCDecreasedDecreased (<80% predicted)
FEV1Decreased (<80% predicted)Decreased
FEV1% (FEV1/FVC)Decreased (<0.7)Normal/Increased (>0.7)
FEF 25-75<50% predictedNormal/decreased
TLCNormal/increasedDecreased
RVIncreasedDecreased
DLCONormal or increased in asthma.
Decreased in emphysema		Decreased in intrinsic restrictive disease. Normal in neuromuscular or musculoskeletal restrictive disease


Other options:
Option A: Restrictive disease shows normal or increased FEV1/FVC ratio.
Option C: Normal lung function has a FEV1/FVC ratio of ~0.8.
Option D: Interstitial lung disease is restrictive in nature; FEV1/FVC ratio is normal or slightly Increased.

stem: Which of the following is true about paroxysmal nocturnal hemoglobinuria?
subject: Medicine, Pathology
explanation:
Solution
• Paroxysmal nocturnal hemoglobinuria (PNH) is an acquired clonal stem cell disorder caused by a mutation in the PIG-A gene on the X chromosome.

• This mutation leads to deficiency of GPI (glycosylphosphatidylinositol) anchor proteins, which are responsible for attaching protective surface proteins like CD55 (Decay Accelerating Factor) and CD59 (Membrane Inhibitor of Reactive Lysis) to the cell membrane.
• Deficiency of these proteins makes RBCs abnormally sensitive to complement-mediated Intravascular hemolysis.
• Hemolysis is typically Intravascular, not extravascular, and occurs more prominently at night due to mild respiratory acidosis during sleep.
• PNH is associated with hemolytic anemia, pancytopenia, and venous thrombosis (especially hepatic or portal veins).
• Diagnosis: Flow cytometry showing absence of CD55 and CD59.
• Treatment: Eculizumab or Ravulizumab (C5 inhibitors); supportive management includes folate and iron supplementation.

Other options:
Option A: The PIG-A mutation is acquired, not inherited.
Option B: Hemolysis is intravascular, not extravascular.
Option D: The anemia is typically normocytic or macrocytic, not microcytic.

stem: Which of the following cardiac chambers is enlarged in mitral stenosis on chest X-ray Initially?
subject: Radiology, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
• The left atrium (LA) is the first cardiac chamber to enlarge in mitral stenosis (MS) due to pressure overload from the stenotic mitral valve.

• LA enlargement can be appreciated on lateral and left anterior oblique chest X-ray views.
• Radiographic signs of MS include:
1. Straightening of the upper left heart border.
2. Posterior displacement of the esophagus by the enlarged LA.
3. Kerley B lines from interstitial edema and lymphatic distension.
• Severe MS with pulmonary hypertension may subsequently enlarge the pulmonary artery, right ventricle, and right atrium.
• The left ventricle typically remains normal in Isolated mitral stenosis.

Other options:
Option B: Right atrium enlargement occurs only later, secondary to pulmonary hypertension.
Option C: Left ventricle is usually unaffected in isolated MS.
Option D: The right ventricle enlarges only in advanced MS with pulmonary hypertension.

stem: Thrombosis of posterior inferior cerebellar artery causes.
subject: Medicine, Anatomy
explanation:
Solution
• Posterior inferior cerebellar artery (PICA) thrombosis results in lateral medullary syndrome (Wallenberg syndrome).

• Clinical features include Ipsilateral facial sensory loss, contralateral body pain/temperature loss, dysphagia, hoarseness, vertigo, nausea, vomiting, and Ipsilateral Horner's syndrome.
• PICA is a branch of the vertebral artery, supplying the lateral medulla and inferior cerebellum.
• Early recognition is important to prevent complications like aspiration due to dysphagia.
• Management focuses on antithrombotic therapy, supportive care, and rehabilitation.
• Medullary syndromes and Weber syndrome:
SyndromeVessel InvolvedClinical features
Medial Medullary syndrome/ Dejerine syndromeVertebral artery/Anterior spinal arteryC/L loss of conscious proprioception
C/L hemiplegia
I/L tongue paralysis
Lateral Medullary syndrome/ Wallenberg syndromeVertebral artery > Posterior inferior cerebellar arteryC/L loss of pain/temperature on the body
I/L loss of pain/temperature on face
Ataxia
Nystagmus
Vertigo
Homer's syndrome
Dysphagia
Ipsilateral bulbar paralysis
Loss of gag reflex
Weber's syndromePosterior cerebral arteryI/L Oculomotor nerve palsy-ptosis with fixed dilated pupil pointed down and out
C/L hemiplegia
C/L paralysis of the lower face

• C/L-Contralateral
• I/L-Ipsilateral

Other options:
Option B: Weber syndrome results from midbrain Infarction (cerebral peduncle), not PICA.
Option C: Medial medullary syndrome is caused by anterior spinal artery occlusion, affecting medial medulla.
Option D: Millard-Gubler syndrome is due to ventral pons lesion, leading to Ipsilateral facial palsy and contralateral hemiplegia.

stem: Which among the following is false?
options: Components of MELD (Model for end stage liver disease) scoring system are creatinine, bilirubin, International normalized ratio (INR), serum sodium (mEq/L)
Components of CTP(Child Turcotte Pugh) score are albumin, bilirubin, INR, ascites, encephalopathy
Components of CTP (Child Turcotte Pugh) are creatinine, bilirubin, International normalized ratio (INR)
MELD score is used to assess and prioritize patients awaiting liver transplantation
subject: Medicine
explanation:
Solution
• The Child-Turcotte-Pugh (CTP) score evaluates prognosis in chronic liver disease and cirrhosis using albumin, bilirubin, INR, ascites, and hepatic encephalopathy.

• MELD (Model for End-Stage Liver Disease) uses serum bilirubin, serum creatinine, INR, and sometimes serum sodium to assess liver disease severity and to prioritize patients awaiting liver transplantation.
• CTP is more qualitative, while MELD provides a numerical, objective score for organ allocation.
• Child-Turcotte-Pugh Score:
Variable1 Point2 Points3 Points
Bilirubin level<2 mg/dL2-3 mg/dL>3 mg/dL
Albumin level>3.5 g/dL2.8-3.5 g/dL<2.8 g/dL
Prothrombin time (Increased seconds)1-34-6≥6
International normalized ratio<1.71.7-2.3>2.3
EncephalopathyNoneControlledUncontrolled
AscitesNoneControlledUncontrolled

• Prognosis of the various CTP classes:
Class1-year survival rate %2-year survival rate %
A10085
B8060
C4535


Other options:
Option A: Correct MELD includes creatinine, bilirubin, INR, and serum sodium (for MELD-Na).
Option B: Correct: CTP uses albumin, bilirubin, INR, ascites, and encephalopathy.
Option D: Correct: MELD is specifically used to prioritize liver transplant candidates.

stem: Identify the false statement about the bundle of Kent:
options: It is faster than AV nodal pathway
It is slower than AV nodal pathway
subject: Medicine, Physiology
explanation:
Solution
• The bundle of Kent is an accessory conduction pathway between the atria and ventricles, seen in Wolff-Parkinson-White (WPW) syndrome.
• It conducts Impulses faster than the AV node, bypassing the normal AV nodal delay.
• This preexcites the ventricles, resulting in a short PR Interval on ECG.
• The QRS complex is prolonged because ventricular depolarization occurs partly through the accessory pathway and partly through the normal AV nodal pathway.
• The most common location is between the left atrium and the free wall of the left ventricle.
• Understanding this pathway is critical for diagnosis and management of WPW-related arrhythmias.
The given ECG shows delta waves marked with red arrows.


Other options:
Option A: True - it is faster than AV nodal conduction.
Option C: True - leads to a short PR Interval due to preexcitation.
Option D: True - causes prolonged QRS from fusion of normal and accessory conduction.

stem: Left ventriculography with contrast was performed in a post-MI patient and is shown below. Infarct involving which portion of the myocardium is most likely to cause this condition?
subject: Medicine, Radiology
explanation:
Solution
• The image is suggestive of a left ventricular aneurysm, a post-myocardial infarction complication.

• Anterior transmural Infarcts are the most common cause of LV aneurysm formation.
• Patients with anterior transmural Infarcts are at increased risk for:
1. Free-wall rupture
2. Expansion of the infarcted area
3. Mural thrombus formation
4. Ventricular aneurysm
• Posterior transmural Infarcts are more likely to cause:
1. Conduction blocks
2. Right ventricular Involvement
3. Or both
• Recognition of the infarct location helps predict complications and management strategies.

Other options:
Option A: Subendocardial infarcts rarely lead to aneurysm formation.
Option C: Posterior transmural infarcts usually cause conduction abnormalities, not aneurysms.
Option D: Inferior wall infarcts may involve the right ventricle but less commonly cause aneurysms.

stem: A patient presents with frequent urination, nocturia, and enuresis. 24-hour urine volume was measured and recorded to be 7 liters. The urine osmolarity was 260 mOsm/L. ADH assay was performed and the recorded values were reported as 0.8 pg/ml. An MRI of the brain was performed and T1 weighted Indicated the absence of the bright spot. What is the most likely diagnosis?
subject: Medicine
explanation:
Solution
• The patient has polyuria (>3 L/day) and low urine osmolarity (<280 mOsm/L).
• Plasma ADH is low (0.8 pg/ml), indicating central (pituitary) diabetes Insipidus (DI) rather than nephrogenic DI, where ADH is normal or elevated.
• MRI T1-weighted Imaging shows an absent posterior pituitary bright spot, which is a classic finding in central DI.
• Primary polydipsia is associated with low serum osmolality and usually normal/high ADH levels, and urine concentration Improves with fluid restriction.
• Mannitol Infusion may cause osmotic diuresis but does not lower ADH or show absent pituitary bright spots.
• Diagnosis is confirmed with water deprivation test and response to desmopressin.

• Diabetes Insipidus (DI):
TypesPrimary pathologyCaused by
Pituitary/Central DIPrimary deficiency of ADH secretionAVP-Neurophysin II gene (most common gene defect)
Primary polydipsiaSuppression of ADH secretion by excessive fluid intake1. Dispogenic DI: Following head trauma, TB meningitis, Multiple sclerosis.
2. Psychogenic DI: Due to psychosis or OCD
3. latrogenic polydipsia: Due to motivated health benefits
Gestational DIDeficiency due to increased degradation of ADHN terminal aminopeptidase produced by placenta
Nephrogenic DIRenal Insensitivity to ADH or drugs like lithium or genetic mutationdefect in gene encoding V2 receptor on chXq28 (most common gene defect)


Other options:
Option A: Nephrogenic DI shows high ADH levels and kidneys resistant to ADH.
Option B: Primary polydipsia presents with concentratable urine after fluid restriction.
Option D: Mannitol infusion causes osmotic diuresis, not central Di.

stem: Nasal polyps are commonly associated withe
subject: Medicine, ENT
explanation:
Solution
• Nasal polyps are commonly seen in Intrinsic (non-allergic) asthma.

• Intrinsic asthma is characterized by adult-onset asthma with negative skin prick tests for common allergens.
• Samter's triad describes the combination of:
1. Intrinsic asthma
2. Aspirin hypersensitivity
3. Nasal polyposis
• Asthma in this setting is usually severe, persistent, and difficult to control.
• Recognition of nasal polyps is important as it may Indicate aspirin-exacerbated respiratory disease (AERD).
• Extrinsic vs Intrinsic asthma:
ComponentExtrinsic asthmaIntrinsic asthma
Other namesAtopic/allergic asthmaNonatopic/non-allergic/idiosyncratic asthma
Associated withAtopy, drug or food allergy (past or family history)Samter's triad (asthma + aspirin sensitivity + sinus disease with recurrent nasal polyps)
OnsetChildhoodAdult
InflammationEosinophil mediatedNeutrophil mediated
IgE levels↑ IgE (type 1 hypersensitivity)Normal IgE (negative skin prick test)
Most common triggerDermatophagoides (House mite)Superantigen - S. aureus enterotoxin
Response to Inhaled corticosteroldsResponds wellDecreased short term response
PrognosisGoodBad


Other options:
Option B: Brittle asthma is characterized by severe, unstable asthma, not specifically associated with nasal polyps.
Option C: Extrinsic asthma is allergic asthma, generally not associated with nasal polyps.
Option D: Exercise-induced asthma triggers bronchoconstriction with physical activity but is not linked to nasal polyps.

stem: Which of the following is the most common type of Guillain-Barré syndrome?
subject: Medicine
explanation:
Solution
• Acute Inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy (AIDP) is the most common form of Guillain-Barré syndrome (GBS), especially in Western countries.

• GBS is an acute, Immune-mediated polyneuropathy that typically presents with ascending weakness and areflexla.
• Other recognized types include:
1. Acute motor axonal neuropathy (AMAN): Purely motor, axonal variant, more common in Asia and Central/South America.
2. Acute motor-sensory axonal neuropathy (AMSAN): Severe, Involves both motor and sensory fibers.
3. Miller Fisher syndrome (MFS): Characterized by ophthalmoplegia, ataxia, and areflexla, often associated with anti-GQ1b antibodies.
• Electrodiagnostic studies and nerve conduction studies help differentiate subtypes and guide prognosis.
• Antibodies seen in Gulllain Barre syndrome:
TypeAntibody
AIDPAnti GM1
AMANAnti GD1a
AMSANAnti GD1a
Miller Fisher syndromeAnti GQ1b

• AIDP = Acute inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy
• AMAN = Acute motor axonal neuropathy
• AMSAN = Acute motor sensory axonal neuropathy

Other options:
Option A: AMAN is less common and mostly seen in children or specific geographic regions.
Option C: AMSAN is a severe, axonal variant, less frequent than AIDP.
Option D: Miller Fisher syndrome is a rare, regional variant of GBS.

stem: All are true about Guillain-Barré syndrome except:
subject: Medicine
explanation:
Solution
• Guillain-Barré syndrome (GBS) typically presents with ascending paralysis, starting in the bilateral lower limbs and progressing to involve the trunk and upper limbs.

• It is an acute, autoimmune, Inflammatory, demyelinating polyneuropathy.
• Symptoms usually develop over hours to a few days and progression typically does not exceed 4 weeks.
• Autonomic dysfunction may occur; cranial nerves, particularly the facial nerve, can be involved causing bulbar weakness.
• Deep tendon reflexes are diminished or absent early in the disease.
• Plasmapheresis and Intravenous Immunoglobulin (IVIG) are established treatment options.
• Antibodies seen in Guillain Barre syndrome:
TypeAntibody
AIDPAnti GM1
AMANAnti GD1a
AMSANAnti GD1a
Miller Fisher syndromeAnti GQ1b

• AIDP: Acute inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy
• AMAN: Acute motor axonal neuropathy
• AMSAN: Acute motor sensory axonal neuropathy

Other options:
Option A: True - GBS is an Inflammatory condition.
Option C: True - Plasmapheresis is used to remove pathogenic antibodies.
Option D: True - It is a demyelinating disorder in most cases (AIDP variant).

stem: All are true about patients with trigeminal neuralgia except:
options: Pain along V2 and V3 division of trigeminal nerve
No objective signs of sensory loss
subject: Medicine, ENT
explanation:
Solution
• Trigeminal neuralgia is characterized by superficial, lancinating facial pain, not deep-seated pain.

• It is more common in females (>60% of cases) and typically occurs after 50 years of age.
