• The pain usually affects the second (V2) and third (V3) divisions of the trigeminal nerve.
• Pain is brief, severe, and paroxysmal, often triggered by touch, cold, wind, or eating.
• Physical examination usually shows no sensory deficits, although tic-like facial spasms (tic douloureux) may be observed.
• Carbamazepine is the drug of choice for medical management.

Other options:
Option A: True - more common in females.
Option B: True - involves V2 and V3 divisions most frequently.
Option D: True - there are no objective signs of sensory loss.

stem: A 47-year-old man with a diagnosis of acute myeloid leukemia with a blood type O negative blood group presents to the transplant clinic to discuss proceeding with an allogenic stem cell transplant. Which of the following would be an optimal donor?
options: His HLA Identical 50-year-old brother who is otherwise healthy and is blood type O+
An HLA Identical matched unrelated donor who is blood type O-
subject: Medicine
explanation:
Solution
• Allogeneic stem cell transplant is the treatment of choice for acute myelold leukemla (AML) in eligible patients.

• Optimal donors are typically HLA-Identical siblings, even if there is a minor ABO mismatch (O-patient, O+ donor), because ABO mismatch is usually manageable.
• Identical twin (Option A) would be an autologous transplant, not true allogeneic; thus, it lacks the graft-versus-leukemia effect.
• Umbilical cord blood (Option B) is an option when no matched sibling is available, but it is associated with slower engraftment and higher risk of complications.
• Matched unrelated donor (Option D) is considered when no HLA-Identical sibling exists; sibling donors are preferred due to better outcomes and lower graft-versus-host disease risk.

Other options:
Option A: Not ideal - lacks graft-versus-leukemia effect.
Option B: Slower engraftment and higher complication risk.
Option D: Acceptable if no sibling available, but higher risk than HLA-Identical sibling.

stem: The production of which of the following hormones is increased in obesity?
subject: Medicine, Biochemistry
explanation:
Solution
• Insulin levels are Increased in obesity due to Insulin resistance, particularly with Intra-abdominal fat accumulation.

• Elevated Insulin stimulates fatty acid and triglyceride synthesis and reduces lipolysis, contributing to fat accumulation.
• Thyroxine, growth hormone, and adiponectin are generally reduced In obesity as they promote lipolysis.
• Adiponectin is an anti-inflammatory adipokine that enhances Insulin sensitivity and fatty acid oxidation, but its levels decrease in obesity.
• Mechanisms contributing to insulin resistance in obesity include:
1. Insulin receptor downregulation
2. Increased free fatty acids Impairing Insulin action
3. Intracellular lipid accumulation
4. Adipocyte-derived cytokines (TNF-a, IL-6, RBP4, resistin) modifying insulin signaling

Other options:
Option B: Thyroxine levels are not elevated; may even be slightly reduced.
Option C: Growth hormone secretion is decreased in obesity.
Option D: Adiponectin levels are decreased, despite its insulin-sensitizing effects.

stem: Which of the following is not a part of the revised trauma score (RTS)?
subject: Surgery, FMT
explanation:
Solution
• Pulse rate is not included in the Revised Trauma Score (RTS).

• RTS is calculated using three parameters:
1. Glasgow Coma Scale (GCS)
2. Systolic blood pressure (SBP)
3. Respiratory rate (RR)
• The RTS ranges from 0 to 12, with higher scores indicating less severe Injury.
• Triage based on RTS:
1. 12 -> Delayed
2. 11 -> Urgent
3. 3-10 -> Immediate
4. <3 -> Deceased

Other options:
Option A: GCS is part of RTS.
Option B: Systolic blood pressure is part of RTS.
Option D: Respiratory rate is part of RTS.

stem: What is the diagnosis of this patient from the ECG shown below?
subject: Medicine
explanation:
Solution
• The ECG shows a heart rate of ~188 BPM, which is tachycardic.
• There are wide QRS complexes (≥0.12 s) with abnormal morphology.
• P waves are absent, indicating AV dissociation.
• These findings are characteristic of ventricular tachycardia (VT), a potentially life-threatening arrhythmia originating in the ventricles.
• Early recognition and treatment are crucial to prevent hemodynamic instability and sudden cardiac death.


• Ventricular fibrillation (Option B) would not show discrete QRS complexes, and hence is not the answer in this case.


• ECG patterns of various ventricular arrhythmias
• Ventricular tachycardia


• Polymorphic ventricular tachycardia associated with QTc prolongation-Torsades de pointes


• Ventricular flutter


• Ventricular fibrillation



Other options:
Option A: Not normal-rate and QRS morphology are abnormal.
Option B: Ventricular fibrillation shows chaotic, irregular, and non-discernible QRS complexes.
Option D: Misplaced leads can alter ECG morphology but do not explain tachycardia with wide QRS and absent P waves.

stem: A 65-year-old female patient is brought to the emergency department in a state of unconsciousness. Her BP is 70/50 mm of Hg. Her ECG is shown below. What is the next best step in the management of her condition?
subject: Medicine
explanation:
Solution
• The ECG shows a narrow QRS complex tachycardia (~188 BPM) suggestive of paroxysmal supraventricular tachycardia (PSVT).

• The patient is hemodynamically unstable (BP 70/50 mmHg and unconscious).
• Synchronized electrical cardioversion is the immediate treatment of choice in unstable tachyarrhythmias.
• It restores sinus rhythm by delivering a synchronized shock during the R wave to avoid inducing ventricular fibrillation.
• Pharmacologic or vagal maneuvers should not delay cardioversion in such cases.
• Shown below is the algorithm for the management of supraventricular tachycardia: Management of supraventricular tachycardia
• Synchronized cardioversion vs Defibrillation
• Synchronized cardioversion:
1. Used in sustained VT.
2. Used in SVT (unstable cases), atrial flutter/fibrillation.
3. The shock delivered in sync with R wave (in absolute refractory period).
• Defibrillation:
1. Used in VT and VF without a pulse.
2. The shock is delivered at any time during cardiac cycle.
• Supraventricular tachycardias:
TachycardiaECG features
Sinus tachycardiaNormal P wave, normal ECG features except for the rate.
Focal atrial tachycardiaA single type of abnormal p wave morphology is seen.
Junctional tachycardiaNo p waves. Heart rate is 100-200/min.
AV nodal reentry tachycardiaNo p waves. Pseudo R' waves may be seen. Heart rate is 150-250/min.
Atrioventricular reentry tachycardiaThe P wave always follows QRS. RP Interval < PR interval.
Atrial flutterSawtooth shaped flutter waves are seen.
Atrial fibrillationNo distinct p waves seen
Multifocal atrial tachycardiaMore than 3 different abnormal p wave morphologies are seen
Atrial flutter/tachycardia with variable blockECG features of heart block are seen

Other options:
Option A: It is used for stable PSVT; contraindicated in hypotension as it can worsen hemodynamic instability.
Option B: It is the first-line drug for stable PSVT; not indicated in unstable or unconscious patients.
Option C: It is a vagal maneuver effective in stable PSVT; contraindicated in unconscious or hypotensive patients.

stem: A lady presents with fever, oral ulcers, photosensitivity, and rashes on her face is shown below. What is the most likely diagnosis?
subject: Medicine, Dermatology
explanation:
Solution
• The Image shows a well-demarcated erythematous butterfly-shaped rash over the malar eminences with sparing of the nasolabial folds, which is characteristic of Systemic Lupus Erythematosus (SLE).
• Fever, oral ulcers, and photosensitivity are common systemic features of SLE.
• The malar rash worsens on sun exposure due to photosensitivity.
• SLE is a multisystem autoimmune disorder involving skin, joints, kidneys, CNS, and hematologic systems.
• Diagnosis is confirmed by ANA and anti-dsDNA antibodies.


• The following Images show the findings in-
Melasma

Rosacea

Butterfly-shaped malar rash in SLE

Acne

Nevus of Ota

Portwine stain

Infantile hemangioma.


• Antibodies in Systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE):
AntibodyPrevalence %Antigen RecognizedClinical utility
Antinuclear Antibody98Multiple nuclearBest screening test: repeated negative test makes SLE unlikely
Anti ds-DNA70DNA (double stranded)High titres are SLE specific & in some patients correlates with disease activity, nephritis & vasculitis
Anti-Sm25Protein complexed to 6 species of nuclear U1 RNASpecific for SLE: No definite correlations: most patients also have ANA. More common in black & asians than white
Anti-RNP40Protein complexed to U1 RNANot Specific for SLE; high titres associated with overlap features of several rheumatic syndromes including SLE. More common in blacks and whites
Anti-Ro(SS-A)30Protein complexed to hy RNA, Primarily 60KDA and 52KDANot Specific for SLE; associated with sicca syndrome, predisposes in subacute cutaneous lupus & to neonatal lupus with congenital heart block associated with decrease risk of nephritis
Anti-La (SS-B)1047kDa protein complexed to hY RNAUsually associated with anti-Ro; associated with decreased risk for nephritis
Antihistone20Histones associated with DNA (in nucleosome & chromatin)More frequent in drug induced lupus than in SLE
Antiphospholipid50Phospholipid, B2 glycoprotein I (B2GPI) cofactor, prothrombin3 tests available-ELISA for cardiolipin & B2GPI, Sensitive prothrombin time (DRVVT). Predisposes to clotting, fetal loss, thrombocytopenia
Antierythrocyte60Erythrocyte membraneMeasured as direct Coombs test, a small proportion develop overt hemolysis
Antiplatelet30Surface & altered cytoplasmic antigens on plateletsAssociated with thrombocytopenia but sensitivity & specificity are not good. This is not a useful clinical test
Antineuronal60Neuronal & lymphocyte surface antigensIn some series, a positive test in CSF correlates with active CNS lupus
Antiribosomal P20Protein in ribosomesIn some series, a positive test in serum correlates with depression or psychosis due to CNS lupus

Other options:
Option A: It presents with heliotrope rash and Gottron's papules, not a malar rash sparing the nasolabial folds.
Option C: It causes papulopustular lesions on the central face but does not spare the nasolabial folds.
Option D: It presents with hyperpigmented macules on sun-exposed areas, without erythema or systemic manifestations.

stem: All of the following are features of Crohn's disease except:
subject: Pathology, Surgery, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
• Lead pipe appearance is a radiologic finding seen in ulcerative colitis, not Crohn's disease.
• It results from the loss of haustral markings in the colon, giving a smooth, featureless appearance on a barium enema.
• Crohn's disease typically shows segmental (skip) lesions and cobblestone appearance.
• It involves transmural inflammation (full-thickness) and can affect any part of the GI tract from mouth to anus.
• Perianal fistulas and rectal sparing are common features of Crohn's disease.
• The image below shows the lead pipe appearance of the colon on barium enema.

• Inflammatory Bowel Disease (IBD):
FeatureCrohn's diseaseUlcerative colitis
Age of onset2nd-4th decades & 7th-9th decades2nd-4th decades & 7th-9th decades
Most common presentationIleocolitis with rectal sparingProctitis (rectum)
Protective factorsNoneSmoking, Appendicectomy
Risk factorsOCP, Smoking, Antibiotic during first year of lifea-Methyldopa, Antibiotic during first year of life
T cell involvedTH1TH2
Gross findings/endoscopic findingsTransmural involvement, Segmental involvement, Skip lesions, Cobblestone appearance, Creeping fat, Longitudinal ulcersMucosal and superficial submucosal involvement. Continuous involvement. Pseudopolyps, Backwash ileitis, Collar button ulcers
Earliest findingAphthous ulcerErythematous mucosa with a fine granular surface
Microscopic findingsDeep, knife-like ulcers, Granulomas, Pyloric metaplasiaSuperficial broad-based, Cryptitis and crypt abscess
ComplicationsFistulas, StricturesHemorrhage, Toxic megacolon
Recurrence after resectionCommonUncommon, curative after resection
Radiological findingsAphthous ulcer, Rose thorn appearanceMucosal granularity, Lead pipe appearance
Extraintestinal manifestationsEpiscleritis, uveitis, Migratory polyarthritis, Ankylosing spondylitis, sacroiliitis, Erythema nodosum, Pyoderma gangrenosum (MC in UC), Primary sclerosing cholangitis (MC in UC), Renal calculi (MC in CD)Same column (MC in UC) with specific features

Other options:
Option A, C, and D: True. Crohn's disease is characterized by transmural granulomatous inflammation. Rectal sparing is a typical feature, unlike ulcerative colitis where rectum is always involved. Perianal fistulas and abscesses are common complications due to transmural inflammation.

stem: A patient with rheumatoid arthritis has been treated with methotrexate and low-dose corticosteroids for the past 4 months. However, the disease is still progressing. What would be your recommendation for the further management of this patient?
options: Stop oral methotrexate and start parenteral methotrexate
subject: Medicine, Pharmacology
explanation:
Solution
• In rheumatoid arthritis (RA), if there is inadequate response to methotrexate after 3-6 months, the next step is to add sulfasalazine and hydroxychloroquine (triple DMARD therapy).

• This combination has shown excellent efficacy and safety in patients with persistent disease activity.
• Methotrexate remains the cornerstone of RA treatment and should be continued unless contraindicated.
• Biologic therapy (e.g., anti-TNF agents) is reserved for patients who fail to respond to combination DMARDs.
• Early aggressive management helps prevent joint damage and disability.

Other options:
Option A: Switching to parenteral methotrexate can improve absorption but is usually tried before adding additional DMARDs.
Option C: Continuing the same regimen despite worsening disease is inappropriate.
Option D: Anti-TNF agents are not first-line after methotrexate monotherapy failure; combination DMARDs are preferred before biologics.

stem: A patient's chest X-ray is shown below. Which among the following options describes the Image?
subject: Radiology, Pediatrics
explanation:
Solution
• The chest X-ray shows a "Coeur en sabot" or boot-shaped heart, which is characteristic of Tetralogy of Fallot (TOF).

• The cardiac apex is elevated due to right ventricular hypertrophy.
• The concavity of the pulmonary arterial segment gives the appearance of a boot.
• Pulmonary vascular markings are decreased due to reduced pulmonary blood flow.
• The aorta appears enlarged and overrides the interventricular septum.
• Important radiological signs in cardiology:
SignCondition
Flask shaped/Pear shaped/ Leather bottle shaped/Money-bag shaped heartPericardial effusion
Boot shaped heart/Coeur-en-sabotFallot's tetralogy
Gooseneck signEndocardial cushion defects
Box-shaped heartEbstein's anomaly
Jug handle appearancePrimary pulmonary artery hypertension
"3" like appearance of the aortaCoarctation of aorta
Egg-on-string appearanceTransposition of great vessels
Egg in cup appearance, Pericardial calcificationConstrictive pericarditis
Snowman's heart, Cottage loaf appearance, Figure of Eight appearanceTotal anomalous pulmonary venous circulation
Scimitar signPartial anomalous pulmonary venous return
Calcification in the heart wallCarcinoid syndrome
Calcification of ascending aortaAtherosclerosis, Syphilis
Hilar dance on fluoroscopyAtrial septal defect
Batwing appearancePulmonary edema
Pruned tree appearance of the pulmonary vasculaturePulmonary artery hypertension
Sitting duck sign of heartPersistent Truncus arteriosus

Other options:
Option A: It is seen in Transposition of the Great Arteries (TGA) due to a narrow mediastinum.
Option C: It is seen in partial anomalous pulmonary venous return, especially of the right lung.
Option D: It is seen in total anomalous pulmonary venous return (TAPVR) of the supracardiac type.

stem: The presence of serpiginous ulcers on esophageal endoscopy indicates,
subject: Medicine, Pathology
explanation:
Solution
• Cytomegalovirus (CMV) esophagitis typically presents with large, linear, or serpiginous ulcers in the distal esophagus.

• It is most commonly seen in immunocompromised individuals, such as those with HIV/AIDS or transplant recipients.
• The ulcers have well-demarcated borders with relatively normal intervening mucosa.
• Diagnosis is confirmed by biopsy, showing owl's eye intranuclear inclusions in endothelial and stromal cells. Treatment includes ganciclovir or valganciclovir.
• Endoscopic Findings in Esophagitis:
ConditionsEndoscopic findings
Infectious esophagitisDiffuse and involve the proximal esophagus.
HSV esophagitisVesicles and small punched-out lesions (volcano ulcers).
CMV esophagitisSerpiginous ulcers
Peptic esophagusSolitary and distal
Eosinophilic esophagitisWhite punctate exudates or Multiple esophageal rings linear furrows (feline esophagus)
Pill esophagusDeep and Singular at points of luminal narrowing with sparing of the distal esophagus.
Candida esophagitisWhite friable plaques, in advance form adherent gray-white pseudomembranes.


CMV esophagitis
Candida esophagitis

Eosinophilic esophagitis

Pill esophagitis

HSV esophagitis

Other options:
Option B: It is characterized by white, adherent plaques that can be scraped off, commonly in immunocompromised patients.
Option C: It shows multiple small, punched-out or vesicular ulcers, usually in the mid-esophagus.
Option D: It presents with diffuse mucosal erythema, friability, and ulceration following radiation therapy.

stem: All of the following are AIDS-defining Ilinesses except:
subject: Medicine, Microbiology
explanation:
Solution
• Oral candidiasis is common in HIV infection but not classified as an AIDS-defining illness.

• AIDS-defining illnesses are those that occur when CD4+ T-cell counts fall below 200 cells/µl or indicate severe immunosuppression.
• Candidiasis Involving the esophagus, trachea, bronchi, or lungs is considered AIDS-defining, but oral thrush alone is not.
• The diagnosis of AIDS can also be made if any AIDS-defining condition develops regardless of CD4 count.
• A case of HIV can be classified Into five stages as per current CDC guidelines (0, 1, 2, 3, and unknown):
StageCriteria
0A negative HIV test within 6 months of first diagnosis
1Based on CD4+ count
2Based on CD4+ count
3AIDS-defining opportunistic illness
UnknownUnavailable CD4+ counts

Other options:
Option A: It is an AIDS-defining illness caused by direct HIV infection of the brain.
Option B: It is an AIDS-defining malignancy strongly associated with HPV infection.
Option C: Tuberculosis of any site (pulmonary or extrapulmonary) is classified as an AIDS-defining condition.

stem: A 40-year-old male patient came to the ED with complaints of weakness, paresthesia, and breathing difficulty. Relevant Investigations were done. The ECG obtained is suggestive of:
subject: Medicine
explanation:
Solution
• Hypokalemia commonly presents with muscle weakness, fatigue, paresthesla, and respiratory difficulty due to impaired muscle excitability.
• ECG findings in hypokalemia include ST-segment depression, flattened or Inverted T waves, and prominent U waves.
• The U wave represents delayed repolarization of Purkinje fibers, which becomes more visible in low potassium states.
• Severe hypokalemia can cause paralysis, rhabdomyolysis, or respiratory fallure in extreme cases.
• Common causes include diuretic use, vomiting, diarrhea, or hyperaldosteronism.
• In the given ECG, leads V1-V6 exhibit T wave Inversion. U waves are well appreciated In leads V2-V5.


• Hypokalemia leads to dynamic ECG changes. Sequential ECG changes are seen in hyperkalemia (Tall T wave, Long PR interval, Wide QRS duration, Sine wave pattern) and hypercalcemia (Shortening of ST interval).







Other options:
Option B: It is characterized by tall, peaked T waves, widened QRS complexes, and possible sine-wave pattern in severe cases.
Option C: It shows prolonged QT interval due to delayed ventricular repolarization.
Option D: It shows a shortened QT interval due to faster repolarization.

stem: Identify the condition given in the image:
subject: Dermatology, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
• Acanthosis nigricans presents as hyperpigmented, velvety thickened plaques commonly Involving axillae, neck, groin, and flexural areas.

• It occurs due to epidermal hyperplasia and Increased melanin deposition in basal keratinocytes.
• The benign form is most often associated with obesity and Insulin resistance (e.g., type 2 diabetes).
• The malignant form presents with rapid onset, extensive lesions, and may be associated with gastric adenocarcinoma or other Internal malignancies.
• It may be accompanied by tripe palms or Leser-Trélat sign (sudden appearance of multiple seborrheic keratoses).
• Treatment: 1. Treat underlying disease. 2. Aesthetic treatment: keratolytics, topical vitamin D, melatonin. 3. Procedures: laser, dermabrasion, chemical peels.
• Paraneoplastic dermatoses:
Skin conditionMost commonly associated malignancy
Acanthosis nigricansGastric adenocarcinoma
Acanthosis palmartsBronchial carcinoma
Sign of Leser-TrélatGastrointestinal adenocarcinoma
Acquired-ichthyosisHodgkin disease
Pityriasis rotundaHepatocellular carcinoma
Paraneoplastic pigmentationSmall cell bronchial carcinoma
Acrokeratosis paraneoplasticaSquamous cell carcinoma of the upper respiratory or gastrointestinal tracts
Erythema gyratum-repensLung cancer
Necrolytic migratory erythemaGlucagon secreting pancreatic islet cell adenoma
DermatomyositisLung cancer in men, breast and gynaecological tumours in women, and colorectal cancers in both sexes
Scleroderma-like skin changesCarcinoid syndrome
Paraneoplastic pemphigusB-cell proliferations and thymoma or thymoma-like neoplasms specifically Non-Hodgkin's lymphoma (42%)
Dermatitis herpetiformisLymphoma
Porphyria cutanea-tarda and variegate porphyrlasHepatocellular carcinoma
Erythroderma and exfoliative dermatitisMycosis fungoldes or Sézary syndrome
Pyoderma gangrenosumHematological malignancy
Sweet syndromeHematological malignancy.
Migratory thrombo-phlebitis (Trousseau's syndrome)Carcinoma Pancreas

Other options:
Option A: It presents with hellotrope rash and Gottron's papules, not velvety plaques.


Option C: It presents as a heraid patch followed by multiple scaly lesions in a Christmas tree pattern.


Option D: It shows hyperpigmented macules over the face, particularly in sun-exposed areas.

stem: Which of the following drugs causes carcinoma bladder?
subject: Pharmacology, Pathology
explanation:
Solution
• Cyclophosphamide is an alkylating agent used in the treatment of various malignancies and autoimmune diseases.

• Its metabolite, acrolein, causes urothelial toxicity, leading to hemorrhagic cystitis and Increased risk of bladder carcinoma.
• The risk correlates with cumulative dose and treatment duration.
• Adequate hydration and co-administration of mesna can reduce urothelial Injury.
• Bladder carcinoma caused by cyclophosphamide typically presents with painless gross hematurla.
• Diagnosis: Done by cystourethroscopy (direct visualization and biopsy)
• Etiological Factors for Bladder Carcinoma:
Histological subtypeRisk factors
Transitional cell carcinoma (urothelial)Smoking. Exposure to aniline dyes, Cyclophosphamide. Pelvic irradiation
Squamous cell carcinomaSchistosomiasis (S. haematoblum), Chronic irritation (stone disease, bladder diverticula, chronic UTI), Smoking
AdenocarcinomaPersistent urachus or trigone of the bladder, Congenital bladder exstrophy

Other options:
Option B: It is associated with nephrotoxicity and ototoxicity, not bladder cancer.
Option C: It may cause neuropathy and myelosuppression, not urothelial malignancy.
Option D: It increases the risk of endometrial carcinoma, not bladder carcinoma.

stem: Which of the following statements is false regarding deep vein thrombosis (DVT)?
options: Most common clinical presentation is pain and tenderness in calf
Some cases may directly present as pulmonary thromboembolism
subject: Surgery, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
• DVT is mostly unilateral, not bilateral. Bilateral involvement occurs in only a minority of cases.

• Typical symptoms include calf pain, tenderness and swelling.
• Clinical assessment alone is unreliable; imaging (Doppler) is required.
• Some patients may present directly with pulmonary embolism due to silent DVT.
• Prophylaxis for DVT:
Mechanical prophylaxisMechanism
Early ambulationActivates calf pump mechanism
Pneumatic compression stockingPrevents stasis by compressing calves
Pharmacological prophylaxisMechanism
Low molecular weight heparin (LMWH-Enoxaparin), FondaparinuxPrevent thrombus by Inhibiting factor Xa
Apixaban, RivaroxabanOral factor Xa inhibitors
Unfractionated heparin (UFH)Prevents action of factors VIII-XII and thrombin/fibrin production; used in renal insufficiency
WarfarinInhibits hepatic production of factors II, VII, IX, X

Other options:
Option A, C, and D: True. Clinical findings alone cannot reliably diagnose DVT. Calf pain and tenderness are the most common presenting features. Pulmonary embolism can be the first manifestation of DVT.

stem: A truck driver presents with dilated, tortuous veins of 4 mm in the foot. What is the stage of clinical classification according to CEAP (Clinical-etiology-anatomy-pathophysiology for chronic venous disorders)?
subject: Surgery
explanation:
Solution
• Dilated, tortuous veins ≥4 mm are classified as varicose veins, corresponding to C2 in the CEAP clinical classification.

• CEAP Clinical Classification:
1. CO: No visible or palpable signs of venous disease
2. C1: Telangiectases or reticular veins
3. C2: Varicose veins
4. C3: Edema
5. C4-C6: Skin changes or ulcers due to chronic venous disease
• CEAP also includes etiological (E), anatomical (A), and pathophysiological (P) classifications.

Other options:
Option A: False - CO has no visible or palpable signs.
Option B: False - C1 refers to telangiectases or reticular veins.
Option D: False - C3 indicates edema without varicose veins.

stem: The most common site of branchial cyst is
options: Junction of upper and middle third of the sternomastoid muscle
Junction of middle and lower third of the sternomastoid muscle
Between the two heads of the sternomastoid muscle
subject: Surgery, ENT
explanation:
Solution
• The most common site of a branchial cyst is at the junction of the upper and middle third of the sternomastold muscle at its anterior border.



• They arise from remnants of the second branchial cleft.
• Present as a painless, smooth, fluctuant swelling in the lateral neck.
• Commonly diagnosed in late childhood or early adulthood.
• Surgical excision is the treatment of choice to prevent recurrent infections.

Other options:
Option B, C, and D: These are not the typical anatomical locations for a branchial cyst.

stem: Which nerve is involved in Frey's syndrome?
subject: Anatomy, ENT
explanation:
Solution
• Frey's syndrome involves the auriculotemporal nerve, a branch of V3 (mandibular division of trigeminal nerve)


• Most commonly develops after parotid surgery due to aberrant regeneration of parasympathetic fibers to sympathetic sweat glands.
• Clinical feature, gustatory sweating-sweating and flushing of the peri-auricular region on stimulation by smell or taste of food,
• Diagnosis: Starch-iodine test.
• Treatment: topical antiperspirants for mild cases: botulinum toxin injection or tympanic neurectomy for severe cases.
• Prevention: careful extracapsular dissection and closure of superficial musculoaponeurotic layer during parotidectomy,
• Innervation of the sculp
• PA
• (
• A
• Parasympathetic/secretomotor (general visceral efferent) innervation of glands.
ParameterLacrimal glandsSubmandibular gland/Sublingual glandParotid glands
NucleusLacrimatory nucleus (pons)Superior salivatory nucleus (pons)Inferior sallvatory nucleus (medulla)
Carried byNervus Intermedius, Facial nerve (VII)Nervus Intermedius, Facial nerve (VII)Glossopharyngeal (IX)
NerveGreater superficial petrosal nerveChorda tympaniLesser petrosal nerve
FormationBranches at Ist genu/Geniculate ganglionBranches in vertical segmentFormed over promontory from tympanic plexus
Exits middle ear throughFacial hiatusPetrotympanic fissure/ Canal of HuguierHiatus for lesser petrosal nerve
CourseTraverses middle cranial fossa floor, exits through foramen lacerum and joins deep petrosal nerve in foramen lacerumTravels on floor of middle cranial fossa & exits via foramen ovale
Further courseForms nerve of Pterygoid canal/ Vidian nerveEnters infratemporal fossa and joins lingual nerveEnters infratemporal fossa
GanglionPterygopalatine ganglionSubmandibular ganglionOtic ganglion
Supplied byLacrimal branch of Ophthalmic division (V1) of Trigeminal nerveAuriculotemporal branch of Mandibular nerve (V3)

Other options:
Option A: False-Facial nerve is motor, not primarily involved in gustatory sweating
Option B: False Oculomotor nerve has no role in Frey's syndrome.
Option D: False-Glossopharyngeal nerve is not implicated in perl-auricular gustatory sweating.

stem: Parathyroid gland post surgery is commonly transferred to:
subject: Surgery
explanation:
Solution
• After parathyroidectomy, the parathyroid tissue is commonly autotransplanted into the brachioradialis muscle of the non-dominant forearm.

• This allows easy access if re-exploration is needed for recurrent hyperparathyroidism.
• Indications for parathyroidectomy include symptomatic primary hyperparathyroidism and selected asymptomatic cases with high calcium, urinary calcium >400 mg/day, low bone mineral density (Z-score <-2.5), age <50 years, or failed medical therapy. Secondary hyperparathyroidism with severe disease is also an indication.
• Surgery Involves neck hyperextension, mobilization of thyroid, identification and resection of affected parathyrold, and transplantation into the brachioradialis.
• Types of transplants:
TypeDefinition
OrthotopicOrgan implanted in the same anatomic location in the recipient as it was in the donor. The diseased organ is removed. Eg: heart, lung, liver, Intestine.
HeterotopicOrgan Implanted in a different anatomic location. The diseased organ is left in place. Eg: Kidney, pancreas.
Auxiliary transplantationPartial tissue from the donor is used which acts as temporary support for the recipient's injured organ, which remains in place. Eg: left lobe of liver.
AutotransplantTransfer of cells/tissues/organs within the same person.
AllotransplantTransfer of cells/tissues/organs within the same species.
XenotransplantTransfer of cells/tissues/organs in a different species.
IsograftTransfer of organ or tissue within genetically identical individuals. Eg: Identical twins.

Other options:
Option A: False Triceps is not used for parathyroid autotransplant.
Option B: False - Biceps is not the preferred site.
Option C: False Sartorius may be used for heterotopic autotransplant but less common.

stem: What is the drug used for estrogen-dependent breast cancer?
subject: Pharmacology, Surgery
explanation:
Solution
• Tamoxifen is a selective estrogen receptor modulator (SERM) used in estrogen receptor-positive (ER+) breast cancer.

• It acts as an antagonist In breast tissue and agonist in bone, uterus, Ilver and pituitary.
• Used as primary therapy for ER+ breast cancer in premenopausal women and as adjuvant therapy after breast-conserving surgery to prevent recurrence.
• Also effective in breast cancer chemoprevention in high-risk women, reducing tumor incidence by 40-50%.
• Common adverse effects include hot flashes, endometrial cancer, thromboembolic events and fatty liver.
• Hormonal therapy in breast carcinoma given for ER/PR-positive cases role: 1. Reduce risk of recurrence 2. Reduce risk of contralateral breast cancer 3. Reduce risk of progression of in situ to invasive carcinoma
Selective estrogen- receptor modulatorAromatase InhibitorsOvarian suppression/ ablation
TamoxifenAnastrazoleOophorectomy
RaloxifeneLetrozoleGnRH analogues
ArzoxifenExemestane
Fenoxifen

Other options:
Option A: Estrogen promotes growth of ER+ breast cancer and is not used as treatment.
Option B: Clomiphene citrate is used for ovulation induction, not breast cancer.
Option D: Adriamycin (doxorubicin) is a chemotherapeutic agent but not estrogen-specific.

stem: Dohiman's procedure is for:
subject: Surgery, ENT
explanation:
Solution
• Dohlman's procedure is a transoral endoscopic approach for Zenker's diverticulum.



• It involves ablation of the septum between the diverticulum and cervical esophagus. Typically used for diverticula measuring 2-5 cm.
• Minimally invasive and avoids external neck incisions. Reduces risk of postoperative complications such as infection and fistula formation.
• Zenker's diverticulum occurs due to herniation of mucosa and submucosa through the Killian's triangle, which is a weak area between two parts of the inferior constrictor (thyropharyngeus and cricopharyngeus) in the posterior pharyngeal wall.
• It is usually seen in elderly patients (>60 years). It is treated with: 1. Cricopharyngeal myotomy 2. Surgical diverticulectomy 3. Marsupialization 4. Dohlman's procedure

Other options:
Option A: Meckel's diverticulum is treated by surgical excision (diverticulectomy).
Option C: Bochadlek hernia (Bochdalek hernia) is a congenital diaphragmatic hernia, repaired surgically.
Option D: Menetrier's disease is a hypertrophic gastropathy managed medically or by gastrectomy in severe cases.

stem: Gall stone chemistry most commonly seen amongst the following is.
subject: Surgery, Pathology
explanation:
Solution
• The most common composition of gallstones is crystalline cholesterol monohydrate.

• These stones are typically yellow-green in color and radiolucent on plain X-ray.
FeatureCholesterol gallstonesBlack pigmentBrown pigment
DemographyWestern countriesNon-western countriesNon-western countries
LocationGallbladder, ductsGallbladder, ductsDucts
Risk factorsAdvancing age, Female, Oral contraceptives and pregnancy, Obesity and insulin resistance, Rapid weight reduction, Gallbladder stasis, Dyslipidemia syndromesChronic hemolysis, Ileal resection, Cystic fibrosis with pancreatic insufficiencyChronic biliary Infection.
AppearancePale yellow, round to ovoid, finely granular, hard external surface, Transection: glistening radiating crystalline palisade>1.5 cm in diameter, friable, speculated and moldedLarge, brown, laminated and soft
ConstituentsCholesterol, phospholipids, bile saltsCalcium bilirubinate, Calcium bicarbonate, Calcium phosphateCalcium bilirubinate, Palmitate, Cholesterol
ConsistencyCrystalline with nucleusHardSoft, friable
% of radio-opaque stones15%60%0%

Other options:
Option B: Crystalline cholesterol dihydrate is rare.
Option C: Amorphous cholesterol dihydrate stones are uncommon.
Option D: Amorphous cholesterol monohydrate stones are infrequent.

stem: A gasless abdomen is seen in:
subject: Surgery, Radiology
explanation:
Solution
• Acute pancreatitis can present with a gasless abdomen on X-ray due to adynamic ileus or suppression of bowel motility.

• Vomiting and peritoneal irritation contribute to reduced intraluminal gas.
• It may be associated with a sentinel loop sign-a localized dilated loop of small bowel near the pancreas.
• Other radiographic features may include colon cutoff sign or calcifications in chronic pancreatitis.
• Common causes of gasless abdomen include: 1. High obstruction: gastric outlet obstruction, congenital atresia 2. Small bowel obstruction 3. Bowel ischemia 4. Ascites 5. Acute pancreatitis 6. Gastroenteritis 7. Large abdominal mass due to displacement 8. Post-surgery following total colectomy, esophagogastrectomy, gastrectomy, and low anterior resection. 9. A paucity of small bowel gas may be a normal finding in some adults.

Other options:
Option A: Ulcerative colitis usually shows mucosal Irregularity and dilated colon rather than gasless abdomen.
Option B: Intussusception shows target sign or bowel obstruction, a mass, and a paucity of gas in the right iliac fossa on imaging.
Option D: Crohn's disease shows skip lesions, strictures, and fistulas rather than gasless abdomen.

stem: The commonest surgical approach to a retrosternal goiter is via:
subject: Surgery
explanation:
Solution
• The majority of retrosternal golters can be removed through a cervical (neck) approach.

• Secondary retrosternal goiters originate in the neck and descend into the mediastinum, making neck access feasible.
• Primary retrosternal goiters are rare and may require sternotomy. Goiters are termed retrosternal if ≥50% of thyroid tissue lies intrathoracically.
• Primary vs Secondary retrosternal goiter:
FeaturePrimary retrosternal goiterSecondary retrosternal goiter
Occurrence1%Majority of retrosternal goiters
OriginArise from ectopic thyroid tissue in the chest. No connection with the thyroid tissue in the neck.Arise from the downward extension of cervical thyroid tissue along the fascial planes of the neck.
Blood supplyIntrathoracic blood vesselsSuperior and inferior thyroid arteries

Other options:
Option B: Chest approach is rarely required, mainly for primary intrathoracic goiters.
Option C: Median sternotomy is reserved for large primary retrosternal golters or those inaccessible via the neck.
Option D: Incorrect a neck approach is usually sufficient.

stem: An Intern placed an intercostal drainage tube in a patient. Which of the following steps is incorrect?
options: Identified 5th intercostal space anterior to mid-axiliary line to place the tube
Inserted tube along the lower border of upper rib
Directed tube posteriorly to prevent injury
subject: Surgery
explanation:
Solution
• The ICD should always be inserted along the upper border of the lower rib, because the intercostal neurovascular bundle runs along the lower border of the upper rib-hence inserting along the lower border of the upper rib risks injury.



• The recommended site for tube insertion is the 5th Intercostal space, anterior to the mid-axillary line, inside the triangle of safety.
• Digital exploration confirms proper pleural entry and prevents damage to lung or surrounding structures.
• Contraindications include pulmonary adhesions, pulmonary trauma, coagulopathy and diaphragmatic hernia.
• Complications of this procedure: 1. Infection at the site of insertion 2. Unintentional injury to the lung-hemothorax 3. Intercostal nerve/vessel injury 4. Subcutaneous hematoma/emphysema

Other options:
Option A: Identifying the 5th intercostal space anterior to the mid-axillary line is the correct and standard location for chest tube placement.
Option C: Directing the tube posteriorly is appropriate for fluid drainage and does not cause injury.
Option D: Performing an incision with digital exploration is a required safety step before placing the tube.

stem: In a blast injury, which of the following organs is least vulnerable to the blast wave?
subject: Surgery, FMT
explanation:
Solution
• Primary blast injury mainly affects air-filled organs due to rapid pressure changes.

• The liver, being a solid organ, is the least vulnerable due to resistance to overpressure injury.
• The ear drum is the most sensitive structure and ruptures at the lowest overpressure.
• Lungs are highly vulnerable and commonly sustain life-threatening barotrauma. The Gl tract is also affected because it contains trapped gas.
• Classification of blast injuries:
ClassificationExplanationInjury typeExamples
Primary blastDue to air pressure from the blastShockwave (overpressure)Tympanic membrane injury, blast lung, intestinal blast injury
Secondary blastFragments from the blast, following a detonationPenetrating/fragmentationAll penetrating injuries
Tertiary blastGross movements of persons, objects, or infrastructure by the blast windBlunt traumaBlunt and crush injuries, Traumatic amputation
Quaternary blastMiscellaneous causesExposure to explosive products or toxic substancesBurns, inhalation injury
Quinary blastThe intentional addition of biological or radioactive material to the explosive deviceEnvironmental contaminantsRadiation sickness, infection

Other options:
Option A: The Gl tract is susceptible because hollow organs are easily damaged by blast waves.
Option B: The lungs are highly vulnerable and frequently injured in explosions.
Option D: The ear drum is the most sensitive to blast pressure.

stem: What is shown in the given image?
subject: Surgery, Pathology
explanation:
Solution
• The image shows a dark-colored punctum (summit), which is characteristic of a sebaceous cyst.

• Sebaceous cysts are retention cysts of sebaceous glands, commonly found on the face, scalp, and postauricular region.
• They contain yellowish-white, cheesy material composed of keratin and lipid debris.
• The postauricular sulcus is a common location for sebaceous cysts near the ear.
• The cyst is typically painless, soft, mobile, and fluctuant (Paget's test positive).
• A punctum is present in -70% of cases, representing the blocked sebaceous gland duct.
• Common sites of ear tumors:
TumorMost common site
Sebaceous cystPost auricular sulcus
Dermoid cystPost auricular area
KeloidPinna around the piercing site
Cutaneous hornHelix
Acoustic neuromaVestibular Nerve
Squamous cell CarcinomaHelix
Basal cell CarcinomaHelix & tragus
Malignant MelanomaAnywhere

Other options:
Option A: Dermoid cysts are congenital lesions containing ectodermal elements and usually lack a punctum.
Option C: Meningioma is an intracranial tumor and does not present as a superficial skin swelling.
Option D: Post-auricular fistula presents as a small external opening, not a cyst with a punctum.

stem: A man presents with a slowly progressive swelling over the past 1 and 1/2 years. On examination, It has a variable consistency and appears as shown in the image. What is the probable cause?
subject: Surgery, ENT
explanation:
Solution
• A slowly progressive, painless swelling over years with variable consistency is typical of pleomorphic adenoma, the most common benign tumor of the parotid gland.

• The swelling classically lifts the earlobe, a hallmark feature of parotid tumors.
• Pleomorphic adenoma arises from the superficial lobe of the parotid and shows mixed epithellal and mesenchymal components.
• The mass is lobulated, firm, and does not cause facial nerve palsy.
• Curtain sign positive the swelling cannot be moved above the zygomatic arch because it is deep to the parotid fascia.
• FNAC is the investigation of choice; open blopsy is avolded to prevent facial nerve injury.
• Salivary gland tumors and their frequency:
Type of Salivary glandLocationFrequency of tumorPercentage of malignancy
MajorParotidCommon10-20%
MajorSubmandibularUncommon50%
MajorSublingualVery rare85%
MinorUpper aero-digestive tractRare90%

Other options:
Option A: Adamantinoma is a rare bone tumor of the mandible or tibia, not a parotid swelling.
Option B: Dermold cysts are congenital, mobile cystic lesions with doughy consistency and do not lift the earlobe.
Option D: Sebaceous cysts show a punctum, are superficial, and do not cause deep parotid swelling or earlobe elevation.

stem: Which of the following statements is false regarding rectal carcinoma?
options: Hartmann's operation is done in elderly debilitated patients
Adenocarcinoma is a rare variant of rectal carcinoma
Early morning spurious diarrhoea and tenesmus can occur
Growth confined to the rectal mucosa is stage A of modified Duke's staging
subject: Surgery, Pathology
explanation:
Solution
• Adenocarcinoma is the most common type of rectal carcinoma.
• It arises from glandular epithelium of the colorectal mucosa The vast majority of rectal cancers are adenocarcinomas, making it far from rare.
• Other variants like mucinous carcinoma and signet-ring cell carcinoma are less common.
• Epidemiological data consistently indicate adenocarcinoma forms the dominant histological subtype of rectal malignancies.
• Symptoms of rectal carcinoma Include: 1. Painless bleeding - earliest and most common 2. Alteration of bowel habits-change in frequency and consistency of the stool. 3. Tenesmus unable to pass a motion even when there is the sensation of needing to evacuate the bowel. It is suggestive of a tumor of the lower half of the rectum. 4. Early morning bloody diarrhea passage of blood and mucus in addition to feces.
• Pain and weight loss are usually late symptoms.
• Modified Duke's classification:
DUKE'S STAGEEXTENT OF TUMORS
AGrowth limited up to the rectal mucosa
B1Tumor invading muscularis propria, no lymph node invasion.
B2Tumors invading into or through serosa, no lymph node Invasion.
C1The tumour did not exceed the bowel wall, lymph node metastasis.
C2Tumour exceeded the intestinal wall and lymph node metastasis.
DDistant metastasis


Other options:
Option A: Hartmann's operation is typically performed in elderly or debilitated patients when primary anastomosis is risky.
Option C: Tenesmus and early-morning spurious diarrhoea are characteristic symptoms of lower rectal tumors.
Option D: Tumor confined to the mucosa corresponds to Modified Duke's Stage A.

stem: What is the size of the given blade used for Incision and drainage of a superficial abscess?
subject: Surgery
explanation:
Solution
• The No. 11 blade is a triangular, pointed, stab-type blade.

• It is specifically designed for precise puncture and stab Incisions.
• Commonly used for Incision and drainage (I&D) of superficial abscesses. The sharp tip allows controlled entry into tense, fluctuant swellings.
• Also used for procedures like arterlotomy and placement of surgical drains.

Other options:
Option A: Blade No. 10 is curved and used mainly for large skin Incisions, not I&D.
Option C: Blade No. 22 is larger and used for long, deeper incisions, e.g., laparotomy.
Option D: Blade No. 15 is small and curved, used for fine, precise dissection, not abscess drainage.

stem: You notice the following appearance in the X-Ray of a patient. Which of the following conditions is the least likely cause for this?
subject: Radiology, Surgery
explanation:
Solution
• The X-ray shows pneumoperitoneum, typically seen as free air under the diaphragm.

• Acute Intestinal obstruction does not cause pneumoperitoneum unless there is secondary perforation.
• Simple obstruction shows dilated bowel loops and air-fluid levels, not free air.
• Common causes of free air include hollow viscus perforation and postoperative residual air.
• Chilalditi syndrome can mimic free air due to interposed bowel loops.
• Causes other than perforation are: 1. Chilaiditi syndrome (where the intestine is seen between the liver and diaphragm giving a false impression of free air) 2. Sub-phrenic abscess 3. Postoperatively air may be present in the abdomen for up to 7 days.

Other options:
Option A: Duodenal ulcer perforation commonly produces pneumoperitoneum.
Option B: After laparoscopy, residual CO₂ may remain under the diaphragm for several days.
Option C: Chilaiditi syndrome mimics pneumoperitoneum due to bowel interposition.

stem: The type of knot seen in the image is:
subject: Surgery
explanation:
Solution
• A reef knot is characterized by both free ends exiting the knot on the same side, without any cross-over.
• It is formed by two opposite half-hitches, producing a flat, square configuration.
• Reef knots are commonly used for ligating vessels and securing tissues. This knot lies flat and secure when properly squared.
• It is one of the most basic and essential surgical ligature knots.
• Types of knots: 1. Granny knot: half-hitch over a half-hitch in the same direction. It has better-holding power than half-hitch 2. Reef knot: half-hitch followed by a second half-hitch after squaring. 3. Triple throw knot: Reef knot with another half-hitch knot for added security. Knots made with prolene sutures require 6-8 throws to prevent slippage. 4. Surgeon's knot: Modification of the reef knot with two half-hitches in the first layer. 5. Slip knot: Modification of either granny/reef knot formed when one end of the thread is kept taut while tightening the knot.


Other options:
Option A: A half hitch knot has a single throw and does not resemble the flat configuration of a reef knot
Option C: A surgeon's knot includes a double first throw to increase friction.
Option D: A crossed half hitch does not produce the flat, symmetrical appearance of a reef knot.

stem: A female patient presents with a breast abscess. An aspiration was attempted under antibiotic cover which failed to reveal any aspirate. What is the next step in management?
options: Stop antibiotics as no aspirate was collected
subject: Surgery
explanation:
Solution
• Ultrasound-guided aspiration is the preferred first-line management for breast abscess, especially in lactating women.


• If the first aspiration ylelds no pus, guidelines recommend a repeat aspiration, as abscess loculation or incorrect needle placement may be the cause.
• Incision and drainage is avoided initially because it may damage milk ducts and increase the risk of a milk fistula, which Impairs breastfeeding.
• Multiple aspirations often successfully treat the abscess while preserving breast tissue Integrity.
• Aspiration also results in faster healing, less scarring, and continued breastfeeding compared with surgical drainage.
• An ultrasonogram of the affected breast is performed to confirm the abscess, and treatment proceeds as follows:
• 1. Analgesics, antipyretics, and breast support for pain and fever relief
• 2. Aspiration is performed under antibiotic cover, and the pus is cultured. If aspiration proves unsuccessful, it should be reattempted after a few days.
• 3. Incision and drainage should be considered in cases of:
• 1. Mastitis unresponsive to antibiotics for 48 hours
• 2. Brawny Induration
• 3. Progressive mastitis with persistent fever
• Difference between Bacterial mastitis and Breast abscess:
ConditionBacterial mastitisBreast abscess
SymptomsLocalized inflammation due to ascending infection or infection due to milk stasis. Dull painOccurs as a complication of mastitis Pus formation. Throbbing pain
Signs (If any)Tenderness ErythemaInduration of skin (if chronic) Lymphadenopathy Fluctuation test +ve Pus point may be present
ManagementAnalgesics Antibiotics (Dicloxacillin 500 mg every 6 hours orally, Erythromycin if allergic) Adequate breast supportAnalgesics Adequate breast support Aspiration of pus under antibiotic cover; repeated aspirations if required Incision & drainage

Other options:
Option A: Incision and drainage is reserved only if repeated aspirations fall or for large/complicated abscesses.
Option B: Continuing antibiotics alone is insufficient because antibiotics cannot penetrate the abscess cavity.
Option D: Antibiotics should not be stopped; they are continued along with aspiration.

stem: De-gloving Injury refers to ?
options: Skin and subcutaneous fat are stripped from the underlying fascia
Skin, subcutaneous fat and fascia are stripped from tendons
Skin, subcutaneous fat, fascia and tendons are stripped from bone
subject: Surgery
explanation:
Solution
• A de-gloving injury involves avulsion of skin and subcutaneous fat from the underlying fascia, typically due to shearing forces.

• It may be open (e.g., finger avulsion) or closed (e.g., road traffic accidents causing shearing).
• The Injury compromises perforating vessels, making large areas of skin non-vlable.
• Closed de-gloving often leads to a Morel-Lavallée lesion, a haemolymphatic collection between fascial planes.
• Degloving injuries can be limited, non-circumferential, or circumferential (single or multiplanar).

Other options:
Option B: This represents a much deeper injury extending beyond classical de-gloving, Involving fascia being stripped from tendons.
Option C: This suggests extensive avulsion up to bone, which is not the standard definition of de-gloving.
Option D: De-gloving always involves both skin and subcutaneous fat, not skin alone.

stem: An 18-year-old boy presents to the orthopedician with complaints of painful swelling below the knee joint. An X-ray was obtained which is given below. Which of the following is the preferred treatment modality for this patient?
subject: Orthopaedics, Surgery
explanation:
Solution
• The X-ray shows an expansile, eccentric, multiloculated lytic lesion at the end of a long bone with a soap-bubble appearance, suggestive of a glant cell tumor (GCT).


• GCT is a benign but locally aggressive tumor commonly Involving the metaphysis and/or epiphysis of long bones in young adults (20-45 years), rarely in teenagers.
• The preferred treatment is extended curettage with bone grafting to remove the tumor while preserving joint function.
• Important X-ray features of GCT:
• 1. Eccentric, solitary lytic lesion
• 2. Involves epiphysis and metaphysis
• 3. Expansile, may thin overlying cortex
• 4. Soap-bubble appearance due to trabeculae within the lesion
• 5. No or minimal reactive sclerosis
• 6. No calcification
• An ABC Is treated by extended curettage and grafting with a bone graft substitute.
• Classical Radiological Appearance of Bone Tumors:
RADIOLOGICAL APPEARANCETUMORS
Sunray appearance/Codman's triangleOsteosarcoma (but can be seen in any malignant lesion)
Onion peel appearanceEwing's sarcoma (but can be seen in any malignant lesion or chronic osteomyelitis)
Soap bubble appearanceGiant cell tumor, adamantinoma
Ground glass appearance/Shepherd's crook deformityFibrous dysplasia
Patchy calcificationChondrogenic tumors (chondrosarcoma and chondroblastoma)
Popcorn, punctate or comma-shaped calcificationChondrosarcoma.
Stippled or Punctate calcificationChondroblastoma
Homogeneous calcificationOsteogenic tumors (osteosarcoma)
Fallen fragment/Trap door signSimple bone cyst
Double-density fluid levels and intra-lesional septationAneurysmal bone cyst
Winking owl signVertebral metastasis
Poika dot sign/Corduroy/Jailhouse signVertebral hemangioma
Moth eaten boneMultiple myeloma

Other options:
Option B: Simple curettage is less effective and has higher recurrence than extended curettage.
Option C: Radiofrequency ablation is mainly used for small bone lesions, not for epiphyseal GCT.
Option D: Radical excision is unnecessary for most GCTs and risks joint function.

stem: A 12-year-old boy presents with swelling and pain for 6 months over the upper tibia. His radiographic image is shown below. The most likely diagnosis is:
subject: Orthopaedics, Radiology
explanation:
Solution
• The radiograph shows a lytic lesion with a dense sclerotic rim and minimal periosteal reaction, suggestive of Brodle's abscess.

• Brodie's abscess is a localized form of subacute osteomyelitis caused by low-virulence organisms in an Immunocompetent host.
• Most commonly involves the metaphysis of the proximal tibia or distal femur.
• Clinical features:
• 1. Prolonged pain near a large joint (weeks to months)
• 2. Swelling and localized tenderness
• 3. Limp and possible muscle wasting
• Radiographic features:
• 1. Circumscribed, round/oval radiolucent cavity (1-2 cm)
• 2. Sclerotic halo surrounding the lesion
• 3. Minimal or no periosteal reaction
• Diagnosis can be supported by radioisotope scan (Increased activity) or MRI, open blopsy with curettage is indicated if diagnosis is uncertain.
The following X-ray and MRI images show Brodle's abscess of the radius in a patient.




• The differentiating features of each of the given conditions are as follows:
ConditionAge groupRadiographic findings
Brodie's abscessChildren, adolescentsLytic lesion with dense sclerotic rim. minimal periosteal reaction
OsteosarcomaBimodal: Children and adolescents, 70-80 yearsPeriosteal reaction leading to sunray appearance and Codman triangle
Osteoclastoma20-40 years (unlikely in children)Lytic lesion with thin sclerotic rim, minimal periosteal reaction
Ewing's sarcoma10-20 yearsDiaphyseal mass and periosteal reaction with onion-peel appearance.

Other options:
Option A: Osteosarcoma usually shows a sunburst appearance, aggressive periosteal reaction, and metaphyseal destruction; more common in older adolescents.
Option B: Osteoclastoma (GCT) typically occurs in epiphysis of long bones in young adults, not children.
Option D: Ewing's sarcoma shows onlon-skin periosteal reaction and occurs in diaphysis of long bones, mainly in adolescents.

stem: Which of the following fractures is most prone to non-union?
subject: Orthopaedics
explanation:
Solution
• Proximal scaphold fractures are most prone to non-union due to their limited blood supply. The more proximal the fracture, the higher the risk of vascular compromise and non-union

• The nutrient artery enters near the distal end, so proximal fractures are at risk of avascular necrosis and delayed or non-union.
• Bones with watershed or limited blood supply are particularly prone to non-union or osteonecrosis: 1. Head of femur: after neck fracture or posterior hip dislocation 2. Proximal scaphold: fracture through the waist 3. Lunate: following dislocation 4. Body of talus: after neck fracture The more proximal the fracture, the higher the risk of vascular compromise and non-union.

Other options:
Option B: Inter-trochanteric fractures typically heal well due to good cancellous blood supply.
Option C: Distal radius fractures generally have excellent healing potential.
Option D: Olecranon fractures rarely result in non-union if properly immobilized or surgically fixed.

stem: Painful arc syndrome is seen in all except:
subject: Orthopaedics
explanation:
Solution
• Complete tear of supraspinatus tendon does not produce painful arc because the tendon is fully torn and cannot be Impinged.

• Painful arc syndrome occurs during mid-range shoulder abduction (60-120°).
Clinical tests- Painful arc test, Hawkin- Kennedy test, Neer's sign, Jobe's test




• Important tests and signs to assess the function of rotator cuff muscles:
TestsMuscles tested
Empty can testSupraspinatus
Resisted external rotationinfraspinatus
Lift-off testSubscapularis
Belly press testSubscapularis
Abduction paradoxSeen in the complete rupture of rotator cuff.
Drop arm signSeen in the complete rupture of rotator cuff
Lag signSeen in tear of supraspinatus or infraspinatus.
Drop signSeen in the tear in the infraspinatus and the posterior part of the rotator cuff.

Other options:
Option B: Fracture of greater tuberosity can cause painful arc due to impingement.
Option C: Subacromial bursitis produces mid-range abduction pain.
Option D: Supraspinatus tendinitis is a common cause of painful arc.

stem: Which of the following does not lead to ARDS (Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome) at the blood-lung interface?
options: Fat embolism syndrome following femur shaft fracture
subject: Medicine
explanation:
Solution
• Pneumonia causes ARDS at the air-lung Interface, not the blood-lung interface.
• ARDS is characterized by rapid-onset severe dyspnea, hypoxemla, and diffuse pulmonary infiltrates on chest X-ray.
• Blood-lung Interface causes include sepsis, transfusion-related lung injury, pancreatitis, fat embolism, and thromboembolism.
• Air-lung Interface causes include aspiration, toxic gas/smoke Inhalation, near-drowning, pneumonia, and pulmonary contusion.
• Differentiating the interface helps in understanding pathophysiology and targeted management.
The following X-ray shows bilateral alveolar infiltrates as seen in ARDS


Other options:
Option B: Fat embolism can trigger ARDS at the blood-lung interface.
Option C: Sepsis is a common cause of blood-lung Interface ARDS.
Option D: Transfusion-related lung injury (TRALI) also occurs at the blood-lung interface.

stem: A 60-year-old woman presents with low backache and on a lumbar CT the following is seen. What is your diagnosis?
subject: Orthopaedics, Radiology
explanation:
Solution
• The biconcave shape of vertebral bodies and biconvex disc appearance (codfish vertebrae) is characteristic of osteoporosis.

• Osteoporosis is a metabolic bone disease with decreased bone mass and density, often seen in postmenopausal women, Cushing's disease, or long-term steroid therapy.
• It commonly affects the dorso-lumbar spine, leading to vertebral compression fractures and kyphosis.
• Other radiological signs include loss of vertebral helght, ground glass appearance of bones, picture frame vertebrae, ghost vertebrae, and trabecular loss in proximal femur.
• DEXA scan is the gold standard for diagnosis: a T-score-2.5 confirms osteoporosis.
The X-ray glven below is of a post-menopausal female with Colle's fracture after trivlal trauma, caused secondary to osteoporosis.

A lateral X-ray of the spine shows anterlor displacement of one vertebra over another. The oblique view In severe spondylolisthesis shows the "beheaded Scottish terrier sign' (due to a break In pars Interarticularis).




Other options:
Option A: Spondylolisthesis shows anterior slippage of one vertebra over another, not codfish appearance.
Option B: Discitis is characterized by endplate destruction and disc space narrowing, not diffuse biconcave vertebrae.
Option D: Pott's spine (tubercular spondylitis) shows vertebral body destruction, paravertebral abscess, and gibbus deformity, not codfish vertebrae

stem: Rheumatoid arthritis management in a patient with deformity is:
subject: Pharmacology, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
• Methotrexate is a DMARD that slows structural progression of RA and is first-line therapy.

• Anti-TNF agents are blologic DMARDs that target cytokines to reduce joint inflammation and prevent further deformities.
• Combination therapy of methotrexate with anti-TNF is most effective in patients with established deformities or Inadequate response to methotrexate alone.
• Sterolds provide rapid symptom relief but are only adjuncts and not used alone due to long-term adverse effects like osteoporosis.
• NSAIDs are supportive for pain and inflammation but do not modify disease progression.

Other options:
Option A & D: Steroids alone or after NSAID failure do not prevent joint deformity progression.
Option B: Methotrexate with steroids is less effective than methotrexate with anti-TNF for limiting deformity.

stem: A 40-year-old patient presents to the emergency department following a road traffic accident. The radiograph of his pelvis is shown below. What is the most likely diagnosis?
subject: Orthopaedics, Surgery
explanation:
Solution
• Posterior hip dislocation is most commonly caused by dashboard Injuries in road traffic accidents.
• The patient typically presents with flexion, adduction, and internal rotation of the affected lower limb.
• Radiological findings: 1. Loss of continuity of acetabulum with the femoral head (break in Shenton's line) 2. Upward and lateral displacement of the femoral head. 3. The femoral head appears smaller than normal 4. The lesser trochanter is not visualized as the thigh is internally rotated 5. Femoral head and posterior acetabular wall fractures may also be seen Treatment: 1. Allis' maneuver. 2. Stimson's maneuver, East Baltimore lift maneuver, and Bigelow's maneuver.





Other options:
Option A: Central dislocation is typically caused by a lateral blow forcing the femoral head into the pelvis.
Option B: Fracture shaft of femur does not commonly present with these classical signs of hip dislocation.
Option D: Anterior dislocation usually presents with the leg externally rotated, abducted and slightly flexed.

stem: Cardiac edema and neuropathy are seen in which of the following vitamin deficiencies?
subject: Medicine, Biochemistry
explanation:
Solution
• Thiamine (Vitamin B1) deficiency causes both cardiac and neurological manifestations.


• Wet beriberi presents with tachycardia, cardiomegaly, cardiac fallure, and edema.
• Dry beribert is predominantly neurological, causing peripheral neuropathy, ataxia, and loss of deep tendon reflexes.
• Early signs include irritability, paresthesia, and proprioceptive loss.
• Chronic deficiency may lead to Wernicke encephalopathy and lactic acidosis.
• Diagnosis can be confirmed by measuring erythrocyte transketolase activity.
• Blochemical Assay for Vitamin Deficiency State:
VitaminAssay and test
Vitamin B1 (Thiamine)RBC Trans-ketolase
Vitamin B2 (Riboflavin)RBC glutathione reductase
Vitamin B6 (Pyridoxine)RBC transaminase
Vitamin B9 (Folic acid)FIGLU excretion/Histidine load test
Vitamin B12 (Cobatamine)

• Vitamin Deficiency States (Water-soluble Vitamins):
VitaminDeficiency State
Thiamine (B1)Beriberi Wernicke-Korsakoff's syndrome
Riboflavin (B2)Chellosis, Magenta-coloured tongue, Glossitis, Seborrheic dermatitis
Niacin (B3)Pellagra Dermatitis, Diarrhea, Dementia
Pantothenic acid (B5)Burning foot syndrome (historically called as "Nutritional melalgia") Dermatitis, enteritis, alopecia, ataxia
Pyridoxine (B6)Microcytic anemia, convulsions, depression
Blotin (B7)Relatively unknown Dermatitis, atrophic glossitis, alopecia, paraesthesia, muscle pain, anorexia, hallucinations
Folic acid (B9)Megaloblastic anernia, neuropathy, neural tube defects, atrophic glossitis
Cobalamin (B12)Pernicious anemia, dementia, spinal cord degeneration, homocysteinemia
Ascorbic acid (C)Scurvy Ecchymosisbleeding , gums, poor wound healing, fatigue, joint effusion, Scorbutic rosary

Other options:
Option A: Biotin deficiency primarily causes dermatitis, alopecia, and neurological symptoms, not cardiac edema.
Option C: Pyridoxine deficiency mainly leads to peripheral neuropathy and seizures.
Option D: Riboflavin deficiency presents with cheilitis, glossitis, and eye changes, not edema or neuropathy.

stem: While you are evaluating a baby, you show him a bright pink teddy bear that he reaches out to with both hands. What is the earliest age by which this milestone is typically achieved?
subject: Pediatrics
explanation:
Solution
• Bidextrous reach (reaching out with both hands) typically appears by 4 months of age.


• Fine motor reflexes develop after the palmar grasp reflex disappears around 3-4 months.
• Unidextrous reach (using one hand) develops by approximately 6 months.
• This milestone is an Important Indicator of neuromuscular and cortical development
• Fine motor milestones:
AgeMilestone
4 monthsReaches out for objects with both hands (Bidextrous reach)
6 monthsReaches out for objects with one hand (Unidextrous reach) and transfers objects
9 monthsImmature pincer grasp
12 monthsMature pincer grasp
15 monthsImitates scribbling and builds a tower of 2 blocks
18 monthsScribbles and builds a tower of 3 blocks
2 yearsBuilds a tower of 6 blocks
3 yearsBuilds a tower of 9 blocks and copies a circle
4 yearsBuilds a bridge with blocks and coples a cross
5 yearsCoples a triangle and builds a gate with blocks

• Gross motor milestones:
AgeMilestone
3 monthsNeck holding
5 monthsRolls over
6 monthsSits with support
8 monthsSits without support
9 monthsStands with support
12 monthsStands without support Creeps well Walks but falis
15 monthsWalks without support Creeps upstairs
18 monthsRuns
2 yearsWalks upstairs and downstairs (2 feet/step)
3 yearsWalks upstairs with alternate foot Rides tricycle
4 yearsWalks downstairs with alternate foot Hopping on 1 foot
5 yearsCan skip without falling to either side

Other options:
Option B: 5 months milestone usually already achieved by this age.
Option C: 6 months-unidextrous reach develops around this age, not bidextrous.
Option D: 7 months considered delayed for bidetrous reach.

stem: The commonest Indication for liver transplantation in children is
subject: Pediatrics, Surgery
explanation:
Solution
• Billary atresla is the most common indication for pediatric liver transplantation.


• Defined as partial or total absence of a permeable bile duct between the porta hepatis and duodenum due to Inflammatory fibrosis.
• Leads to obstruction of bile flow, causing conjugated hyperbilirubinemia.
• Clinical features include Jaundice, pale stools, dark urine, hepatomegaly, and eventually cirrhosis with ascites.
• HIDA scan is the investigation of choice; Intraoperative cholanglogram is the gold standard.
• Children with biliary atresia, who do not achieve successful drainage after hepatoportoenterostomy (Kasal's procedure), require liver transplantation within the first year of life.
• Management of extrahepatic billary atresla:
TypeDefinitionTreatment
Type 1Atresia restricted to the common bile ductRoux-en-Y hepaticojejunostomy
Type IIAtresia of the common hepatic ductKasai procedure (Roux-en-Y hepatoportoenterostomy)
Type IIIAtresia of the right and left hepatic ductKasai procedure (Roux-en-Y hepatoportoenterostomy)

• Indications of liver transplantation:
Adult (Indications)Children (Indications)
Non-cholestatic (65%) Viral hepatitis B and C Alcoholic liver cirrhosis CryptogenicBiliary atresia (58%) Inborn errors of metabolism (11%)
Cholestatic (14%) Primary biliary cirrhosis Primary sclerosing cholangitisCholestatic (9%) Primary sclerosing cholangitis Alagille syndrome
Autoimmune (5%)Autoimmune (4%)
Malignant neoplasm (2%) Miscellaneous (14%)Viral hepatitis (2%) Miscellaneous (16%)

Other options:
Option A: It is a rare congenital liver disease, less common cause for transplant.
Option C: It is an uncommon congenital bile duct malformation.
Option D: It is rare in children and not a common indication.

stem: The most common cause of HIV infection in a newborn is
subject: Pediatrics, Microbiology
explanation:
Solution
• Perinatal transmission is responsible for 70-80% of HIV infections in newborns.

• Occurs primarily via mucosal exposure to infected maternal blood and cervicovaginal secretions during delivery.
• Intrauterine contractions during labor may increase risk of microtransfusions, facilitating virus transfer.
• Vertical transmission can occur in utero, during birth, or postnatally via breastfeeding (least common).
• Risk factors: high maternal viral load, preterm delivery (<34 weeks), prolonged rupture of membranes (>4 hours), low birth weight, low maternal CD4 count, vitamin A deficiency.

Other options:
Option B: Breast milk transmission is less common compared to perinatal transmission.
Option C: Transplacental infection is uncommon.
Option D: Transmission via exchange transfusion is extremely rare.

stem: A child in your unit is diagnosed with severe acute malnutrition (SAM). According to the WHO criteria, SAM is defined best by:
subject: Pediatrics, PSM
explanation:
Solution
• Severe acute malnutrition (SAM) is defined as weight-for-height below -3 SD of the WHO child growth standards.

• Other diagnostic criteria include presence of bipedal edema and mid-upper arm circumference (MUAC) <11.5 cm.
• Children with complicated SAM (severe edema, failed appetite test, medical complications, or danger signs) require inpatient management.
• Children with uncomplicated SAM can be managed under supervised home-based care with therapeutic feeding and monitoring.
• SAM most commonly affects children 6-59 months of age.
• World Health Organization (WHO) classification of nutritional status of Infants and children:
Nutritional statusCriteria (from birth to 5 years)
ObeseWeight-for-height/length >3 SD, BMI-for-age >3 SD
OverweightWeight-for-height/length >2 SD and ≤3 SD, BMI-for-age >2 SD and ≤3 SD
Moderately underweightWeight-for-age <-2 SD and ≥-3 SD
Severely underweightWeight-for-age <-3 SD
Moderate acute malnutritionWeight-for-height/length ≥-2 SD and ≥-3 SD, BMI-for-age ≤-2 SD and ≥-3 SD, Mid-upper arm circumference ≥115 mm and <125 mm
Severe acute malnutritionWeight-for-height/length <-3 SD, BMI-for-age <-3 SD, Mid-upper arm circumference <115 mm, Bilateral pitting edema
Moderately stunted (or) moderate chronic malnutritionHeight/Length-for-age ≥-2 SD and ≥-3 SD
Severely stunted (or) severe chronic malnutritionHeight/Length-for-age <-3 SD
Moderately wastedWeight-for-height/length ≥-2 SD and ≥-3 SD
Severely wastedWeight-for-height/length <-3 SD

Other options:
Option A: Weight for age less than -2SD defines moderate underweight.
Option B: Weight for height less than +2SD is normal.
Option C: Weight for age less than +3SD is also normal.

stem: Molecular defect causing liver disease in Dubin-Johnson syndrome is ?
subject: Pathology, Pediatrics
explanation:
Solution
• ABCC2 gene defect leads to Dubin-Johnson syndrome.

• ABCC2 codes for canalicular ATP-binding cassette protein (MRP2). Mutation causes Impaired excretion of conjugated bilirubin into bile.
• Clinically presents with mild conjugated hyperbilirubinemia, often asymptomatic. Liver biopsy shows darkly pigmented liver due to accumulation of epinephrine metabolites.
• Conditions causing conjugated hyperbilirubinemia are summarised in the table below:
Dubin-Johnson syndromeRotor syndrome
Autosomal recessiveAutosomal recessive
The absent function of multiple drug-resistant proteins (MRP-2)Defects in anion transport
Black liver on biopsy (due to accumulation of epinephrine metabolite)No abnormal liver pigmentation
Normal levels of coproporphyrin in urineIncreased levels of coproporphyrin in urine
The gall bladder is not visualized on oral cholecystographyThe gall bladder is visualized on oral cholecystography

• Important diseases and their molecular defects:
GeneDisease
ATP7AMenke's disease
ATP7BWilson disease
CFTRCystic fibrosis
SERPINA 1alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency
ABCC2 (MRP2 protein defect)Dubin Johnson syndrome

• Hereditary Hyperbilirubinemias:
DisorderInheritanceDefects in bilirubin metabolismClinical course
Crigler-Najjar syndrome type IARAbsent UGT1A1 activityFatal in the neonatal period
Crigler-Najjar syndrome type IIARDecreased UGT1A1 activityGenerally mild, occasional kernicterus
Gilbert syndromeAR (common) AD (occasional)Decreased UGT1A1 activityInnocuous
Dubin-Johnson syndromeARImpaired biliary excretion of bilirubin glucuronides due to a mutation in canalicular multidrug resistance protein 2 (MRP2)Innocuous with black pigmentation of liver
Rotor syndromeARDecreased hepatic uptake and storage. Decreased billary excretionInnocuous

Other options:
Option A: It is defective in Menkes disease.
Option B: It is defective in Wilson disease.
Option D: It is defective in alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency causing liver disease.

• ABCC2 gene defect leads to Dubin-Johnson syndrome.


• ABCC2 codes for canalicular ATP-binding cassette protein (MRP2).
• Mutation causes Impaired excretion of conjugated bilirubin into bile.
