stem: Most common heart defect in congenital rubella syndrome is:
subject: Pediatrics, Obstetrics and Gynaecology
explanation:
Solution
• Patent ductus arteriosus (PDA) is the most common cardiac defect in congenital rubella syndrome.

• Pulmonary stenosis (PS) is the second most common defect. Cardiac anomalies occur in -50% of children infected during the first 8 weeks of gestation.
• Incidence of heart defects: PDA > PS > VSD > ASD.
• Congenital rubella syndrome: The triad of Gregg in congenital rubella syndrome includes: 1. Sensorineural hearing loss - Nerve deafness is the single most common finding among infants with congenital rubella syndrome. 2. Congenital cataract 3. Congenital heart disease - The most common cardiac defect is patent ductus arteriosus.
• Maximum damage to the fetus occurs in the first trimester Infection in the second trimester causes only deafness If infection occurs after 16 weeks there are no major abnormalities Diagnosis - Presence of IgM rubella antibodles in the infant shortly after birth or persistence of IgG antibodies for >6 months.
The given image shows bilateral caterect in an infant with congenital rubella syndrome

The given image shows Blueberry Muffin Rash in an infant with congenital rubella syndrome

The given image shows the salt and paper fundus



Other options:
Option A: ASD is less common.
Option B: VSD occurs less frequently.
Option D: PS is second most common after PDA.

stem: Which one of the following is not a feature in cerebral palsy:
subject: Pediatrics, Neurology
explanation:
Solution
• Cerebral palsy (CP) is primarily an upper motor neuron disorder.

• Features of CP include hypotonia, microcephaly, ataxia, spasticity, and abnormal reflexes. Flaccid paralysis is a lower motor neuron feature and not seen in CP.
• The subtypes of cerebral palsy are given in the table below:
TypesEtiologyPresentationMRI findings
Spastic (m/c type) Diplegia (second m/c in India)Prematurity, Ischemia, InfectionBilateral spasticity (LL>UL), Scissoring gait, Commando crawl, Learning disabilities, Seizures (33%)Periventricular leukomalacia
HemiplegiaVascular insult, Infection, GeneticEarly hand preference (UL>LL), Walking delayed up to 18-24m, Equinovarus deformity of footPorencephalic cyst, Focal infarct changes
Quadriplegia (most severe, m/c type in India)Ischemia, Infection, AsphyxiaMarked motor impairment, Flexion contractures, Strong association with seizures, Severe intellectual impairment and GDD, Difficulty swallowing (supranuclear palsy)Multi-cystic encephalomalacia, Severe periventricular leukomalacia
Dyskinetic (15-20%) Extrapyramidal, Athetoid, ChoreoathetosisKernicterus, Mitochondrial, GeneticRigidity/hypotonia, dystonia, athetosis, chorea, Cognitive disability, Hip displacement, SeizuresBasal ganglia scarring, Thalamic scarring

Other options:
Option A: Hypotonia is often present in infants with CP.
Option B: Microcephaly may be seen in some forms of CP.
Option C: Ataxia is characteristic of cerebellar involvement in CP.

stem: Where would you place the pulse oximeter to measure pre-ductal oxygen saturation in an infant who was born 3 minutes ago:
subject: Pediatrics
explanation:
Solution
• Pre-ductal oxygen saturation reflects oxygenation before the blood mixes with the ductal flow from the pulmonary artery.

• The right upper limb receives blood from the brachlocephalic artery, which branches off the aorta before the ductus arteriosus.
• This allows an accurate assessment of systemic oxygenation immediately after birth.
• Lower limbs and left upper limb are considered post-ductal

Other options:
Option A: Left upper limb-post-ductal, may show lower saturation.
Option B: Left lower limb-post-ductal.
Option D: Right lower limb-post-ductal.

stem: Which of the following is the main factor for the ductal closure postnatally:
options: Increase in partial pressure of oxygen (pa02)
subject: Pediatrics, Physiology
explanation:
Solution
• Increase in paO2 after birth is the primary stimulus for functional closure of the ductus arteriosus.

• Oxygen causes constriction of the smooth muscle in the ductal wall.
• Decrease in prostaglandins after birth also supports closure, but oxygen is the main trigger.
• Other factors like systemic vascular resistance and pulmonary resistance changes play a secondary role.
• The fetal circulatory adjustments after birth are given below:
StructureFunctional closureAnatomical closure
Ductus venosusAfter removal of the placenta7 days
Foramen ovaleAt birth after breathing is initiatedMonths to years
Ductus arteriosus10-15 hours10-21 days

Other options:
Option B: Increase in systemic vascular resistance contributes but not the main factor.
Option C: Increase in prostaglandin levels actually keeps ductus open.
Option D: Decrease in pulmonary venous resistance supportive, not primary.

stem: The most common manifestation of congenital toxoplasmosis is
subject: Pediatrics, Microbiology
explanation:
Solution
• Chorioretinitis is the most frequent clinical manifestation in congenital toxoplasmosis.

• Classic triad includes chorioretinitis, hydrocephalus, and Intracranial calcifications. • Other features: hepatosplenomegaly, jaundice, intrauterine growth retardation, prematurity, peripheral retinal scars, mild thrombocytopenia.
• Infection occurs transplacentally if the mother acquires Toxoplasma gondii during pregnarnicy.
• Severity is higher if maternal infection occurs in the first trimester.
• Spiramycin can be administered for cases of acute infection in early pregnancy to reduce vertical transmission. It does not cross the placenta and therefore cannot be used to treat an infected fetus.

Other options:
Option A: Hydrocephalus - part of triad but less common than chorioretinitis.
Option C: Hepatosplenomegaly seen occasionally.
Option D: Thrombocytopenia mild and less frequent.

stem: All among the following are clinical features of necrotizing enterocolitis except
subject: Pediatrics, Surgery
explanation:
Solution
• NEC typically causes metabolic or respiratory acidosis, not alkalosis.
• Clinical features include abdominal distension, tenderness, feed Intolerance, occult/gross blood in stool.
• Systemic signs include hypotension, bradycardia, lethargy, temperature instability, shock, and DIC.
• Risk factors include prematurity (most important), formula feeding, and bacterial colonization. Radiological findings include pneumatosis intestinalis; portal vein gas suggests severe disease, and pneumoperitoneum Indicates perforation.
• Staging is done using the modified Bell's staging criteria:
StageFeaturesRadiological signsManagementTreatment
I Suspected NECMild abdominal distention, vomiting, Temperature instability. apnea, bradycardia, A: Occult blood in stools, B: Gross blood in stoolsNormal or intestinal dilatation, mild lleusMedical: Nil per oral, total parenteral nutrition-IV fluids containing dextrose, IV antibiotics ampicillin. gentamycin, metronidazoleAntibiotics for 3 days
II A Mildly IIIAbsent bowel sounds, Abdominal wall edemaPneumatosis intestinalisMedicalAntibiotics for 14 days
II B Moderately IIIThrombocytopenia, Hyponatremia, Metabolic acidosisII A+ portal venous gas, with or without ascitesMedicalAntibiotics for 14 days
III A Severely III, bowel IntactPeritonitis, Definite ascitesII B + definite ascitesSurgical (due to bowel perforation)Stable-Laparotomy (resection of necrosed part, followed by anastomoses), Unstable Primary peritoneal drainage. and once stabilized definitive surgery
III B Severely Ill, bowel perforatedIntestinal perforationPneumoperitoneumSurgicalSame as above


Other options:
Option A: It is common in NEC
Option B: It can occur due to localized Inflammation or bowel wall edema.
Option C: It may be seen when inflammation extends to the abdominal wall.

stem: A 9-year-old boy has swelling of knee joints and non-blanching rashes as seen in the picture. Urine exam reveals haematuria +++ and proteinuria +. Platelet levels are normal. What is the probable diagnosis?
subject: Pediatrics, Dermatology
explanation:
Solution
• The child has palpable purpura with normal platelets, which is characteristic of HSP.

• Additional features such as arthritis of knees and renal Involvement (hematuria + proteinurla) strongly support HSP.
• HSP is an IgA-mediated small-vessel vasculitis, commonly affecting children 3-8 years old.
• Typical triggers include a recent upper respiratory tract infection.
• Other manifestations may include abdominal pain, vomiting, or GI bleeding.

Other options:
Option A: It is associated with malar rash, cytopenias, and positive ANA, not normal platelets with palpable purpura.
Option C: It presents with thrombocytopenia, whereas this child has normal platelet counts.
Option D: It typically causes nodules, livedo reticularis, and systemic vasculitis features rather than IgA-mediated palpable purpura.

stem: A baby assessed 5 minutes after birth is found to be cyanosed with irregular gasping respiration. Heart rate is 60 beats/min, and shows minimal resporise to stimulation with some flexion of extremities. The Apgar score for this newborn is?
subject: Pediatrics
explanation:
Solution
• The Apgar score for this infant is calculated as follows:
• Cyanosed (Appearance): 0
• Irregular gasping respiration (Respiration): 1
• Heart rate is 60 beats/min (Pulse): 1
• Minimal response to stimulation (Grimace): 1
• Some flexion of extremities (Activity): 1
• Total Score = 0 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 = 4.



Other options:
Option A: Too low based on component scoring.
Option B: Apgar score is a whole number, not a decimal.
Option C: Lower than expected from individual components.

stem: Which of the following is not seen in a child with cystic fibrosis?
options: Sweat chloride test chloride conc of 70 mEq/L
subject: Pediatrics, Pathology
explanation:
Solution
• Children with cystic fibrosis typically develop hypokalemla, not hyperkalemia.

• Excessive loss of chloride in sweat activates the renin-aldosterone system, causing intracellular shift of potassium → hypokalemia.
• Loss of sodium chloride also causes Increased bicarbonate reabsorption, leading to contraction alkalosis. Sweat chloride >60 mEq/L Is diagnostic for CF.
• Immunoreactive trypsinogen levels are elevated and used for newborn screening.

Other options:
Option A: It is seen in CF and is used as a diagnostic criterion.
Option B: It is elevated in CF and used in newborn screening.
Option D: It is commonly seen due to salt loss and RAAS activation.

stem: Which of the following is an absolute contraindication to OCP use?
subject: Obstetrics and Gynaecology, Pharmacology
explanation:
Solution
• Thromboembolism is an absolute contraindication to the use of combined oral contraceptive pills.

• Estrogen-containing OCPs increase the risk of venous thromboembolism by enhancing hepatic synthesis of clotting factors.
• Women with a past history of DVT/PE have a high recurrence risk with estrogen use.
• Other absolute contraindications include severe hypertension, cardiovascular disease, stroke, migraine with aura, breast cancer, and active liver disease.
• OCPs work by suppressing FSH and LH, thereby inhibiting ovulation.

Other options:
Option A: It is not an absolute contraindication to OCP use.
Option C: It is not a contraindication to OCP use.
Option D: Diabetes without vascular disease is not an absolute contraindication.

stem: Gestational trophoblastic neoplasm does not include?
subject: Obstetrics and Gynaecology, Pathology
explanation:
Solution
• A partial mole is part of gestational trophoblastic disease (GTD) but not part of gestational trophoblastic neoplasia (GTN).

• GTN refers to the malignant spectrum of GTD, which includes invasive mole, choriocarcinoma, PSTT, and ETT.
• Partial moles typically contain fetal tissue and are less aggressive than complete moles.
• They rarely progress to malignant GTN compared to complete moles. GTN requires chemotherapy, while partial moles usually resolve after evacuation.
• Modifled WHO Classification of GTD:
Molar PregnanciesTrophoblastic tumors
Hydatidiform mole (benign) - Partial and CompleteChoriocarcinoma (malignant)
Invasive mole (malignant)Placental site trophoblastic tumor (malignant)
Epithelioid trophoblastic tumor (malignant)

Other options:
Option B: It is included in GTN.
Option C: It is included in GTN.
Option D: It is included in GTN.

stem: What is the chromosome number of a partial hydatidiform mole?
subject: Obstetrics and Gynaecology
explanation:
Solution
• A partial hydatidiform mole typically has a triploid karyotype, most commonly 69 XXY.

• It occurs due to fertilization of an ovum by two sperms (dispermy) or by a single diploid sperm.
• Partial moles contain both fetal tissue and abnormal villi, unlike complete moles.
• They have focal trophoblastic proliferation rather than diffuse proliferation. Partial moles carry a very low risk of progression to malignant GTN.
• Molar pregnancy is of 2 types complete mole and partial mole:
FeaturePartial moleComplete mole
Karyotype69 XXY or 69 XYY46 XX or 46 XY
DiagnosisMissed abortionMolar gestation
Uterus sizeSmall for dateLarge for date
Theca-lutein cystsRare25-30% of cases
Initial hCG levels<1 lakh mlU/ml>1 lakh mlU/ml
Medical complicationsRareUncommon
Rate of subsequent GTN<1%2.5%
Embryo-fetusOften presentAbsent
Amnion, fetal erythrocytesOften presentAbsent
Villous edemaFocalWidespread
Trophoblastic proliferationFocal, slight to moderateSlight to severe
Trophoblast atyplaMildMarked
p57KIP2 immunostainingPositiveNegative

Other options:
Option A: This represents a normal female karyotype, not a partial mole.
Option B: This is not a recognized karyotype for a partial mole.
Option C: Triploidy with XY is possible but less common than 69 XXY.

stem: A pregnant lady with a history of recurrent abortions is now diagnosed to have antiphospholipid syndrome. What would be the best mode of treatment for her?
subject: Obstetrics and Gynaecology, Pharmacology
explanation:
Solution
• The best treatment for antiphospholipid syndrome (APS) during pregnancy is low-dose aspirin combined with heparin.

• APS causes thrombosis of uteroplacental vessels, leading to recurrent pregnancy loss.
• Aspirin inhibits platelet aggregation, while heparin prevents thrombosis-together Improving placental blood flow.
• Combined therapy significantly increases live-birth rates in APS patients. • Steroids are not recommended because they increase maternal complications without improving fetal outcomes.

Other options:
Option A: It is insufficient alone and does not prevent pregnancy loss effectively.
Option C: Steroids are not indicated for APS treatment in pregnancy.
Option D: This combination is not effective and is not recommended in guidelines.

stem: A 22-year-old pregnant lady presented with a fever and was started on antibiotics. The fever did not subside. On further Investigation, she was diagnosed to have septic pelvic thrombophlebitis. Which among the following is the next course of treatment for her?
subject: Obstetrics and Gynaecology, Pharmacology
explanation:
Solution
• Persistent fever despite adequate antibiotics suggests septic pelvic thrombophlebitis (SPT).

• The standard management is broad-spectrum antibiotics combined with anticoagulation (heparin). • Heparin prevents further clot propagation and septic emboli formation.
• SPT Involves thrombosis of pelvic veins, commonly the ovarlan veln, due to infection plus the hypercoagulable state of pregnancy.

Other options:
Option A: Stopping antibiotics is incorrect, as infection is the underlying cause.
Option C: Surgical embolectomy is reserved for massive, life-threatening pulmonary emboli.
Option D: Hysterectomy is a drastic measure and not first-line for SPT.

stem: Magnesium sulphate has no role in:
options: Prevention of seizures in severe pre-eclampsia
Prevention of recurrent seizures in eclampsia
Prevention of RDS In premature baby
subject: Obstetrics and Gynaecology, Pharmacology
explanation:
Solution
• Magnesium sulphate has no role in the prevention of respiratory distress syndrome (RDS) in premature bables.


• RDS occurs due to surfactant deficiency, and magnesium sulphate does not influence surfactant production.
• Magnesium sulphate is primarily used as an antiepileptic drug in obstetrics.
• It prevents and treats seizures in pre-eclampsia and eclampsia.
• It also acts as a tocolytic, reducing uterine contractility in preterm labor.
• Magnesium in the MgSO4 competes with calcium on voltage-gated calcium channels, thereby altering its uptake and release.
• Functions of MgSO4 include:
• 1. Membrane protector and neuro stabilizer. It is used in the prevention of cerebral palsy in very low birth weight neonates.
• 2. Reduction of motor endplate sensitivity to acetylcholine, and thus is used as an adjunct in the management of acute severe asthma in children and adults.
• 3. Blocking of neuronal calcium Influx, and is thus used in the conservative management of pre-term labor.
• 4. Inducing cerebral vasodilatation, dilating the uterine arteries, Increasing the production of endothellal prostacyclin, and Inhibiting platelet activation.

Other options:
Option A: It is routinely used to prevent seizures in severe pre-eclampsia.
Option B: It is the drug of choice to prevent recurrent seizures in eclampsia.
Option D: It decreases uterine tone and is used as a tocolytic.

stem: Which of the following is seen in the vertex presentation with complete flexion attitude of head?
subject: Obstetrics and Gynaecology
explanation:
Solution
• In vertex presentation with complete flexion of the fetal head, the presenting diameter is the suboccipito-bregmatic diameter.

• This diameter measures 9.5 cm, making it the smallest anteroposterior diameter that enters the pelvis.
• Complete flexion allows the fetal head to present optimally, facilitating normal labor progression.
• Flexion reduces the presenting diameter compared to partial flexion or deflexion.
• It is the most favorable relationship of the fetal head for vaginal delivery.
• Diameters of the fetal skull:
DiametersAttitude of the head
SuboccipitobregmaticComplete flexion
Suboccipito-frontalIncomplete flexion
OccipitofrontalMarked deflexion
Mento-verticalPartial extension
SubmentoverticalIncomplete extension
SubmentobregmaticComplete extension

Other options:
Option B: It is seen in incomplete flexion, not complete flexion.
Option C: It is seen in deflexion of the head.
Option D: It refers to a fetal position, not a diameter.

stem: What is the dose of Ulipristal used in emergency contraception?
subject: Obstetrics and Gynaecology, Pharmacology
explanation:
Solution
• Ulipristal acetate is used for emergency contraception as a single dose of 30 mg.

• It is effective when taken within 72 hours, and can be used up to 120 hours (5 days) after unprotected intercourse.
• It acts as a selective progesterone receptor modulator (SPRM). Its primary mechanism is delaying or Inhibiting ovulation.
• It is more effective than levonorgestrel, especially closer to ovulation.
• Methods of emergency contraception:
DrugDose
Levonorgestrel0.75 mg stat and after 12 hours
Ethinyl estradiol 50 µg + Norgestrel 0.25 mg2 tab stat and 2 tab after 12 hours
Conjugated estrogen15 mg BD x 5 days
Ethinyl Estradiol2.5 mg BD x 5 days
Copper IUDsInsertion within 5-7 days after accidental unprotected exposure. Not suitable for rape victims
Centchroman2 tablets (60 mg) to be taken twice at an interval of 12 hours within 24 hours of intercourse
Ulipristal30 mg within 72 hours

Other options:
Option B: It is not a recommended dose.
Option C: It is not the approved emergency contraceptive dose.
Option D: It is never used for contraception.

stem: A pregnant woman with a fibroid uterus develops severe acute pain abdomen at 28 weeks of gestation. The most likely diagnosis is:
subject: Obstetrics and Gynaecology
explanation:
Solution
• Severe acute localized abdominal pain without fever or leucocytosis suggests torsion of a subserous pedunculated fibroid.

• Increased vascularity of the uterus during pregnancy softens the fibroid, predisposing it to torsion.
• Venous obstruction at the site of attachment leads to acute pain.
• Red degeneration usually presents with pain plus low-grade fever and mild leucocytosis (not seen here).
• Preterm labour is associated with uterine contractions and cervical changes, which are absent in this case.
• The complication is more local than systemic, so systemic signs like fever and elevated WBC are typically absent.

Other options:
Option A: Pain may occur, but fever or leucocytosis is usually present.
Option B: Not consistent with localized acute pain.
Option D: Incorrect: torsion is the most likely cause.

stem: What is the first line of treatment of mastitis in a lactating mother?
subject: Obstetrics and Gynaecology, Pharmacology
explanation:
Solution
• Dicloxacillin is the first-line antibiotic for mastitis in lactating mothers.

• It is usually given for 10-14 days to ensure complete eradication of infection.
• Mastitis commonly occurs due to Staphylococcus aureus infection. Risk factors include Inexperienced breastfeeding and nipple trauma, especially during weaning or Infant teething.
• Early treatment helps prevent breast abscess formation and continuation of breastfeeding.
• Clinical features of mastitis Include: 1. Fever and malaise 2. Localized pain and tenderness 3. Purulent nipple discharge 4. Redness and swelling of the affected breast.

Other options:
Option B: Cefazolin is an alternative but not considered the first-line oral agent.
Option C: Ceftriaxone is a broad spectrum IV antibiotic, not typically used for simple mastitis.
Option D: Ampicillin is ineffective as most staphylococci produce beta-lactamase.

stem: What is the treatment of choice for a woman with atrophic vaginitis with complaints of itching?
subject: Obstetrics and Gynaecology
explanation:
Solution
• Topical estrogen is the treatment of choice for atrophic vaginitis.

• Atrophic vaginitis occurs in postmenopausal women due to thinning of the vaginal mucosa from estrogen deficiency.
• Common symptoms include itching, dryness, dyspareunia, and occasionally yellowish or blood-stained vaginal discharge.
• Before initiating estrogen therapy, exclude coexisting carcinoma of the cervix or uterus.
• Systemic estrogen therapy may be considered based on patient profile and symptom severity.

Other options:
Option A: Steroids are not effective for atrophic vaginitis.
Option C: Antihistamines alone do not treat the underlying estrogen deficiency.
Option D: Not using any therapy will not relieve symptoms.

stem: In which trimester of pregnancy does acute fatty liver manifest most commonly during pregnancy?
subject: Obstetrics and Gynaecology, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
• Acute fatty liver of pregnancy (AFLP) most commonly occurs in the third trimester or early postpartum.

• It is a leading cause of acute liver failure in pregnancy.

The DisordersHELLP syndromeObstetric cholestasisAcute fatty liver of pregnancyViral hepatitis
Onset in pregnancyMid to lateLateLateVariable
Differentiating featuresEpigastric pain, hypertension, elevated Indirect bilirubinPruritus, elevated direct bilirubinSigns of liver failure like hypoglycemia.Fever
AST (U/L)<300<200200-8002000+
Bilirubin (mgdL/)1-41-54-105-20
Platelets↓↓Normal↓Normal
FibrinogenNormalNormal↓Normal
D-dimer↑Normal↑Normal
Prothrombin timeNormalNormal↑↑
Hemolysis↑↑NoNoNo

Other options:
Option A: Rarely occurs in the first trimester.
Option B: Less common than in the third trimester.
Option D: First and second trimester involvement is unusual.

stem: Which of the following is not a contraindication for the induction of labor?
options: History of lower transverse cesarean section in last three pregnancies
subject: Obstetrics and Gynaecology
explanation:
Solution
• Heart disease is not an absolute contraindication for induction of labor, vaginal delivery is often preferred.

• Induction may still be done with careful maternal and fetal monitoring.
• Contraindications for induction include conditions where vaginal delivery is unsafe, such as pelvic obstruction or active genital infections.
• Previous cesarean section (especially classical or multiple scars) is a relative or absolute contraindication depending on the type of incision and number of prior surgeries.
• Other maternal comorbidities may modify the induction plan but do not absolutely prohibit it.
• Indications for induction of labor:
1. Uncontrolled maternal diabetes mellitus
2. Gestational hypertension
3. Pre-eclampsia
4. Post-term pregnancy
5. Abruption placentae
6. Premature rupture of membranes
7. Rh-isoimmunization
8. Intra-uterine growth restriction (IUGR)
9. Intra-uterine death (IUD)
10. Oligohydramnios
• Contraindications for induction of labor:
1. Contracted/distorted pelvis and cephalopelvic disproportion
2. Placenta previa or vasa previa
3. Active genital herpes
4. Pelvic tumor & carcinoma cervix
5. Transverse lie or footling breech
6. Appreciable macrosomia
7. Severe hydrocephalus
8. Umbilical cord prolapse
9. High-risk pregnancy with fetal compromise
10. Previous classical cesarean or hysterotomy

Other options:
Option A: It is a contraindication because pelvic obstruction may prevent vaginal delivery.
Option B: Active genital herpes is a contraindication due to risk of neonatal infection.
Option D: Multiple prior cesarean sections increase the risk of uterine rupture, contraindicating induction.

stem: Major contributor for amniotic fluid post 20 weeks is?
subject: Obstetrics and Gynaecology, Anatomy
explanation:
Solution
• Fetal urine is the primary source of amniotic fluid after 20 weeks of gestation.

• Early pregnancy amniotic fluid originates mainly from maternal plasma ultrafiltrate.
• During the second trimester, fetal skin diffusion contributes to amniotic fluid.
• After 20 weeks, keratinization of fetal skin prevents further diffusion, so urine predominates.
• Fetal kidneys start producing urine around 12 weeks, reaching 7-14 mL/day by 18 weeks.
• Normal urine production is essential for maintaining amniotic fluid volume and fetal well-being.
• Change in the amniotic fluid volume through gestation:
Period of gestationAmniotic fluid volume
10 weeks10-20 mL
16 weeks250 mL
33 weeks800 mL
38-39 weeksplateaus at 1000 mL
40 weeks800 mL

Other options:
Option B: Contributes only before 20 weeks via skin diffusion.
Option C: Fetal lung fluid contributes minimally to total amniotic fluid.
Option D: Maternal plasma is the primary contributor only in early pregnancy.

stem: What will be the level of the anti-Mullerian hormone in low ovarian reserve?
subject: Obstetrics and Gynaecology
explanation:
Solution
• Anti-Mullerian hormone (AMH) levels are low (<1ng/mL) in women with low ovarian reserve.

• AMH is secreted by granulosa cells of early (primordial and small antral) ovarian follicles. It plays a key role in oocyte maturation and mono-follicular development. AMH levels are stable throughout the menstrual cycle, making it a reliable marker.
• Low AMH indicates reduced quantity of remaining ovarian follicles and predicts poor ovarian response.
• It is used clinically for fertility assessment and planning assisted reproductive techniques.
AMH levels in ng/mlInterpretation
2-6.8Normal
<1Poor ovarian reserve
>10PCOD Hyperstimulation syndrome.

Other options:
Option B: Represents normal ovarian reserve.
Option C: Indicates high ovarian reserve.
Option D: Suggests very high ovarian reserve, often seen in PCOS.

stem: You discover that there is evidence of cardiac malformation in a fetus during a routine USG scan at 16 weeks. What is the minimum level of fasting blood sugar at which you can suspect overt diabetes?
subject: Obstetrics and Gynaecology, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
• Overt diabetes is diagnosed when fasting blood sugar is ≥126 mg/dL.

• These are women who are already diabetic before pregnancy or have hyperglycemia severe enough to meet diagnostic criteria.
• Early detection is important as hyperglycemia in early gestation is teratogenic, contributing to fetal malformations like cardiac defects.
• Fasting blood sugar is a simple and reliable screening tool for overt diabetes.
• Management includes tight glycemic control before and during pregnancy to reduce maternal and fetal complications.
• Women with fasting blood sugar below this threshold should be monitored for gestational diabetes later in pregnancy.
• A patient is said to have overt diabetes if:
1. Fasting blood sugar ≥126 mg/dL
2. A random blood sugar of ≥200 mg/dL
3. HbA1C ≥6.5%

Other options:
Option A: Below the threshold for overt diabetes.
Option C: Below the diagnostic cut-off.
Option D: Above threshold but not the minimum required level.

stem: Which of the following decreases in pregnancy?
subject: Obstetrics and Gynaecology, Physiology
explanation:
Solution
• Functional residual capacity (FRC) decreases in pregnancy due to the upward displacement of the diaphragm by the enlarging uterus.

• Tidal volume increases, while respiratory rate remains largely unchanged. Vital capacity is maintained because inspiratory and expiratory reserve volumes adjust.
• Inspiratory capacity may slightly increase to compensate for increased oxygen demand.
• The decrease in FRC contributes to reduced oxygen reserve, making pregnant women more prone to hypoxia during apnea or respiratory compromise.
• The changes in the respiratory system during pregnancy are:
• Increase:
1. Tidal volume
2. Inspiratory capacity
3. Minute O2 uptake
4. Minute ventilation
• Decrease:
1. Functional residual capacity
2. Expiratory reserve volume
3. Residual volume
4. Total lung capacity
• Unaffected:
1. Respiratory rate
2. Vital capacity
3. Inspiratory reserve volume
(Mnemonic: TIMM increases in pregnancy)

Other options:
Option A: Respiratory rate generally remains unchanged or slightly increases.
Option B: Vital capacity does not decrease significantly.
Option D: Inspiratory capacity remains the same or increases.

stem: The maximum dose of PGF2 alpha in postpartum hemorrhage is
subject: Obstetrics and Gynaecology, Pharmacology
explanation:
Solution
• Maximum dose of PGF2a in postpartum hemorrhage is 2 mg.

• PGF2a is a prostaglandin that promotes myometrial contractility, helping to control bleeding.
• It is primarily produced by the decidua and acts locally at the site of administration.
• Other prostaglandins have distinct roles: PGE2 from fetal membranes induces labor and cervical ripening; PGI2 from myometrium regulates uterine blood flow.
• Used carefully due to potential side effects such as hypertension, fever, or bronchospasm.

Other options:
Option A: Below therapeutic dose.
Option C: Exceeds maximum recommended dose.
Option D: Extremely high and unsafe dose.

stem: Which of the following is true about vaginal changes in a normal pregnancy?
options: Increased glycogen metabolism in the epithellum
subject: Obstetrics and Gynaecology
explanation:
Solution
• There is increased glycogen metabolism in the vaginal epithelium during pregnancy.

• Vaginal epithelium shows marked proliferation, not thinning, and the squamocolumnar junction extends downward.
• Vaginal pH decreases, becoming more acidic (3.5-6.0), Lactobacillus acidophilus numbers increase, metabolizing glycogen and producing lactic acid, maintaining acidic pH.
• Vascularity increases, causing a violet/purplish color (Chadwick's sign).
• Cervical secretions increase, producing thick white vaginal discharge.

Other options:
Option A: Incorrect - number of Lactobacilli increases.
Option C: Incorrect - pH decreases, not >6.8.
Option D: Incorrect - epithelium thickens, does not thin.

stem: The hormone acting on post ovulatory endometrium is:
subject: Obstetrics and Gynaecology, Physiology
explanation:
Solution
• Progesterone acts on the post-ovulatory endometrium (secretory phase).
• The functional zone of the endometrium responds to hormones, while the basal zone remains unaffected.
• During the secretory phase (15th day to menstruation), progesterone from the corpus luteum causes spiraling of arteries and saw-toothed/tortuous glands.
• Endometrial thickness reaches its maximum (~12 mm) under progesterone influence.
• Menstruation occurs if fertilization does not happen, due to withdrawal of progesterone and estrogen, leading to shedding of the functional zone.
• Estrogen (option D) mainly acts in the proliferative phase, prior to ovulation.





Other options:
Option A: LH triggers ovulation, not endometrial changes post-ovulation.
Option B: FSH stimulates follicular growth, not post-ovulatory endometrium.
Option D: Estrogen prepares the endometrium pre-ovulation, not post-ovulation.

stem: The given image shows what grade of placenta previa?
subject: Obstetrics and Gynaecology, Radiology
explanation:
Solution
• Grade III placenta previa is when the placental edge covers the internal os partially.
• The cervix may be partially covered, but some part of the os is still visible.
• It is associated with painless vaginal bleeding in the second or third trimester.
• Ultrasound (transvaginal preferred) is used for accurate diagnosis. Management depends on gestational age, severity of bleeding, and fetal/maternal status.
• Cesarean section is usually planned if placenta previa persists near term.


Other options:
Option A: Grade IV involves complete coverage of the internal os.
Option C: Grade II covers the internal os partially but not reaching the margin.
Option D: Grade I (low-lying placenta) approaches but does not reach the os.

stem: A baby was born at 34 weeks of pregnancy, the appearance of the baby is given below. What is the mother suffering from?
subject: Obstetrics and Gynaecology, Pediatrics
explanation:
Solution
• The baby shows macrosomia, which is most commonly associated with gestational diabetes mellitus (GDM).

• GDM is defined as carbohydrate intolerance with onset or first recognition during pregnancy.
• Uncontrolled maternal hyperglycemia leads to fetal hyperinsulinemia, stimulating excessive growth.
• Macrosomic babies may have increased risk of birth trauma and neonatal hypoglycemia.
• Early diagnosis and management of GDM reduce maternal and fetal complications.
• DIPSI criteria: Venous plasma glucose >140 mg/dL 2 hours after 75 g oral glucose load.

Other options:
Option B: Rubella causes congenital anomalies (e.g., cataract, deafness), not macrosomia.
Option C: Maternal hypothyroidism can lead to low birth weight or neurodevelopmental delay, not large babies.
Option D: Gestational hypertension may result in IUGR, not macrosomia.

stem: A 45-year-old woman presented with a lower abdominal mass and lower abdominal discomfort. On examination, the uterus appears uniformly enlarged and adnexa is free. MRI shows the presence of a myometrial cyst. What is the most probable diagnosis?
subject: Obstetrics and Gynaecology, Radiology
explanation:
Solution
• Adenomyosis is the growth of endometrial tissue (glands and stroma) within the myometrium.
• Commonly seen in multiparous women >40 years.
• Patients present with menorrhagia, dysmenorrhea, lower abdominal discomfort, and sometimes dyspareunia.
• On examination, the uterus is symmetrically enlarged, soft, and tender, with adnexa free.
• Imaging: TVS shows sub-endometrial striations ("Venetian blind" appearance) and salt-and-pepper myometrial texture; MRI shows myometrial cysts, which is highly suggestive.
• Definitive diagnosis is via histopathology. Treatment: Total hysterectomy is preferred in women past childbearing age; localized excision or Levonorgestrel IUCD can be used in younger women.



Other options:
Option A: Does not cause myometrial cysts and usually involves endometrial tissue outside the uterus.
Option C: Typically presents as a focal firm uterine mass rather than diffuse symmetrical enlargement.
Option D: Involves thickening of the endometrial lining, not the myometrium, and rarely causes a palpable mass.

stem: What is the ideal time for nuchal translucency evaluation?
subject: Obstetrics and Gynaecology, Radiology
explanation:
Solution
• Nuchal translucency evaluation is a component of first-trimester aneuploidy screening. It measures the maximum thickness of the subcutaneous translucent area between the skin and soft tissue over the fetal spine at the back of the neck.
• Ideal timing: 11-14 weeks gestation, when fetal crown-rump length is between 45-84 mm.
• Increased nuchal translucency is associated with a higher risk of fetal aneuploidy (e.g., Down syndrome) and structural anomalies, particularly congenital heart defects.


Other options:
Option A: Too early; fetal structures are not adequately developed for accurate measurement.
Option C: After 14 weeks, nuchal translucency measurement loses accuracy for aneuploidy screening.
Option D: Beyond the first trimester, evaluation is not reliable for first-trimester screening purposes.

stem: A 55-year-old lady presents with third degree uterine prolapse. Which of the following is the best management for her?
options: Vaginal hysterectomy with pelvic floor repair
subject: Obstetrics and Gynaecology, Surgery
explanation:
Solution
• Vaginal hysterectomy with pelvic floor repair is the treatment of choice for third-degree uterine prolapse in postmenopausal women.

• Third-degree prolapse involves the descent of the cervix and uterine body outside the vaginal introitus.
• It occurs due to ligamentous laxity from childbirth, aging, or trauma, leading to pelvic floor weakness.
• Patients typically present with pelvic heaviness, lower back discomfort, urinary symptoms (incontinence, urgency), bowel disturbances (constipation, tenesmus), and sexual discomfort. Surgical correction restores anatomical support and alleviates associated symptoms.
• Management of Uterine prolapse:
CategoryTreatment
Nulliparous or congenital prolapseAbdominal sling operations: Abdominal wall cervicopexy, Shirodkar's abdominal sling operation, Khanna's abdominal sling operation
Pregnancy and postnatal periodRing pessary (up to 16 weeks period of gestation)
<40 yearsPelvic floor exercises, Fothergill's repair (Manchester operation), Shirodkar's procedure
>40 years with completed familyVaginal hysterectomy and pelvic floor repair
>60 years unfit for surgeryLe Fort's repair, Colpocleisis. These are done if the patient doesn't want to retain sexual function

Other options:
Option A: Fothergill repair is mainly indicated for uterine prolapse with preserved uterus, typically in younger women; not ideal for postmenopausal third-degree prolapse.
Option B: Sling operations are primarily used for stress urinary incontinence, not for correcting advanced uterine prolapse.
Option D: Shirodkar procedure is a cervical cerclage used in preventing preterm birth, unrelated to prolapse management.

stem: All are normal physiological changes in pregnancy except:
options: Mild ST changes in inferior leads
subject: Obstetrics and Gynaecology, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
• A pansystolic murmur is not a normal physiological finding in pregnancy and usually indicates underlying structural heart disease such as mitral or tricuspid regurgitation.

• Normal physiological cardiac changes in pregnancy include left axis deviation due to cardiac rotation and diaphragm elevation.
• Mild ST-T changes in inferior leads are common and reflect normal hemodynamic adaptation.
• Atrial and ventricular premature contractions are often seen due to increased circulating blood volume and adrenergic tone.

Other options:
Option A: Left axis deviation is a normal adaptation caused by the enlarging uterus and diaphragm elevation.
Option B: Mild ST changes in inferior leads are benign and common in pregnancy.
Option C: Atrial and ventricular premature beats are physiological due to increased blood volume and cardiac output.
